findings.title: Combo open sound only fires when clicking the rightmost 20px arrow, not the whole collapsed face
findings.rust_location: src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/combos.rs:703-709 (handle_combo_mouse_down, no-dropdown-open branch via combo_arrow_at) and :683-695 (open branch)
findings.gamemd_behavior: In OwnerDraw_ComboBox_00617250 the WM_LBUTTONDOWN(0x201)/WM_LBUTTONDBLCLK(0x203) branch calls VocClass__PlayAtPos (GUIComboOpenSound) UNCONDITIONALLY as the first side effect, and ONLY THEN tests `client_width-0x14 < mouse_x` to decide whether to toggle the dropdown. So a mouse-down anywhere on the 24px-high collapsed combo face (text/swatch area, not just the arrow) plays the open sound; the dropdown only toggles when the click is in the rightmost 20px.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust gates BOTH the sound AND the open on combo_arrow_at, which uses arrow_hit_rect = the rightmost COMBO_ARROW_RESERVE_W(20)px only. A click on the left/text/swatch portion of a collapsed enabled combo produces no sound and no action. gamemd would have played GUIComboOpenSound on that same click.
findings.evidence: Ghidra decompile_function 0x00617250: label switchD_00617d4e_caseD_201 -> `VocClass__PlayAtPos(); if (client_width-0x14 < (mouse_x & 0xffff)) { SendMessage 0x157; PostMessage 0x14f }`. Doc SKIRMISH_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_SOUNDS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 3.1: 'The first side effect in that branch is a VocClass__PlayAtPos call ... Only after the sound call does it compare mouse X against client_width - 0x14'.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed: `combo_arrow_at` at combos.rs:514-526 uses `arrow_hit_rect` which restricts to the rightmost `COMBO_ARROW_RESERVE_W` (20px) pixels of the combo face. Both the `GuiComboOpenSound` push (line 707) and the dropdown-open action (line 704) are inside the `if let Some(id) = combo_arrow_at(...)` guard at line 703. A click on the text/swatch portion of a collapsed enabled combo produces no sound and no state change in Rust.

gamemd side confirmed via `SKIRMISH_OWNERDRAW_COMBOBOX_00617250_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` Section 3 "Dropdown Creation and Close" (verified from Ghidra decompile of `OwnerDraw_ComboBox_00617250 @ 0x00617250`): "on `WM_LBUTTONDOWN 0x201` or `WM_LBUTTONDBLCLK 0x203`, it plays a click sound; it toggles dropdown only when mouse X is greater than `client_width - 0x14`." The doc also confirms `client_width - 0x14` = 20px (0x14) arrow zone for toggle. The sound fires first, unconditionally, before the zone test.

No correction needed to the finding — both the Rust and gamemd sides are stated accurately. The drift is real: a click anywhere on the collapsed combo face in gamemd plays `GUIComboOpenSound`; in Rust the same click produces silence unless it lands in the rightmost 20px.

findings.title: Collapsed combo only toggles on arrow-zone click; gamemd toggles only on arrow zone too but Rust additionally swallows non-arrow face clicks differently
findings.gamemd_behavior: gamemd: a click on the non-arrow part of a collapsed combo face is consumed by the combo WndProc (returns 0, plays open sound, does not open). It does NOT fall through to any other control. The combo face is one Win32 control occupying the full combo rect.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust handle_combo_mouse_down returns false when the click is on the non-arrow portion of a closed combo (combo_arrow_at returns None), letting the click fall through to whatever the caller checks next (e.g. player-name edit, other hit-tests). gamemd's combo control would have consumed that mouse-down (and played the open sound). Whether this produces an observable difference depends on overlapping controls, but the click-consumption/sound contract differs.
findings.evidence: Ghidra 0x00617250 WM_LBUTTONDOWN branch always `return 0` after the sound+arrow-gate (consumes the message). Rust combos.rs:711 returns false on non-arrow face clicks.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Binary decompilation of `OwnerDraw_ComboBox_00617250` at `0x00617250` (verified via `decompile_function 0x00617250`) shows the `WM_LBUTTONDOWN 0x201` / `WM_LBUTTONDBLCLK 0x203` handler as:

```
switchD_00617d4e_caseD_201:
    VocClass__PlayAtPos();   // click sound — unconditional
    if (in_stack_000000d8 + -0x14 < (int)((uint)in_stack_00002b90 & 0xffff)) {
        // arrow zone only: post 0x14f to toggle dropdown
        LVar15 = SendMessageA(in_stack_00002b84, 0x157, 0, 0);
        PostMessageA(in_stack_00002b84, 0x14f, (uint)(LVar15 != 1), 0);
    }
    return 0;   // unconditional — both paths
```

Two verified facts:
1. `VocClass__PlayAtPos()` (click sound) fires **before** the arrow-zone gate — it is unconditional for every WM_LBUTTONDOWN on the combo control, including non-arrow face clicks.
2. `return 0` is unconditional — the message is consumed regardless of click position.

Rust side (`src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/combos.rs:703-711`): `handle_combo_mouse_down` calls `combo_arrow_at`; if `None` (non-arrow face click), it returns `false` with no sound pushed. Control returns to `handle_option_mouse_down` (`trackbars.rs:169`), which continues testing checkboxes and trackbars. In `app.rs:1358-1366`, the return value is discarded and the function ends — so the click is not additionally routed anywhere harmful. However, no click sound is emitted.

The finding's claim that the click "falls through to player-name edit" is mis-stated: `player_name_edit_rect_hit` is checked at `app.rs:1328`, *before* the combo path, not after. A non-arrow face click does not reach the player_name check on the way back. It does fall through to checkbox/trackbar hit-testing inside `handle_option_mouse_down`, but since combos and checkboxes/trackbars occupy geometrically separate screen regions, no spurious activation occurs in practice.

The real confirmed drift is: **missing click sound on non-arrow combo face clicks**. gamemd plays the sound on every face click; Rust plays it only on arrow-zone clicks. This is audible every time a player clicks the label/text portion of a combo control rather than the arrow — which is common since the non-arrow face occupies the majority of the combo width (`combo_face_width - COMBO_ARROW_RESERVE_W` px vs `COMBO_ARROW_RESERVE_W` px). The consumption difference is real but lower-impact because no observable control mis-activation results from the fall-through (the combo face area does not overlap checkboxes, trackbars, or the player name edit in the standard 800x600 layout).
verdict_obj.correction: The finding correctly identifies the Rust return-false path and the gamemd return-0 path. However, the characterisation of "falls through to player-name edit" is wrong: player_name_edit_rect_hit is checked before the combo handler (app.rs:1328), not after. The click falls through to checkbox/trackbar hit-testing inside handle_option_mouse_down, not to the player-name edit. The primary and most observable drift is the missing click sound (VocClass__PlayAtPos fires unconditionally in gamemd before the arrow-zone gate, not only on arrow clicks).

findings.title: Clicking a second combo's arrow while one dropdown is open plays Close+Open and immediately opens the second; gamemd's captured popup likely only closes
findings.gamemd_behavior: When a ComboDropWin popup is open it holds the mouse capture (SetCapture(pHVar8) at 0x00618424). A subsequent mouse-down is delivered to the captured ComboDropWin, whose WndProc plays GUIComboCloseSound and closes the popup; the click does not in the same event reach a different combo's arrow to open it. So clicking a different combo's arrow while one is open dismisses the first (one Close sound) without opening the second in the same click.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust, when open and the click hits a DIFFERENT combo's arrow, pushes GuiComboCloseSound AND GuiComboOpenSound and sets open_combo_dropdown to the new combo in a single mouse-down (combos.rs:687-690). This is two sounds plus an immediate second-combo open in one click, versus gamemd's single Close + closed state.
findings.evidence: Ghidra 0x00617250: open path ends with `SetCapture(pHVar8); ShowWindow(pHVar8,1)`. Doc SKIRMISH_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_SOUNDS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 3.2 describes ComboDropWin owning close on outside click (GUIComboCloseSound). The captured-window dismissal means the second combo is not opened by the same click.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side verified at combos.rs:683-695: when a dropdown is open and combo_arrow_at returns a different combo's id, Rust pushes GuiComboCloseSound then GuiComboOpenSound and sets open_combo_dropdown to the new combo in a single mouse-down event — two sounds plus an immediate second-combo open.

gamemd side verified via decompile_function at 0x00617250 and assembly context at 0x0060E4A0-0x0060E624. The OwnerDraw_ComboBox_00617250 decompile confirms SetCapture(pHVar8) at LAB_00618424, where pHVar8 is the ComboDropWin HWND. Win32 SetCapture semantics mean all subsequent mouse messages (including the next WM_LBUTTONDOWN) are routed to the ComboDropWin, not to any other window under the cursor. The ComboDropWin WndProc at 0x0060E4A0 performs a bounds check (0x0060e4af-0x0060e523); a click landing visually "on another combo's arrow" is outside the ComboDropWin content area, so it takes the out-of-bounds path: plays GUIComboCloseSound (CALL 0x00750920 at 0x0060e500 using Rules+0x1A8), then ReleaseCapture (0x0060e600), then CB_SHOWDROPDOWN 0x14F wParam=0 to close (0x0060e606-0x0060e616), then returns (RET at 0x0060e624). The click is consumed by ComboDropWin and never reaches OwnerDraw_ComboBox_00617250 for the other combo — so no second open sound and no second-combo open occurs in that click. The user must click the second combo's arrow a second time to open it.

The research doc SKIRMISH_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_SOUNDS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md correctly documents the open/close sound call sites but does not address the captured-window routing for the cross-combo click scenario. The finding's gamemd claim is accurate. The Rust behavior (Close+Open sounds, second combo immediately open) is confirmed drift from gamemd (one Close sound, all combos closed after the click).

findings.title: Clicking the open combo's own arrow plays only Close; verify gamemd toggle-close sound source
findings.gamemd_behavior: gamemd dismisses the open popup via the captured ComboDropWin which plays GUIComboCloseSound. The collapsed combo's own arrow-toggle path (which would play GUIComboOpenSound) is not reached because the popup has capture and consumes the click. Net: one Close sound when re-clicking to close.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust plays one GuiComboCloseSound and closes (combos.rs:684-686). This matches gamemd's single Close sound for the close-on-reclick case, so no extra Open sound here — appears correct. Flagged only as the counterpart to the switch-combo finding; no disparity proven for the same-combo case.
findings.evidence: Ghidra 0x00617250 capture model; doc sec 3.2. This row documents a verified-correct point, not a disparity.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust code at combos.rs:683-686 (the `id == open.id` branch): when the same combo's arrow is clicked while its dropdown is open, Rust plays exactly one `GuiComboCloseSound` and sets `open_combo_dropdown = None`. No `GuiComboOpenSound` is emitted on this path.

gamemd side (verified via `SKIRMISH_OWNERDRAW_COMBOBOX_00617250_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` line 182 and `SKIRMISH_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_SOUNDS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` section 3.1/3.2): when the popup opens, gamemd calls `SetCapture(dropdown_hwnd)` on the `ComboDropWin` popup. With Win32 capture active, the re-click on the combo arrow is delivered to `ComboDropWin` (not to `OwnerDraw_ComboBox_00617250`). `ComboDropWin`'s WndProc block at `0x0060D540` handles the outside-bounds click, plays `GUIComboCloseSound` at `0x0060E4E9..0x0060E500`, and closes the popup. The collapsed combo's `OwnerDraw_ComboBox_00617250` open-sound path (which fires unconditionally before the rightmost-20-px gate at `0x006184A2..0x006184BA`) is never reached.

Net gamemd output: one `GUIComboCloseSound`, popup closes. Net Rust output at combos.rs:684-686: one `GuiComboCloseSound`, `open_combo_dropdown = None`. Outputs are identical. The finding itself states "no disparity proven for the same-combo case" — it is a self-documented verified-correct point included only as a counterpart note, not an actual drift claim. No algebraic proof or exhaustive check is needed here because the finding explicitly concedes parity and flags no disparity.

findings.title: Arrow hit zone uses 20px reserve from face right edge; matches gamemd's client_width-0x14 boundary
findings.gamemd_behavior: gamemd toggle gate is `client_width - 0x14 (20) < mouse_x_within_client`, i.e. the rightmost 20px of the combo CLIENT rect toggles the dropdown.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust arrow_hit_rect = rightmost COMBO_ARROW_RESERVE_W(20)px of combo_face_rect (face = combo rect with COMBO_FACE_H height). This matches the 20px boundary. No disparity in the zone width; included to confirm the boundary constant is correct (the disparity is the sound/open gating in ss-combos-open-sound-only-on-arrow-zone, not the zone width).
findings.evidence: Ghidra 0x00617250 `in_stack_000000d8 + -0x14 < (mouse_x & 0xffff)`; layout.rs:23 COMBO_ARROW_RESERVE_W=20. Verified-correct point.

verdict_obj.reasoning: Verified at gamemd 0x00617250 (decompiled via mcp__ghidra-mcp__decompile_function): the WM_LBUTTONDOWN/WM_LBUTTONDBLCLK handler (messages 0x201/0x203) gates the dropdown toggle on `in_stack_000000d8 + -0x14 < (int)((uint)in_stack_00002b90 & 0xffff)`. `in_stack_000000d8` is the `right` field of the RECT filled by `GetClientRect`, which for a top-level control equals its pixel width. `(uint)in_stack_00002b90 & 0xffff` is the low word of lParam, i.e., the mouse X within the client. So the gamemd condition is: mouse_x > client_width - 20, meaning the rightmost 20 px of the combo client rect opens the dropdown. Rust side (src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/combos.rs:490-498): `arrow_hit_rect` computes `[face.x + face.w - COMBO_ARROW_RESERVE_W, face.y, COMBO_ARROW_RESERVE_W, face.h]` where `COMBO_ARROW_RESERVE_W = 20` (layout.rs:23) and `face.w = rect.w` (layout.rs:390). This is algebraically identical: the rightmost 20px of the combo rect. The finding itself declares no disparity and was included only to document that the 20px constant is correct. Outputs are bit-identical for all valid mouse positions.

findings.title: Rust adds mouse-wheel scrolling of open dropdown; no scoped gamemd callback handles WM_MOUSEWHEEL
findings.rust_location: src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/trackbars.rs (handle_option_mouse_wheel) / combos.rs scroll_open_combo_by_rows path referenced by SKIRMISH_0X102 doc sec 4 as state.rs:506
findings.gamemd_behavior: No WM_MOUSEWHEEL (0x20A) handler was found in OwnerDraw_ComboBox_00617250, ComboDropWin (0x0060D540), or OwnerDraw_ScrollBar_0061C690. Whether retail scrolls an open dropdown via OS/native wheel translation was NOT runtime-captured (doc marks DEFERRED OQ-CG-006 / OQ-SK-COMBO-SND-007).
findings.rust_behavior: Per SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 4, current Rust has direct dropdown wheel scrolling. If gamemd does not scroll the dropdown on wheel, this is added behavior the player would notice (wheel-over-open-side-combo scrolls country list). Cannot prove gamemd side either way from binary; marked unchecked.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 2 (mouse-wheel row: 'no direct callback path found') and sec 4 ('Treat Rust wheel scrolling as a parity risk unless runtime parent translation is verified'); Ghidra 0x00617250 has no 0x20A case.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Verified via binary scan and decompilation:

1. `OwnerDraw_ScrollBar_0061C690` (decompiled): message dispatch covers 0xf, 0x14, 0x84, 0x87, 0xe0, 0xe1, 0xe2, 0x113, 0x200, 0x201, 0x202, 0x203, 0x4d2, 0x4f1 — no 0x20A case.

2. `OwnerDraw_ComboBox_00617250` (decompiled): message dispatch covers 0xf, 0x2, 0x7–0xe, 0x14, 0x2d, 0x111, 0x141, 0x14e, 0x14f, 0x150–0x151, 0x199–0x19a, 0x201, 0x203, and 0x497–0x4f1 range — no 0x20A case.

3. ComboDropWin WndProc at LAB_0060d540 (confirmed via `FUN_0060d450` registration code: `local_28.lpfnWndProc = (WNDPROC)&LAB_0060d540`): raw memory scanned from 0x0060D540 through 0x0060F400 in 256-byte blocks. `search_byte_patterns` for `0a 02 00 00` (LE encoding of 0x20A) found zero matches in this region. No CMP against 0x20A exists anywhere in the ComboDropWin WndProc body.

4. Rust side (`src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/trackbars.rs` lines 248–263): `handle_option_mouse_wheel` explicitly intercepts winit `WindowEvent::MouseWheel` (routed through `app.rs:1870–1881` → `handle_skirmish_shell_mouse_wheel` → `handle_option_mouse_wheel`) and calls `scroll_open_combo_by_rows` on the open dropdown. This is a deliberate Rust addition.

5. Windows behavior: ComboDropWin calls `SetCapture` so all mouse messages go to it, but WM_MOUSEWHEEL without a handler falls through to `CallWindowProcA`/`DefWindowProc`, which does not scroll a custom owner-draw popup. No OS-level translation produces scrolling behavior that matches Rust's explicit scroll.

The finding's gamemd claim is accurately stated. The Rust behavior (wheel-over-open-side-combo scrolls country list) is a genuine added feature not present in gamemd.exe.

findings.title: Dropdown scrollbar track click jumps to absolute thumb-centered top index (matches corrected gamemd)
findings.gamemd_behavior: OwnerDraw_ScrollBar_0061C690 track clicks centre the thumb on the click and convert to an absolute current/top value (NOT page-by-visible-rows).
findings.rust_behavior: Rust top_index_from_scrollbar_track_click centres thumb on click (mouse_y - thumb.h/2) and maps to absolute top index — matches gamemd. The stale 'Current Rust Status' note in the geometry doc (sec 4: 'Rust track-click behavior scrolls by visible row count') no longer reflects the code. Verified-correct point, no disparity.
findings.evidence: Ghidra-backed doc SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 2 'Track clicks ... jump the scrollbar current value by centering the thumb on the click'; Rust combos.rs:251-256 matches.

verdict_obj.reasoning: Rust code at combos.rs:251-256 computes `thumb_top = (mouse_y - thumb.h / 2).clamp(...)` then maps proportionally to a top index — this is exactly the click-centered absolute-jump behavior. The gamemd behavior is correctly stated: `OwnerDraw_ScrollBar_0061C690` (doc SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 2, row 9) confirms track clicks center the thumb on the click and convert to a proportional top index. The geometry doc sec 4 stale note ("Rust track-click behavior scrolls by visible row count") was superseded — the recheck doc SKIRMISH_COMBODROPWIN_DROPDOWN_VISUAL_INPUT_RECHECK_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 12 explicitly states "Current Rust has closed ... proportional track-click." Both sides are confirmed equivalent; no drift exists.

findings.title: Dropdown scrollbar geometry constants (20px width, 22px arrow buttons, 14px min thumb, 23px row) match gamemd
findings.gamemd_behavior: Row height = font_height+6 = 23 with GAME.FNT; scrollbar width = DAT_00AC1DF0*2+0x12 = 20; arrow buttons 22px high; minimum thumb 14px.
findings.rust_behavior: All four constants match the verified values. Verified-correct point, no disparity.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 2 table; Rust layout.rs:19-22.

verdict_obj.reasoning: Rust constants at layout.rs:19-22 read: COMBO_DROPDOWN_ROW_H=23, COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_W=20, COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_BUTTON_H=22, COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_MIN_THUMB_H=14. The research doc SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 2 (table rows 6-8) confirms all four values against live Ghidra decompiles: scrollbar width from decompile 0x0060F9A0 and 0x00618D40 (DAT_00AC1DF0=1, so 1*2+0x12=20); arrow-button height 22px and min thumb 14px from decompile 0x0061C690; row height font_height+6=17+6=23 from decompile 0x00617250 init block 0x00618AEA..0x00618B46. The doc's own section 4 explicitly states "Rust uses COMBO_DROPDOWN_ROW_H=23, COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_W=20, arrow-button height 22, and min thumb 14, which matches the verified standard constants." There is no drift on these four constants; the finding is self-described as a "Verified-correct point, no disparity" and the adversarial check finds no evidence to the contrary.

findings.title: Color combo population (sentinel -2 + colors 0..7, row 8 omitted) matches gamemd
findings.gamemd_behavior: FUN_004E45A0 adds a sentinel row (string 0x20A) with item data -2, then inserts only color rows 0..7; the 9th initialized swatch (row 8, grey 0x606060) is NOT inserted into normal dropdowns.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust pushes ColorSentinel(-2) then iterates color 0..HOUSE_COLOR_COUNT (0..8 = indices 0-7), filtering taken colors. With HOUSE_COLOR_COUNT=8 it never exposes a row-8 grey swatch. Matches gamemd. Verified-correct point, no disparity.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_COLOR_COMBO_POPULATION_AND_SWATCH_ORDER_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 3-4 (rows 0..7 inserted, row 8 omitted); Rust combos.rs:284-291.

verdict_obj.reasoning: Rust code at combos.rs:282-293 pushes ColorSentinel(-2) then loops `for color in 0..HOUSE_COLOR_COUNT` where HOUSE_COLOR_COUNT=8 (skirmish_launch.rs:12), yielding indices 0..7 only. Row 8 is never inserted. This matches gamemd exactly: FUN_004E45A0's owner-pointer loop stops at 0x008B40A0 (before row 8), as verified in SKIRMISH_COLOR_COMBO_POPULATION_AND_SWATCH_ORDER_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 3 OQ2 (evidence: Ghidra disassembly at 0x004E46BD..0x004E472C). The research doc also confirms the sentinel item data is -2 and that nine swatches are initialized but only rows 0..7 are inserted into normal dropdowns. The finding itself is self-described as a "verified-correct point, no disparity" — it is not a drift finding but a confirmation of parity, making it a false positive as a drift report.

findings.title: AI-type combo order (None, Easy, Normal, Hard) with item data (-1,2,1,0) matches gamemd
findings.gamemd_behavior: FUN_006AE6E0 inserts four entries in order GUI:None(-1), GUI:AIEasy(2), GUI:AINormal(1), GUI:AIHard(0), then CB_SETCURSEL index 0 (None) before persisted state applies.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust combo_items lists [None, Easy, Normal, Hard]; item_data returns None=-1, Easy=2, Normal=1, Hard=0. Order and item data match exactly. Verified-correct point, no disparity.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_AI_ROW_STATE_LABELS_AND_ITEM_DATA_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 2; Rust combos.rs:265-273, state.rs:189-196.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Read both cited Rust locations and the research doc.

Rust side (state.rs:188-196, combos.rs:265-273): `SkirmishAiRowType::item_data` returns None=-1, Easy=2, Normal=1, Hard=0. `combo_items` for `AiType` lists [None, Easy, Normal, Hard] in that order. A dedicated test at `src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/tests.rs:1019-1023` asserts all four values bit-exactly.

gamemd side (SKIRMISH_AI_ROW_STATE_LABELS_AND_ITEM_DATA_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §2, verified from binary assembly `0x006AE7AA..0x006AE858`): `FUN_006AE6E0` inserts GUI:None(-1), GUI:AIEasy(2), GUI:AINormal(1), GUI:AIHard(0) in that order, then sends CB_SETCURSEL index 0. The doc is ghidra/verified status and the assembly range is cited inline for each insertion.

The values and insertion order are identical. The doc's §6 ("Rust has no item-data order") was written before the skirmish shell implementation landed (commit acc8cbf / 470fae5, 2026-05-27); it is a stale snapshot, not evidence of a current gap. No correction to the finding's factual claims is needed — the finding correctly identifies this as a matching, disparity-free point.

findings.title: Row-1 AI defaults to Easy in Rust, but gamemd resets every AI-type combo to None (index 0) before applying persisted slot state
findings.gamemd_behavior: After populating the four AI-type entries, FUN_006AE6E0 sends CB_SETCURSEL with index 0, selecting GUI:None (-1) for EVERY AI row; the actual selected difficulty comes from persisted [Skirmish] Slot%02d profile values, not a hardcoded Easy on row 1. The default visible state of a fresh row-1 combo depends on persisted profile state, which on a clean profile defaults to closed/None for AI rows except as the profile dictates.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust hardcodes opponents[0].row_type = Easy (one active opponent), so the first AI row shows 'Easy' by default regardless of persisted profile. gamemd derives the selection from saved Slot data after a reset-to-None. This is a default-selection disparity for a fresh skirmish session; the visible AI-row state on first open may differ (e.g. gamemd could show None or a profile-saved difficulty).
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_AI_ROW_STATE_LABELS_AND_ITEM_DATA_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 2 ('CB_SETCURSEL ... index 0 ... selects GUI:None ... before persisted/default slot state is applied') and sec 5 ([Skirmish] Slot%02d). Rust state.rs:263-267 hardcodes Easy on idx 0.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed at state.rs:263-264: `if idx == 0 { SkirmishAiRowType::Easy } else { SkirmishAiRowType::None }`, so opponent slot 0 is initialized to Easy with `enabled: true`.

gamemd side verified via live Ghidra decompilation:

1. `FUN_006AE6E0` (0x006AE6E0): the first loop resets all 7 AI row-state combos (`0x50B`, `0x50E`, `0x516`, `0x51A`, `0x51B`, `0x51C`, `0x51D`) by clearing them (`CB_RESETCONTENT 0x14B`), inserting 4 items in order (None/-1, Easy/2, Normal/1, Hard/0), then sending `CB_SETCURSEL 0x14E` with index 0 — selecting GUI:None for every row. Verified at assembly `0x006AE85A..0x006AE864` (doc sec 2) and confirmed in decompiled pseudocode: `SendMessageA(pHVar3, 0x14e, 0, 0)` inside the loop.

2. `SessionClass__ReadSkirmishSettings` (0x00697F10): for each slot 1..7, calls `FUN_00477440` with default slot-type value `0xfffffffe` (-2). `FUN_00477440` (0x00477440): calls `CCINIClass__ReadString`; if the INI key is absent (fresh profile, no `[Skirmish] Slot%02d` written), it returns without writing to the output pointers, leaving the slot-type at the caller-supplied default of -2.

3. Back in `FUN_006AE6E0`'s second loop (the readback/apply phase): converts stored slot-type code via: `1→-1`, `4→0`, `5→1`, `6→2`, else `(0xffffffff & 0xfffffffd) + 2 = -1`. For slot-type `-2` (0xfffffffe), the else branch fires: `local_14 = -1`. That sends `CB_FINDITEMDATA` for item data -1 (GUI:None), then `CB_SETCURSEL` to that index — the row shows GUI:None and all sibling controls (country/color/start/team) are disabled via `EnableWindow(..., 0)`.

Conclusion: on a fresh profile, gamemd displays all 7 AI rows as GUI:None (inactive, siblings disabled). Rust shows row 0 as Easy (active, enabled). This is a visible disparity on first launch or any clean-profile open of the skirmish shell — the player sees one active Easy opponent in Rust where gamemd would show an empty lobby with all slots closed.

findings.title: Side/country combo omits Observer (-3) row; correct for standard offline skirmish
findings.gamemd_behavior: Country/side combo item data is -3 (observer, conditional/not-normal-offline), -2 (Random), 0..9 (countries). Observer -3 is gated and not part of normal offline human/AI selection.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust inserts Random then the 10 countries (item data equivalent 0..9 by ordering), no Observer. Matches the standard offline set. Verified-correct point, no disparity.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_SIDE_COUNTRY_TEAM_FINAL_WRITES_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 2 (observer -3 'Conditional, not the normal standard offline'); Rust combos.rs:274-281 and main_menu.rs:54-65 country order.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Read combos.rs:274-281 — Rust builds the Side arm as `Random` then `SkirmishCountry::ALL` (10 entries). Read main_menu.rs:54-65 — `ALL` order is America, Korea, France, Germany, GreatBritain, Libya, Iraq, Cuba, Russia, Yuri, mapping to item data 0..9.

Read SKIRMISH_SIDE_COUNTRY_TEAM_FINAL_WRITES_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 2 (verified Ghidra report): gamemd `FUN_004E3A00` populates the standard offline combo as Random (-2) first, then countries 0..9 in INI order (Americans=0 … YuriCountry=9). Observer row (-3) is inserted only via a separate gated path (`FUN_004E3B00`/`FUN_004E3560`) conditioned on row/local observer state — "Conditional, not the normal standard offline human/AI selection."

Rust country order (America=0 … Yuri=9) matches the gamemd INI order exactly. No observer row in Rust matches gamemd standard offline behavior. The finding's gamemd claim is correctly stated and the Rust output is identical for every standard offline skirmish input. No disparity exists to confirm.

findings.title: Side combo dropdown cap is 7 visible rows, producing a scrollbar for the 11-row Random+10-country list
findings.gamemd_behavior: Side/country combos use 0x4DE max-visible cap of 7 rows; with Random + eligible houses the list overflows and a scrollbar appears.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust caps Side at 7 (11 items -> scrollbar). Matches gamemd cap. Verified-correct point, no disparity.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 3 (side cap 7); Rust combos.rs:55.

verdict_obj.reasoning: Rust code at combos.rs:55 returns 7 for `SkirmishComboId::Side(_)` in `combo_dropdown_max_visible_rows`. The research doc SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md section 3 (table row for Side/country) states cap `7`, verified via Ghidra calls against `0x006AE6E0`, `0x004E3A00`, `0x004E45A0`. The doc section 80 coverage ledger marks "Family row counts and caps" as verified with those same addresses. SkirmishCountry::ALL has 10 entries (main_menu.rs:54-65), plus 1 Random sentinel inserted at combos.rs:274, giving exactly 11 items — which overflow the cap of 7 and trigger a scrollbar on both gamemd and Rust. The behavior is identical: same cap value, same item count, same scrollbar appearance. No disparity exists.

findings.title: Team combo uses uncapped max-visible (0) in Rust; gamemd sets 0x4DE cap of 9
findings.gamemd_behavior: Team combos send 0x4DE max-visible-rows cap = 9 (same as color/start). Team list is at most 5 rows (None + A,B,C,D), so the 9-cap never bites; the popup height = min(items,9)*row_h.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust returns 0 (uncapped) for Team, so visible_rows = item_count directly. Because team item_count (<=5) is always < 9, the computed popup height and scrollbar presence are identical to gamemd's cap-9 result in every reachable case. No observable difference; logged as a constant mismatch only (cap 0 vs 9) for fidelity.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 3 (team cap 9); Rust combos.rs:57 maps Team to 0.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed at combos.rs:57: `SkirmishComboId::Team(_) => 0` (uncapped). The logic at lines 40-44 uses `visible_rows = item_count` when `max_rows == 0`.

Gamemd side confirmed via SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 3 (line 55): Team cap is 9 in gamemd (`0x4DE` field).

Algebraic proof of output identity: `combo_items` for Team (combos.rs lines 311-323) uses two hardcoded fixed-size slices — `&[-2, 0, 1, 2, 3]` (5 items, normal mode) or `&[0, 1, 2, 3]` (4 items, `must_ally` mode). These are static arrays; there is no dynamic growth path. Item count is bounded to {4, 5} for all reachable inputs.

For all reachable Team item counts: `min(x, 9) = x` when `x ∈ {4, 5}`, so gamemd's capped result equals Rust's uncapped result exactly. The outputs — `visible_rows`, popup height (`visible_rows * COMBO_DROPDOWN_ROW_H`), scrollbar presence (`combo_dropdown_needs_scrollbar`), and all downstream geometry — are bit-identical. No scenario exists where Team item_count reaches 9, since the items are a fixed enum (None/A/B/C/D), not one-per-player-slot.

findings.title: AI-type combo uncapped (0) matches gamemd (no 0x4DE cap set, 4 rows)
findings.gamemd_behavior: AI-type combo does not set a 0x4DE cap and has 4 rows; popup shows all 4.

findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 3 ('AI type ... 4 rows, no 0x4DE cap needed'); Rust combos.rs:57.

The research doc SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md section 3 table (line 51) and finding row (line 36) confirm via decompile of `0x004E3A00` and init addresses `0x00618A67..0x00618A6E` that gamemd never sets a 0x4DE cap for the AI type combo and always populates exactly 4 rows (None/Easy/Normal/Hard). findings.title: Start combo population (Auto/Random + numbered, reservation eviction) matches gamemd ordering and uniqueness
findings.gamemd_behavior: FUN_004E50C0 adds Random(item data -2) first, then numbered starts 1..N (item data 0..N-1) that are owner -1 (available) or owned by this row; a numbered start chosen by another row is omitted from this row's list (reservation table). Map start-count limits N.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust adds StartPosition::Auto first, then positions 0..capacity (capacity = map waypoints clamped to slot count), inserting a position only if selected-by-this-row or not taken by another row. This reproduces the Random-first ordering, the map-count limit, and the cross-row uniqueness omission. Verified-correct point, no disparity in list composition.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_START_POSITION_COMBO_POPULATION_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 3 (Random first item data -2, numbered 0..7, owner-based omission); Rust combos.rs:294-308.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Read combos.rs:294-310 and 406-419 against the verified gamemd behavior in SKIRMISH_START_POSITION_COMBO_POPULATION_GHIDRA_REPORT.md (FUN_004E50C0).

Gamemd (doc Section 3, verified): Auto/Random (item data -2) is inserted first unconditionally, then numbered entries 0..7 are iterated; each is inserted only if owner is -1 (unclaimed) or equals this row's index; entries for another row's index are skipped. Map count is enforced by FUN_004E4FC0 marking slots beyond the map's start count as -2, which the loop skips.

Rust (combos.rs:301): `StartPosition::Auto` is pushed first unconditionally — matches gamemd. The loop `for position in 0..capacity` (capacity = `map.multiplayer_start_waypoints.len().min(8)`) replicates the map-count clamp. Each `Position(x)` is inserted if `selected == Some(start) || !start_position_taken_by_other_row(state, row, start)` — this is the exact gamemd condition (own reservation OR unclaimed). `start_position_taken_by_other_row` checks the player row (row 0) and all opponent rows except the current one; inactive opponents always hold `StartPosition::Auto` (enforced by `apply_inactive_combo_defaults` at state.rs:243), so they never spuriously block a numbered position.

The `Auto` sentinel is never passed into `start_position_taken_by_other_row` because it is hardcoded outside the loop; only `Position(x)` values flow through that function. No two rows can simultaneously hold the same `Position(x)` because the populated list omits taken positions, preventing the user from selecting a taken one.

The doc's Section 6 ("Rust does not yet model the reservation table") is stale — it pre-dates the current implementation in combos.rs. The current Rust output is bit-for-bit equivalent to gamemd's FUN_004E50C0 for all valid inputs (0..8 map starts, 0..8 slot rows, all ownership combinations). No observable disparity exists.

findings.title: Team None row insertion gated by must-ally matches gamemd vtable +0x2C semantics
findings.gamemd_behavior: FUN_004E5B60 inserts GUI:None (item data -2) only when the selected MPModes mode object's vtable +0x2C returns -2 (MustAlly false); when MustAlly is true it returns 0 and None is omitted, leaving A,B,C,D (item data 0..3).
findings.rust_behavior: Rust: when selected_mode_must_ally is true -> [0,1,2,3] (A-D only); else -> [-2,0,1,2,3] (None + A-D). Matches the MustAlly gate exactly. Verified-correct point, no disparity.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_SIDE_COUNTRY_TEAM_FINAL_WRITES_GHIDRA_REPORT.md sec 2 + OQ-SCT-007 (vtable +0x2C returns -2 when MustAlly false); Rust combos.rs:312-321.

verdict_obj.reasoning: Rust lines 311-322 (combos.rs) gate Team None insertion on `state.selected_mode_must_ally` exactly as described. The gamemd claim is verified by the dedicated doc SKIRMISH_TEAM_NONE_INSERTION_VTABLE_0X2C_GHIDRA_REPORT.md: vtable +0x2C at 0x005D5DC0 returns -2 when MustAlly byte at +0x3F is 0, and 0 when nonzero; FUN_004E5B60 inserts None only on negative return (OQ-TN-001 through OQ-TN-005 all resolved). The AlliesAllowed clearing edge case (gamemd constructor 0x005D5D0C-0x005D5D11) is also reproduced in skirmish_modes.rs:98-100 (`if !mode.allies_allowed { mode.must_ally = false; }`), and a test at line 286 covers exactly this boundary (AlliesAllowed=no + MustAlly=yes => must_ally false). Rust output is bit-identical to gamemd for all valid inputs including the AlliesAllowed boundary. No disparity exists.

findings.title: Click on rail (outside thumb) wrongly begins a drag in Rust; gamemd only drags when grabbing the thumb
findings.gamemd_behavior: In OwnerDraw_Trackbar_0061D950, mouse-down outside the thumb but in the y-band remaps the value ONCE and does NOT set the thumb-drag flag (iStack_124 stays 0). The WM_MOUSEMOVE path only remaps from the cursor when iStack_124 != 0, i.e. only when the initial mouse-down landed inside the thumb [thumb_x, thumb_x+12). So holding and dragging from the rail does nothing further; you must grab the thumb to drag.
findings.rust_behavior: handle_option_mouse_down sets state.trackbar_drag = Some{dragging_thumb:false} on an outside-thumb click (and remaps once). handle_option_mouse_move then continues to remap on every move whenever trackbar_drag.is_some(), ignoring dragging_thumb. Result: a rail click-and-drag continuously tracks the cursor — a drag that gamemd does not perform.
findings.evidence: Ghidra decompile_function 0x0061D950: thumb-drag flag iStack_124 set to 1 only in 'else if (param_2 == 0x201)' inside-thumb branch; cursor remap block guarded by 'if (iStack_124 == 0) {...} else { GetCursorPos; remap }', and WM_MOUSEMOVE invalidate guarded by 'if (iStack_124 != 0)'. Rust: trackbars.rs handle_option_mouse_move uses `let Some(drag) = state.trackbar_drag` with no dragging_thumb check.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust code at trackbars.rs:228-238 (`handle_option_mouse_move`) checks only `state.trackbar_drag.is_some()` — it does not inspect `drag.dragging_thumb`. This means a rail click (which sets `dragging_thumb: false` at lines 196-204) causes continuous cursor-remapping on every subsequent mouse-move while the button is held.

Ghidra decompile of `OwnerDraw_Trackbar_0061D950` (verified via `decompile_function 0x0061D950`) confirms the gamemd behavior exactly as stated in the finding. In the `LAB_0061e4ce` block handling WM_LBUTTONDOWN (0x201): when the click lands outside the thumb `[iStack_cc, uStack_128)`, the value is remapped once but `iStack_124` (the thumb-drag flag) remains 0 — only an inside-thumb click on WM_LBUTTONDOWN sets `iStack_124 = 1`. In the WM_MOUSEMOVE (0x200) handler: `InvalidateRect` (which triggers the WM_PAINT path using `GetCursorPos` to remap) is guarded by `if (iStack_124 != 0)`. A rail-click drag (`iStack_124 == 0`) with mouse button held (`param_3 & 1 != 0`) falls through to `LAB_0061e609` with no remap — the value is frozen after the initial rail click. Rust instead remaps on every move event whenever `trackbar_drag.is_some()`, ignoring `dragging_thumb`. The gamemd claim is correct and the Rust divergence is real.

findings.title: Credits/unit-count ranges, step, and defaults are hardcoded constants instead of read from [MultiplayerDialogSettings] rules INI
findings.gamemd_behavior: FUN_006AE6E0 seeds the trackbar ranges/step/initial position from RulesClass fields MinMoney(+0x1480)/MaxMoney(+0x1488)/MoneyIncrement(+0x148C)/Money, MinUnitCount(+0x1490)/MaxUnitCount(+0x1498)/UnitCount, and GameSpeed, which come from [MultiplayerDialogSettings] in rules(md).ini. The shell ranges therefore change if the INI is modded.
findings.rust_behavior: CREDITS_MIN=5000, CREDITS_MAX=10000, CREDITS_STEP=100, UNIT_COUNT_MIN=0, UNIT_COUNT_MAX=10, GAME_SPEED 0..6, and the defaults 10000/10/1 are compile-time constants. They equal stock YR INI values so stock output is identical, but they are not parsed from rules(md).ini; modded MinMoney/MaxMoney/MoneyIncrement/MinUnitCount/MaxUnitCount/Money/UnitCount/GameSpeed would not change the sliders.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_TRACKBAR_DISABLED_RUNTIME_ENABLE_FLOW_GHIDRA_REPORT.md section 2/4 and SKIRMISH_CHECKBOX_TRACKBAR_PIXEL_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md section 6 (ranges from Rules +0x1480..+0x1498, step +0x148C); ini/rulesmd.ini:3017..3026. Rust constants in trackbars.rs:17-25 and game_options.rs:72-75 are literals.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side verified at src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/trackbars.rs:17-25: CREDITS_MIN=5000, CREDITS_MAX=10000, CREDITS_STEP=100, UNIT_COUNT_MIN=0, UNIT_COUNT_MAX=10 are compile-time constants. src/sim/game_options.rs:72-75 hardcodes starting_credits=10000, unit_count=10, game_speed=1. No Rust code reads MinMoney/MaxMoney/MoneyIncrement/MinUnitCount/MaxUnitCount from the INI (confirmed by grep returning zero matches in src/ for those keys).

gamemd side confirmed by multiple research docs: RULESCLASS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md section "[MultiplayerDialogSettings] (00671EA0)" lists MinMoney at RulesClass+0x1480, Money+0x1484, MaxMoney+0x1488, MoneyIncrement+0x148C, MinUnitCount+0x1490, UnitCount+0x1494, MaxUnitCount+0x1498 — all read by RulesClass__ReadMultiplayerDialogSettings @ 0x00671EA0. The SKIRMISH_MPMODES_OVERRIDE_PAYLOAD_VALUES_GHIDRA_REPORT.md and SKIRMISH_MPMODES_MIX_OVERRIDE_PAYLOADS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md both cite this function reading all these keys. SKIRMISH_CREDITS_TRACKBAR_CLICK_DRAG_TRACE.md line 70 explicitly flags this as an adjacent finding: "gamemd sources them from Rules fields. This should become data-driven before accepting modded rules parity."

For stock rulesmd.ini (lines 3018-3024) the constants happen to be numerically identical, so stock-YR output is bit-identical. However under CLAUDE.md burden of proof, "same value in one sampled trace" is explicitly not an acceptable refutation. Modded rules (different MinMoney/MaxMoney/MoneyIncrement/MinUnitCount/MaxUnitCount) would produce different slider ranges and defaults in gamemd but not in Rust — confirmed drift. The finding's facts on both sides are stated correctly.

findings.title: WM_HSCROLL high word is masked with &0xffff in Rust but gamemd does an unmasked shift
findings.gamemd_behavior: OwnerDraw_Trackbar_0061D950 builds the notification wParam as `(uVar5 + uVar10) * 0x10000 | 5` where (uVar5+uVar10) = min + relative = the absolute visual value, with NO &0xffff mask before the <<16.
findings.rust_behavior: trackbar_hscroll_wparam does `((visual_value as u32) & 0xffff) << 16 | LOW_WORD`. For all stock values (game speed 0..6, credits <=10000, unit count <=10) min+rel never exceeds 16 bits, so output is bit-identical to gamemd. This is an internal-encoding-only difference (the consumer reads the high word back); listed for completeness and because the doc previously asserted a &0xffff that the binary does not contain.
findings.evidence: Ghidra decompile_function 0x0061D950 final branch: `SendMessageA(hWnd,0x114,(uVar5 + uVar10) * 0x10000 | 5, ...)` — no AND mask. Rust trackbars.rs:139 applies `& 0xffff`. Equivalent for all reachable values (proven by value range), so no observable drift.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Ghidra decompile of `0x0061D950` (live call this session) confirms the gamemd SendMessageA line: `SendMessageA(hWnd, 0x114, (uVar5 + uVar10) * 0x10000 | 5, ...)` — no `& 0xffff` mask before the shift. The finding's gamemd claim is accurate.

Rust at `trackbars.rs:138-141` applies `(((visual_value as u32) & 0xffff) << 16) | 5`. The mask is present in Rust and absent in gamemd — the internal encoding differs.

Equivalence proof (algebraic, covering full reachable input space): the three trackbars have these ranges from `trackbars.rs:17-25`: GameSpeed `[0, 6]`, Credits `[5000, 10000]`, UnitCount `[0, 10]`. All are non-negative and at most 10000 = 0x2710. For any integer `v` with `0 <= v <= 10000`, we have `v < 2^16`, so `v & 0xffff == v` identically. Therefore `((v as u32) & 0xffff) << 16 == v * 0x10000` for every value the skirmish shell can emit. The wParam written to SendMessageA is bit-identical in all reachable cases. No boundary case (negative input, v >= 65536) is reachable given the clamped trackbar ranges. Output is proven equivalent — false positive.

findings.title: WM_LBUTTONDBLCLK on the rail remaps in gamemd; Rust has no double-click path
findings.gamemd_behavior: In OwnerDraw_Trackbar_0061D950, WM_LBUTTONDBLCLK (0x203) reaches LAB_0061e4ce and performs the same outside-thumb remap / inside-thumb drag-start logic as a normal mouse-down.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust only routes mouse down/move/up to the trackbar; a double-click is delivered as a second mouse-down so it remaps a second time to the same value, producing the same end state. Observably indistinguishable in normal play (the dblclk just re-sets the value already set by the first down), but the message path differs.
findings.evidence: Ghidra decompile_function 0x0061D950: `if (param_2 != 0x203) {...} LAB_0061e4ce:` — 0x203 falls through to the same down/remap logic. Rust app dispatches dblclk as another mouse-down. End-state identical for trackbars (idempotent remap).

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Verified via decompile_function 0x0061D950 and reading src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/trackbars.rs lines 160-210 and src/app.rs lines 1820-1841.

Gamemd binary (LAB_0061e4ce): When param_2 == 0x203 (WM_LBUTTONDBLCLK), the code explicitly branches on `if (param_2 == 0x201)` inside LAB_0061e4ce — meaning LBUTTONDBLCLK sets iStack_100=0 and calls ReleaseCapture(), while LBUTTONDOWN sets iStack_100=1 and calls SetCapture(). Additionally, the inside-thumb drag start (`else if (param_2 == 0x201) { iStack_124 = 1; }`) is gated on LBUTTONDOWN only — LBUTTONDBLCLK does NOT enter drag mode for an inside-thumb hit.

Rust (app.rs:1820-1841): winit delivers `WindowEvent::MouseInput { state: Pressed, button: Left }` for both the first and second click of a double-click sequence (winit has no separate double-click event). The second press calls `handle_skirmish_shell_mouse_down`, which calls `handle_option_mouse_down`. That function resets `trackbar_drag = None` (line 167) and then, if the cursor is over the thumb, re-enters `dragging_thumb: true` drag mode (line 192). There is no capture concept in Rust.

Observable difference: In gamemd, a double-click on the trackbar thumb releases capture and does NOT start a new drag — subsequent mouse-move before mouse-up does not move the thumb. In Rust, the second click re-enters drag mode, so mouse-move after the dblclk does move the thumb. This is player-visible whenever a user double-clicks the thumb (e.g., trying to make small precise adjustments with rapid clicks).

The finding's gamemd claim that LBUTTONDBLCLK performs "the same inside-thumb drag-start logic as a normal mouse-down" is incorrect — gamemd explicitly blocks drag-start on LBUTTONDBLCLK and releases capture instead.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding states LBUTTONDBLCLK performs "the same outside-thumb remap / inside-thumb drag-start logic as a normal mouse-down." This is wrong for the inside-thumb path. At LAB_0061e4ce, gamemd branches on `if (param_2 == 0x201)`: LBUTTONDOWN sets capture and starts drag (iStack_124=1); LBUTTONDBLCLK releases capture (ReleaseCapture) and does NOT set iStack_124, so no drag is started. The claim that the two messages are observably indistinguishable is also incorrect — after a dblclk on the thumb, gamemd does not track mouse-move, while Rust re-enters drag mode and does.

findings.title: Main-menu buttons do a 1 Hz hover frame swap (button_hover art) that gamemd never performs
findings.gamemd_behavior: Main-menu owner-draw buttons (control IDs 0x683/0x684/0x578/0x686/0x55C/0x3EE) use the PCX visual path (iVar14 == piVar17[0x2c] == 0). In that branch OwnerDraw_Button_00612B70 selects the 'u'/'d' PCX suffix from ONLY the pressed bit (pWStack_d8 & 1 == piVar17[0x3a] & 1) and NEVER reads the +0xC5 blink byte. The +0xC5 blink/highlight frame is read exclusively in the SHP paths iVar14 == 1/2/3 (g_SDBTNANM_SHP and other shell buttons). Furthermore no 0x4DC sender targets dialog 0xE2 at all, so +0xC4/+0xC5 are never even set there. Net: hovering a main-menu button produces NO art change in gamemd; only pressing it does.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust computes hover_highlight = elapsed_since_hover/1000 % 2 == 1 and swaps to atlas.button_hover for the hovered (non-pressed) button, producing a 1 Hz default<->highlight art flash on hover. This is a visible animated highlight that does not exist in gamemd.
findings.evidence: decompile_function 0x00612B70: PCX branch `iVar14==0` sets uStack_f4._3_1_ from `(uint)pWStack_d8 & 1` only ('u'/'d'); +0xC5 read only under iVar14==1/2/3. HOVER_DISPATCHER_FUN_007B66C0_FAMILY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §4.3 ('PCX path reads only pressed-bit, not +0xC5') and §1 ('Hovering a main-menu owner-draw button on dialog 0xE2 has NO visual effect'). The 0x4DC=hover hypothesis in the older MAIN_MENU_HOVER_MESSAGES report is explicitly REFUTED there (§6).

verdict_obj.reasoning:
The finding's entire gamemd premise is refuted by `MAIN_MENU_BUTTON_DISPATCH_LAB_0060A330_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` (2026-05-19, HIGH confidence, verified via live `decompile_function 0x00612B70` and `disassemble_function 0x00612B70`).

**What the finding claims about gamemd:** Dialog 0xE2 owner-draw buttons use the PCX path (`iVar14 == 0`) in `OwnerDraw_Button_00612B70`, so `+0xC5` is never read, and hovering produces no art change.

**What the binary actually shows:** `LAB_0060A330` (an `EnumChildWindows` callback) writes `record+0xB0 = 1` for all six dialog 0xE2 owner-draw buttons at `WM_INITDIALOG` time (verified: `MOV EAX, 1; MOV [EDX+0xB0], EAX` at the callback exit; called from `FUN_00622820` and `FUN_00622B50`). When `OwnerDraw_Button_00612B70` runs, `iVar14 = piVar17[0x2c]` therefore equals 1, not 0. Dialog 0xE2 buttons take the **SHP path (`iVar14 == 1`, SDBTNANM frames 2/3/4)**, not the PCX path.

In the SHP path (`iVar14 == 1`), `+0xC5` IS read: when `+0xC5 != '\0'` and not pressed, `local_f0 = 0x3` (frame 3, the hover/focus-flash frame). This is directly stated in `MAIN_MENU_BUTTON_DISPATCH_LAB_0060A330_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` §1.1, decompile excerpt lines 58-60. LAB_0060A330 §1.1 also states: "In retail YR the practical effect under a standard mouse-over IS a frame-3 swap, so the 'hover' state in the existing Rust port (SDBTNANM frame 3) is visually correct."

**Rust behavior (src/app_main_menu_shell_render.rs:54-66, 135-167):** `button_frame` returns `atlas.button_hover` when `hover_highlight` is true; `hover_highlight` is derived from `elapsed_since_hover/1000 % 2 == 1` (lines 150-153). This selects frame 3 (SDBTNANM) on hover, which matches the gamemd output per LAB_0060A330.

**Secondary contradiction:** The finding cites `HOVER_DISPATCHER_FUN_007B66C0_FAMILY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §4.3` as authority for "PCX path reads only pressed-bit, not +0xC5." That section's §4.3 opening sentence is "this is what main-menu uses" referring to `iVar14 == 0` — but this claim was made before LAB_0060A330 proved 0xE2 uses `iVar14 == 1`. The HOVER_DISPATCHER doc is stale on that specific attribution (it correctly describes the PCX path, but incorrectly attributes it to dialog 0xE2). LAB_0060A330 is the more authoritative and more recent finding.

**Timing nuance (not a refutation issue):** Rust uses continuous wall-clock elapsed time to derive the hover frame toggle, while gamemd uses a WM_TIMER fired 1 second after 0x4DC delivery with a 1-second period — meaning gamemd starts at frame 2 for 1 second before first switching to frame 3, while Rust starts at frame 2 for 1 second too (elapsed / 1000 % 2 == 1 is false for the first second). The periods match (1 Hz). This is a minor internal-mechanism difference but the output cadence is the same: frame 2 for ~1 s, then alternating 1 s each. This does not affect the false-positive verdict on the core claim.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding incorrectly states dialog 0xE2 uses the PCX path (iVar14 == 0) in OwnerDraw_Button_00612B70. The correct gamemd behavior: LAB_0060A330 writes record+0xB0 = 1 for all six dialog 0xE2 buttons at WM_INITDIALOG, so iVar14 == 1 (SDBTNANM SHP path) is used. In this path, +0xC5 IS read, and frame 3 IS selected on hover. Hovering a main-menu button in gamemd DOES produce a visual art change (frame 3 swap), making the Rust hover-frame behavior correct, not drift. The finding's §gamemd_behavior paragraph is wrong on both the path taken (PCX vs SHP) and the consequence (no art change vs frame-3 swap).

findings.title: hover_started_at timer state and its 'gamemd arms SetTimer(...,1000,...) on hover' comment are based on a refuted mechanism
findings.gamemd_behavior: The 1000 ms SetTimer in OwnerDraw_Button_00612B70's 0x4DC handler is an input-validation error-blink, triggered by network/WOL dialog validators sending 0x4DC to control 0x59F (netdlg2.cpp / wonline.cpp). Dialog 0xE2 has no 0x59F control and receives no 0x4DC, so no such timer is ever armed on a main-menu button. The 'do not re-arm if already armed' guard (piVar17[0x31]==0) the Rust comment cites is the error-set guard, not a hover guard.
findings.rust_behavior: state.rs tracks hover_started_at (set on hover-identity change, cleared on unhover) explicitly to drive the 1 Hz frame toggle, with a comment asserting gamemd arms this timer 'in the WM_LBUTTONDOWN/hover-mutator path'. The mechanism does not exist for the main menu; the state and its only consumer (the phantom frame swap above) should not exist.
findings.evidence: HOVER_DISPATCHER_FUN_007B66C0_FAMILY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3 (all 6 push-0x4DC sites target GetDlgItem(_,0x59F) in netdlg2.cpp/wonline.cpp), §3.4 (0x59F not in 0xE2 control list), §6 (corrections: 'On error-set, SetTimer... Trigger is input validation failure, not mouse hover'). decompile_function 0x00612B70 confirms 0x4DC case is the only SetTimer(...,0,1000,...) site and it is gated by piVar17[0x31] error-state, unrelated to mouse position.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust code confirmed at state.rs:37-41 and app_main_menu_shell_render.rs:146-156.

state.rs:37-40 comments assert gamemd arms `SetTimer(hwnd, 0, 1000, NULL)` "in the WM_LBUTTONDOWN/hover-mutator path" with `piVar17[0x31] == 0` as the hover guard. Both claims are wrong per HOVER_DISPATCHER_FUN_007B66C0_FAMILY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3 and §4.1 (verified via decompile_function 0x00612B70 and search_byte_patterns 68 DC 04 00 00): the six `push 0x4DC` sites all target GetDlgItem(_, 0x59F) in netdlg2.cpp / wonline.cpp; `piVar17[0x31]` at offset +0xC4 is the error-state active boolean, not a hover guard; and dialog 0xE2 has no control 0x59F (§3.4 RT_DIALOG parse), so 0x4DC is never delivered to any main-menu button.

The consumer at app_main_menu_shell_render.rs:150-156 drives a 1 Hz wall-clock frame toggle selecting SDBTNANM frame 3 (highlight) vs frame 2 (default) based on hover_started_at. In gamemd, +0xC5 (the blink-frame byte) is only ever set by the 0x4DC handler's SetTimer→WM_TIMER chain (HOVER_DISPATCHER §4.1-4.2, decompile_function 0x00612B70). Because 0x4DC never reaches 0xE2 buttons, +0xC5 stays 0 permanently for main-menu buttons, and WM_PAINT's iVar14==1 branch always selects frame 2 (HOVER_DISPATCHER §4.3: "local_f0 = 0x2; if +0xC5 != 0: local_f0 = 0x3"). Frame 3 is never rendered on hover in retail. The Rust engine renders frame 3 on hover — a visible frame difference every time the cursor touches a main-menu button.

The MAIN_MENU_BUTTON_DISPATCH_LAB_0060A330_GHIDRA_REPORT.md line 79-81 says "the practical effect under a standard mouse-over IS a frame-3 swap" — this is a retained error from the pre-HOVER_DISPATCHER swarm; the HOVER_DISPATCHER doc (same date, more thorough) conclusively refutes it at §1 and §6, and the HOVER_DISPATCHER investigation is authoritative (direct binary enumeration of all 6 push-0x4DC sites, full decompile of OwnerDraw_Button_00612B70, RT_DIALOG control list cross-check).

Finding's stated gamemd mechanism is accurate. hover_started_at and the frame-3 toggle should not exist for dialog 0xE2.

findings.title: Tooltip status line updates only on hovered-control identity change, not on every mouse-move like gamemd's WM_NCHITTEST
findings.rust_location: src/ui/main_menu_shell/state.rs:116-122 (mouse_move updates hovered_owner_draw_button only on change); src/app_main_menu_shell_render.rs:291-301 (tooltip drawn from hovered_button)
findings.gamemd_behavior: FUN_00622B50's WM_NCHITTEST handler runs on EVERY mouse-move over the dialog client area; each invocation re-hit-tests the child, calls FUN_006040B0, loads the CSF string, and sends 0x4B2 to control 0x695 to repaint the status line unconditionally (no ToolTips gate). The status line text result is recomputed continuously while the cursor moves.
findings.rust_behavior: The visible text result is the same string per hovered button, so the displayed text matches; but the recompute/repaint cadence is identity-gated rather than per-frame. This is observationally equivalent for the text content itself, so the only potential divergence is repaint timing, not shown text. Surfaced for completeness; low practical visibility.
findings.evidence: MAIN_MENU_DIALOG_0XE2_TOOLTIP_HOVER_FLOW_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §2 (WM_NCHITTEST fires every mouse move, sends 0x4B2 each time), 'Trigger frequency: every WM_NCHITTEST message'. state.rs:118 only mutates hovered_owner_draw_button when new_hover != current.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side check (state.rs:116-122, app_main_menu_shell_render.rs:291-301, 432-437): The identity gate at state.rs:118 only controls when `hovered_owner_draw_button` is mutated and `hover_started_at` is reset. It does NOT gate the repaint. `build_text_draws` is called every GPU frame (line 432) with the current `hovered_owner_draw_button` value, and the `if let Some(id) = hovered_button` block at line 291 emits the tooltip label unconditionally on every frame while any button is hovered. `tooltip_csf_key_for_control(id)` and `resolve_csf` also run every frame (lines 292-293). So both the recompute and the repaint are continuous, not identity-gated.

Gamemd side (MAIN_MENU_DIALOG_0XE2_TOOLTIP_HOVER_FLOW_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §2): On every WM_NCHITTEST, gamemd re-hits-tests, re-resolves the CSF key, and sends 0x4B2 to control 0x695, which blits and calls InvalidateRect unconditionally. This is functionally continuous repaint per mouse-move event.

The finding claims "the recompute/repaint cadence is identity-gated" for Rust — this is factually wrong about the repaint cadence. Rust repaints every GPU frame regardless of button-identity changes. The identity gate is only a state-mutation guard (matching gamemd's SetTimer guard, as the comment at state.rs:113-115 correctly documents). The observable output — tooltip text drawn on screen — is produced on every render frame in both engines. No output divergence exists: same text, continuously displayed, no frame skip. The finding's own hedge ("the only potential divergence is repaint timing, not shown text") is also refuted: Rust's per-frame GPU render fires at display refresh rate, at least as frequently as gamemd's WM_NCHITTEST-driven invalidation at mouse-move rate.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding mis-states the Rust repaint cadence. The identity gate at state.rs:118 controls only when `hovered_owner_draw_button` (and `hover_started_at`) are mutated — it does not gate the tooltip render. `build_text_draws` runs every GPU frame (app_main_menu_shell_render.rs:432-437) and emits the tooltip label on every frame as long as a button is hovered. Both recompute (CSF key lookup) and repaint are continuous in Rust, not identity-gated.

findings.title: Tooltip status-line text is drawn H-centered in Rust; gamemd 0x695 alignment (style-bit driven) is unverified
findings.rust_location: src/app_main_menu_shell_render.rs:291-301 (push_label ... ShellAlign::H_CENTER) over layout.tooltip_line (455 px wide at 800x600)
findings.gamemd_behavior: Control 0x695 (OwnerDraw_Static_006153E0) draws its text via FUN_00621040 with an alignment/flags value (uVar10 = 0x10/0x11/0x12) derived from the control's window style bits (WS_*). The actual horizontal alignment of the bottom-left status line depends on the 0xE2 dialog-template style bits for 0x695, which were not extracted. With a ~455 px-wide rect, left vs center vs right is a large, very visible difference.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust hard-codes H_CENTER for the tooltip line. If gamemd's 0x695 is left-aligned (the conventional status-line layout), the hover text would sit at a different horizontal position than gamemd.
findings.evidence: decompile_function 0x006153E0: WM_PAINT (case 0xF, piVar11[0x1c]==0/1) calls FUN_00621040(&pHStack_50, piVar11[0x19], color, uVar10, piVar11[0x2b], ...) where uVar10 is selected from GetWindowLongA(param_1,-0x10) style bits (0x10/0x11/0x12). Alignment not resolvable without the 0xE2 template style bits for 0x695. MAIN_MENU_DIALOG_0XE2_TOOLTIP_HOVER_FLOW_GHIDRA_REPORT.md does not specify alignment.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed: `src/app_main_menu_shell_render.rs:299` passes `ShellAlign::H_CENTER` to `push_label` for `layout.tooltip_line`, which calls `draw_in_rect` and horizontally centers the text within the ~455 px-wide rect.

gamemd side verified via binary: The dialog template for 0xE2 is stored as an RT_DIALOG resource found via `FindResourceA` → resource directory at `0x00b7aa10` → RESOURCE_DATA_ENTRY at `0x00b7b3f8` (OffsetToData RVA `0x3F74C4` → VA `0x00BF74C4` in `.rsrc`). Template is DLGTEMPLATEEX (header bytes `01 00 FF FF`). Control 0x695 item starts at `0x00BF7660` (verified by `read_memory 0x00BF7660`): DLGITEMTEMPLATEEX with helpID=0, exStyle=0, style bytes `[00 02 00 50]` = DWORD `0x50000200` (WS_CHILD|WS_VISIBLE|SS_CENTERIMAGE), x=2, y=355, cx=303, cy=12, id=`95 06 00 00` = 0x695, windowClass=`FF FF 82 00` (predefined STATIC 0x82). Style bits 0 and 1 are both 0 (`0x50000200 & 1 = 0`, `& 2 = 0`).

In `OwnerDraw_Static_006153E0` WM_PAINT (decompiled at `0x006153E0`): `uVar3 = GetWindowLongA(param_1, -0x10)` → `uVar10 = 0x10` (left) by default; `if (uVar3 & 1) uVar10 = 0x11` (center); `if (uVar3 & 2) uVar10 = 0x12` (right). With style=0x50000200, bits 0,1 = 0, so `uVar10 = 0x10` = LEFT-aligned. Text is drawn LEFT-aligned in gamemd.

Rust hard-codes H_CENTER; gamemd draws LEFT. The finding's claim that alignment was "unverified" is resolved: it is definitively LEFT. The text position difference is approximately (cx/2 - text_width/2) ≈ up to ~200px horizontal offset for short tooltip strings in a 455px-wide rect — large and player-visible on every button hover.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding stated alignment was "unverified" and speculated it might be left-aligned. This is now confirmed: control 0x695's DLGITEMTEMPLATEEX style in the dialog template is 0x50000200 (WS_CHILD|WS_VISIBLE|SS_CENTERIMAGE), bits 0,1 = 0, yielding SS_LEFT = left-aligned text in the WM_PAINT handler. The gamemd claim in the finding is otherwise accurate (alignment driven by window style bits 0 and 1 via GetWindowLongA). The 0xE2 template style bits the finding said were "not extracted" have now been extracted from the binary resource at 0x00BF7660.

findings.title: Main menu shows OS cursor; gamemd hides the OS cursor and software-blits MOUSE.SHA frame 0
findings.rust_location: src/app_main_menu_shell_render.rs:371-529 (render_main_menu_shell_to_target draws movie/chrome/buttons/text only — no cursor sprite drawn); no ShowCursor/cursor-hide path in the shell render
findings.gamemd_behavior: Over dialog 0xE2 the OS cursor is hidden for the entire process lifetime (WWMouseClass::Constructor calls ShowCursor(0)), and the engine software-blits the MOUSE.SHA cursor (cursor ID 0, frame 0, hotspot 0,0, single frame, no animation, no hover variants) into the back surface every flush. The player sees the RA2 SHP arrow, not the Windows arrow.
findings.rust_behavior: The egui/winit-based main-menu shell does not hide the OS cursor and does not draw the MOUSE.SHA SHP cursor; the player sees the native Windows cursor over the main menu. Different cursor bitmap (and hotspot semantics) than gamemd.
findings.evidence: MAIN_MENU_CURSOR_SHP_AND_RULES_GHIDRA_REPORT.md Executive Summary + 'What the player sees' (MOUSE.SHA frame 0, OS cursor hidden via ShowCursor(0)). The Rust shell render file contains no cursor draw or OS-cursor-hide call.

verdict_obj.reasoning: Rust side confirmed by direct code inspection: `render_main_menu_shell_to_target` (src/app_main_menu_shell_render.rs:387-529) draws only movie/chrome/buttons/text — no cursor sprite, no OS-cursor-hide call. `AppState.software_cursor` is initialized to `None` at startup (app.rs:2138) and is only assigned during the map-load transition path (app_transitions.rs:130, sourced from app_init.rs:1025). `use_software_cursor()` (app.rs:399-401) returns false when `software_cursor` is `None`, so the OS cursor-hide logic in the CursorMoved handler (app.rs:1784-1785) and egui integration (render/egui_integration.rs:129) does not fire during the main menu. The `build_ui_instances` cursor-draw path (app_render/build_instances.rs:489) is only reached during in-game rendering, not from the main-menu shell render. gamemd side confirmed via MAIN_MENU_CURSOR_SHP_AND_RULES_GHIDRA_REPORT.md (Ghidra-verified): `WWMouseClass::Constructor` at 0x007B8730 calls `ShowCursor(0)` at process startup, hiding the OS cursor for its entire lifetime; the engine then software-blits MOUSE.SHA frame 0 (hotspot 0,0, no animation, no hover variants) into the back surface every frame. No refutation possible: the drift is real and directly observable — the player sees a native Windows arrow over the main menu instead of the RA2 SHP arrow cursor.

findings.title: Yuri Website control ID modeled as 0x71B and excluded from hover hit-test; gamemd maps the website BUTTON 0x55F to STT:MainButtonYuriWebSite
findings.rust_location: src/ui/main_menu_shell/state.rs:15 (YuriWebsite0x71b), :91 (tooltip key STT:MainButtonYuriWebSite for 0x71b); layout.rs:305-330 (buttons[] excludes the website control, so it is never hit-tested for hover)
findings.gamemd_behavior: FUN_006040B0's 0xE2 branch maps control 0x55F -> STT:MainButtonYuriWebSite (the Yuri website button). The hover/status tooltip for the website link is keyed off control 0x55F, which is hit-tested by ChildWindowFromPointEx like the other buttons (present only when SpecialFlags enables the web link). 0x71B is a different static control, not the website button.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust names the website control 0x71B (a static per HOVER_DISPATCHER doc) and, because it is not in the 6-entry buttons layout, it is never hit-tested by hit_test_owner_draw_button, so hovering the website link never surfaces its STT:MainButtonYuriWebSite tooltip. Wrong control ID and missing hover tooltip for the web link.
findings.evidence: MAIN_MENU_DIALOG_0XE2_TOOLTIP_HOVER_FLOW_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §1 table (0x55F -> STT:MainButtonYuriWebSite) and 'CSF Keys Used' table (Yuri Website = control 0x55F). HOVER_DISPATCHER doc §3.4 lists 0xE2 statics as 0x71B/0x71C/0x694/0x695/0x71D (0x71B is a static). state.rs:15 uses YuriWebsite0x71b; layout buttons array omits it.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed via Read:
- `state.rs:15`: enum variant is `YuriWebsite0x71b` (ID 0x71B)
- `state.rs:91`: `tooltip_csf_key_for_control` returns `"STT:MainButtonYuriWebSite"` for that variant — the CSF key string is correct
- `layout.rs:305-330`: `buttons[]` contains exactly 6 entries (SinglePlayer 0x683, WwOnline 0x684, Network 0x578, MoviesAndCredits 0x686, Options 0x55C, ExitGame 0x3EE); `YuriWebsite0x71b` is absent
- `hit_test_owner_draw_button` (state.rs:95-105) iterates only `layout.buttons`, so hovering the website link area returns `None` — the status line is never updated with `STT:MainButtonYuriWebSite`

gamemd side confirmed via MAIN_MENU_DIALOG_0XE2_TOOLTIP_HOVER_FLOW_GHIDRA_REPORT.md:
- §1 table (line 70): control `0x55F` maps to `STT:MainButtonYuriWebSite` in `FUN_006040B0`'s 0xE2 branch — HIGH confidence (decompiled, all 7 control IDs and CSF keys visible)
- "CSF Keys Used" table (line 200): Yuri Website → `0x55F` confirmed
- §2: `FUN_00622B50`'s WM_NCHITTEST handler uses `ChildWindowFromPointEx` which naturally reaches all child controls including `0x55F` — the website button IS hit-testable in gamemd

Two distinct drifts both confirmed:
1. Wrong control ID in enum name (`0x71B` vs `0x55F`); this is cosmetic in Rust since the enum is not compared against raw Win32 IDs, but signals the wrong mental model
2. The website button is completely absent from `layout.rs`'s `buttons[]`, so `hit_test_owner_draw_button` never returns `YuriWebsite0x71b`, and the `STT:MainButtonYuriWebSite` tooltip path in `tooltip_csf_key_for_control` is dead code — gamemd surfaces this tooltip on every mouse-move over the website link

No refutation basis: the CSF key string is correct in Rust but unreachable. Cannot claim equivalence — the tooltip is never shown.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding's gamemd claim is accurate: control 0x55F (not 0x71B) maps to STT:MainButtonYuriWebSite in FUN_006040B0. The doc also notes that 0x71B is NOT listed among the statics that return null (those are 0x694, 0x71A, 0x71C, 0x71D), so 0x71B's identity in the binary is not positively confirmed by the doc — it may be unlisted or absent. The finding's description of the Rust side is accurate: the website control is absent from buttons[], making the tooltip unreachable. The primary player-visible drift is the missing hover tooltip, not the enum name typo.

findings.title: Status line text rendering correctly clears when not over a button, but is never shown blank/initial via 0x695 control surface
findings.rust_location: src/app_main_menu_shell_render.rs:291-301 (tooltip drawn only when hovered_button is Some; nothing drawn into tooltip_line otherwise)
findings.gamemd_behavior: When the cursor is over a control with no CSF mapping (movie 0x71A, heading 0x694, version 0x71C/0x71D, the status static itself 0x695) or empty area, FUN_006040B0 returns null and the WM_NCHITTEST handler sets control 0x695 to the global empty wide string DAT_00887734 — i.e. the status line is explicitly repainted blank. The 0x695 static remains a persistent owner-draw control on screen at all times (its backing surface exists; only the text is empty).
findings.rust_behavior: Rust draws nothing for the tooltip line when no button is hovered. The observable text result (blank) matches; there is no separate persistent 0x695 control surface, but since gamemd's blank is also visually empty, this is observationally equivalent for the bottom-left text. Surfaced as low-visibility completeness note.
findings.evidence: MAIN_MENU_DIALOG_0XE2_TOOLTIP_HOVER_FLOW_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §1 ('When FUN_006040B0 returns null, the status line is cleared (set to a global empty wide string &DAT_00887734)') and §3 (0x695 initial display blank, GUI:Blank).

verdict_obj.reasoning: Rust side verified at src/app_main_menu_shell_render.rs:291-301: when hovered_button is None, no push_label is emitted for tooltip_line. The render pass (src/app_main_menu_shell_render.rs:465-486) uses wgpu::LoadOp::Clear(CLEAR_COLOR) on every frame, wiping the entire framebuffer before any draw calls. The movie, chrome, and button layers then paint on top, leaving the tooltip_line area showing the underlying movie/chrome art — no stale text can persist between frames. In gamemd, control 0x695 is an owner-draw static whose 0x4B2 handler (verified in MAIN_MENU_DIALOG_0XE2_TOOLTIP_HOVER_FLOW_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3) repaints the backing surface and calls InvalidateRect, which causes WM_PAINT to fill the region with the dialog backdrop before drawing any text. When the empty wide string DAT_00887734 is sent, the control repaints to blank (backdrop visible). Both paths yield identical pixel output in the tooltip_line region: background art is visible, no text. The finding's own rust_behavior section acknowledges "the observable text result (blank) matches" but labels it drift anyway because Rust has no persistent 0x695 surface. That internal difference does not produce any observable difference — the full-frame GPU clear is an algebraic substitute for the per-control InvalidateRect+WM_PAINT cycle: both erase any prior content before compositing the current frame's layers. No 1-pixel, 1-frame, or timing disparity is introduced by this mechanism difference.

findings.title: First loading-renderer composition omits the mmpb.shp assigned-player marker pass
findings.gamemd_behavior: The first verified Skirmish loading renderer (0x00552D60) composes in order: (0) BSurface setup, (1) ls640/ls800<country>.shp background frame 0, (2) FUN_00640A40 assigned-player marker subpass drawing mmpb.shp frame 0 at the LS background's start-position anchors (marker offsets -3 X, -2 Y, color from assigned house ColorScheme+0x30C) when assigned slots/color schemes exist, (3) text labels, then later (4) PROGBARM.SHP progress. The mmpb markers draw ON the loading background before the bar.
findings.rust_behavior: build_native_loading_instances builds only four layers: background (G1), solid backing fill (G3), clipped PROGBARM bar (G2), and an optional side icon (G4). No mmpb.shp assigned-player markers are drawn over the LS background at any point.
findings.evidence: LOADING_FIRST_RENDERER_00552D60_GHIDRA_REPORT.md Composition Ledger order 2 (FUN_00640A40 @ 0x00553687, mmpb.shp frame 0) and Asset Role Matrix; SKIRMISH_MMPB_ASSIGNED_PLAYER_MARKER_CONTEXT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md. Rust: src/app_loading.rs:782-836 has no mmpb layer.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed at src/app_loading.rs:782-836: `build_native_loading_instances` builds exactly four layers (background G1, solid fill G3, clipped PROGBARM bar G2, side icon G4). No mmpb layer exists at any depth or conditional path. Grep of all src/ for "mmpb" returns only src/render/skirmish_shell_chrome.rs, where it is loaded as `assigned_player_marker_mmpb` for the offline setup-dialog preview context — not the loading-screen path. The loading-screen function has zero reference to mmpb or assigned-player markers.

Gamemd side confirmed from two Ghidra-verified docs. LOADING_FIRST_RENDERER_00552D60_GHIDRA_REPORT.md Composition Ledger order 2 (verified via decompile `0x00552D60`, xref `0x00553687`, decompile `0x00640A40`, string `mmpb.shp @ 0x00836DF4`): after the ls640/ls800country.shp background draw, `0x00552D60` calls `FUN_00640A40` at address `0x00553687`, passing the manager surface. SKIRMISH_MMPB_ASSIGNED_PLAYER_MARKER_CONTEXT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md confirms `FUN_00640A40`'s only recovered direct caller is `0x00553687` inside `0x00552D60`; the function iterates assigned slots from `ScenarioClass+0x1180`, draws mmpb.shp frame 0 at offsets -3 X / -2 Y with color from house color scheme `+0x30C` for each passing gate.

Trigger frequency: In any normal Skirmish (game mode 5), `ScenarioClass__Full_Init` calls `0x00552D60`, and the slot table at `+0x1180` has at least the human player's start assignment populated — the gates (valid waypoint, slot not -1, mmpb loaded, color scheme non-null) pass for every assigned human slot. "Every-match" is accurate for the human player's marker. The finding's stated conditionality is not a TS-legacy or off-by-default path; it is conditional only on the trivially-satisfied condition that players are assigned to starts, which is true in every Skirmish match.

No refutation basis: the gamemd claim is algebraically distinct from the Rust output (0 mmpb draws vs ≥1 draw per assigned player on loading screen), the Rust code has no mmpb layer in the loading path, and "conditional" does not downgrade the finding because the condition fires every normal match.

findings.title: Progress bar fill/backing use ramp-shade approximations instead of ColorScheme +0x308/+0x30C
findings.gamemd_behavior: FUN_00643400 fills the full PROGBARM frame rect with a solid background color derived from the local player's ColorScheme+0x308 (because Skirmish sets +0x71=1), then draws the clipped SHP span remapped through ColorScheme+0x30C convert (because +0x74 override is 0). These are specific palette entries, not ramp shades.
findings.rust_behavior: Backing uses player ramp shade index 11; filled bar uses ramp shade index 0 as a tint on the baked PROGBARM frame. Code comments explicitly call this a 'value-precision follow-up' and 'a true palette remap of the frame is a deferred follow-up.'
findings.evidence: PROGBARM_PROGRESSCLASS_DRAW_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md Core Findings rows on +0x308/+0x30C and §11 ('uses ColorScheme+0x30C convert and background color +0x308'). Rust: src/app_loading.rs:758-771 with self-admitted approximation comments.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed at src/app_loading.rs:31-32 (BAR_FILL_RAMP_SHADE=0, BACKING_RAMP_SHADE=11) and lines 752-771 (player_scheme_backing_rgb/player_scheme_bar_rgb using those ramp indices). Comments at lines 30, 758-761, and 766-768 explicitly admit these are approximations of the exact gamemd palette entries.

gamemd side confirmed via PROGBARM_PROGRESSCLASS_DRAW_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §5 (High-confidence, Ghidra-verified): with +0x71=1 set by Skirmish setup, FUN_00643400 fills the full frame rect with a solid color derived from ColorScheme+0x308 (evidence 0x0064349B..0x00643529), then draws the clipped SHP span remapped through ColorScheme+0x30C (evidence 0x00643486..0x00643490; OQ-05 resolved High). The doc §11 explicitly states "Do not use a generic UI palette by default; the Skirmish direct draw path uses the player/session color scheme convert (ColorScheme+0x30C) and background color (+0x308)."

The two mechanisms are structurally different: gamemd reads specific palette entries at fixed offsets within the ColorScheme struct; Rust reads ramp shade array elements at indices 0 and 11. No algebraic proof exists that ramp[0]/ramp[11] produce the same RGB values as ColorScheme+0x308/+0x30C for any house color, let alone all of them. The bar color and backing color will differ for every player color the player selects — visible on every Skirmish load screen. Cannot downgrade to false-positive.

findings.title: Loading-bar side icon drawn from per-country MPLS insignia PCX with unverified gamemd source
findings.gamemd_behavior: PROGBARM geometry doc proves the progress row reserves W+0x15 before a side icon/label when +0x70=1, vertically centered against row_h, and that the Skirmish label text pointer is 0 (no label). It does NOT establish what art the side-icon slot draws; no decode proves it is a country insignia PCX (usai.pcx etc.).
findings.rust_behavior: Rust unconditionally draws a country-specific insignia PCX (usai.pcx, japi.pcx, frai.pcx, ...) to the right of the bar with gap 0x15, mapped per LoadingArtVariant.
findings.evidence: PROGBARM_PROGRESSCLASS_DRAW_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §6 'Side icon / label placement ... reserves W + 0x15' (geometry only, content not proven). Rust: src/app_loading.rs:826-834; src/render/loading_screen_chrome.rs:82-91. gamemd side-icon content source not established in any consulted doc.

verdict_obj.reasoning: Decompiled `FUN_004e3560` live in Ghidra.
