The function is an exact switch on a country integer index that calls `FUN_006ba140` (the PCX loader) with one of: rani.pcx (-2), usai.pcx (0), japi.pcx (1), frai.pcx (2), geri.pcx (3), gbri.pcx (4), djbi.pcx (5), arbi.pcx (6), lati.pcx (7), rusi.pcx (8), obsi.pcx (-3), yrii.pcx (9). This is precisely the "loading-side-icon resolver" the Rust comment at `src/render/loading_screen_chrome.rs:81` cites. Every PCX filename in the Rust `side_icon_pcx()` match (lines 82-95) is bit-identical to the gamemd output for the corresponding country: Americans→usai.pcx, Alliance→japi.pcx, French→frai.pcx, Germans→geri.pcx, British→gbri.pcx, Africans→djbi.pcx, Arabs→arbi.pcx, Confederation→lati.pcx, Russians→rusi.pcx, Yuri→yrii.pcx, Observer→obsi.pcx. The finding's claim that "no decode proves it is a country insignia PCX" is false — the binary proves it exhaustively. The only gamemd entry not covered by Rust is index -2 → rani.pcx (random-country selection), which is a separate narrow gap outside this finding's scope.
verdict_obj.correction: The research doc was indeed silent on side-icon content, but the Rust code's inline comment correctly cited FUN_004e3560 as the verified source. The finding's "gamemd side-icon content source not established" claim is wrong: FUN_004e3560 is the exact resolver and all 11 country→PCX mappings match Rust verbatim. The only real gap is the unhandled index -2 (rani.pcx, random country), which Rust silently omits.

findings.title: Random country/color selection hard-errors instead of native RNG resolution
findings.gamemd_behavior: After packing node/AI records, SessionClass__ProcessRandomAssignments (0x0069B8C0) resolves random country via Random__RandomRanged(0,9) and random color/start-color via Random__RandomRanged(0,7) with uniqueness checks; the local color helper (FUN_0069B7E0) also randomizes to an unused color when color is -2. Start succeeds with a Random selection.
findings.rust_behavior: launch_country_from_menu returns LaunchValidationError::RandomSelectionUnverified whenever country_random is set, so any slot left on 'Random' blocks Start entirely (launch_session returns Err). No RNG resolution path exists.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_START_GAME_HANDOFF_SESSION_PACKING_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3 'SessionClass__ProcessRandomAssignments ... Random__RandomRanged(0,9)/(0,7)' and FUN_0069B7E0 -2 randomization. Rust: src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/launch.rs:19-21.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed at `src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/launch.rs:19-21`: `launch_country_from_menu` returns `Err(LaunchValidationError::RandomSelectionUnverified { slot })` unconditionally when `random` is true. The same error propagates from `launch_session` at lines 123 and 136, so any slot with country_random or opponent.country_random set causes `launch_session` to return `Err`, blocking Start entirely. The `RandomSelectionUnverified` variant is defined at `src/skirmish_launch.rs:238-240` with no resolution path.

Gamemd side verified via `decompile_function 0x0069B8C0` (`SessionClass__ProcessRandomAssignments`): the function is unconditional (no YR feature gate), iterates human player nodes checking `*(iVar5+0x4f) == -2` (country-needs-random flag) and calls `Random__RandomRanged(0,9)`, then checks `*(iVar5+0x57) == -2` (color-needs-random flag) and calls `Random__RandomRanged(0,7)` in a collision-retry loop. The AI array loop does the same for AI slots. This is active in YR with no guard. The research doc `SKIRMISH_START_GAME_HANDOFF_SESSION_PACKING_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` line 106-108 and `LOBBY_SESSION_HOUSE_CREATION_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` lines 67-76 both document this with matching evidence addresses; both mark "Active in YR: Yes."

No refutation is possible: the gamemd behavior (resolve random to a concrete value, then proceed) is categorically different from the Rust behavior (hard-error on any random slot). Player impact is direct: clicking Start with any slot set to Random is a session-blocking failure in Rust, whereas in gamemd the game starts normally with a randomly chosen country/color.

findings.title: Progress-bar origin omits the upstream LoadProgressMgr+0x1C point
findings.gamemd_behavior: Stored ProgressClass origin (+0x68/+0x6C) is FUN_00552BE0(LoadProgressMgr) = the LoadProgressMgr+0x1C point PLUS (12,256) at default width or (16,321) at non-default width; row/inset math then adds +5 helper and +3 inset. The +0x1C point participates in the absolute position.
findings.rust_behavior: standard_skirmish_row_origin returns (0x0C,0x100)=(12,256) below 800px and (0x10,0x141)=(16,321) at >=800px as ABSOLUTE base, treating the offsets as the whole origin and never adding the LoadProgressMgr+0x1C point.
findings.evidence: PROGBARM_PROGRESSCLASS_DRAW_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §6 'Stored origin = FUN_00552BE0 result. For non-campaign this is LoadProgressMgr+0x1C point plus (12,256)/(16,321)' and §12 'Do not recenter from screen size alone; upstream loading-manager point participates.' Rust: src/app_loading.rs:703-709.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Verified the full call chain in the binary.

FUN_00552B10 (0x00552B10, called from ScenarioClass__Read_Scenario before FUN_00552BE0) writes LoadProgressMgr+0x1C and +0x20 for non-campaign/Skirmish:
  +0x1C = (g_ScreenWidth - refWidth) / 2
  +0x20 = (g_ScreenHeight - refHeight) / 2
where refWidth = DAT_007F5BE0 (640) if screen==640, else DAT_007F5BE4 (800); refHeight similarly.

FUN_00552BE0 disassembly (0x00552c34..0x00552c4b) confirmed: for g_GameMode != 0 (Skirmish), it does LEA EDX,[ECX+0x1C], copies that point via FUN_0072A9C0 (which returns EAX=dest_ptr), then adds 0x0C/0x100 or 0x10/0x141. So the stored origin at ProgressClass+0x68/+0x6C = (LoadProgressMgr[+0x1C] + 12, LoadProgressMgr[+0x20] + 256) or (+16, +321).

Rust src/app_loading.rs:703-709 returns [0x0C, 0x100] or [0x10, 0x141] as absolute values, never adding the upstream centering point.

Correction to the finding's severity/frequency: for 640×480 and 800×600 (the two standard YR resolutions), FUN_00552B10 produces LoadProgressMgr+0x1C/+0x20 = (0,0), because (640-640)/2=0 and (800-800)/2=0. Rust's hardcoded values are pixel-exact for those two resolutions. Drift only fires at non-standard resolutions (e.g., 1024×768 would give +0x1C = (1024-800)/2 = 112, producing a 112px horizontal shift). The finding mis-states trigger_frequency as "every-match" — it only triggers at resolutions other than 640 and 800. The gamemd mechanism description is otherwise factually correct.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding's gamemd and Rust descriptions are accurate, but the trigger frequency is wrong. LoadProgressMgr+0x1C/+0x20 = (0,0) for both standard YR resolutions (640×480 and 800×600), so Rust's hardcoded offsets produce identical output at those resolutions. Drift only occurs at non-standard resolutions (1024+). trigger_frequency should be "non-standard-resolution-only", not "every-match".

findings.title: Row-height/vertical centering uses bar height as a stand-in for the loading font height
findings.gamemd_behavior: row_h = max(side_icon_h, H+6, font_h) + 4, where font_h is the actual loading font height. The vertical centering term (row_h - (H+6))/2 then depends on the true font height.
findings.rust_behavior: Substitutes font_h = bar_height (H), so the font term can never dominate H+6; code comment marks this a 'small vertical follow-up.' If the real loading font is taller than H+6, both row height and the bar's centered Y will be off.
findings.evidence: PROGBARM_PROGRESSCLASS_DRAW_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §6 'row_h = max(side_icon_h, H + 6, font_h) + 4'. Rust: src/app_loading.rs:717-722 with stand-in comment.

verdict_obj.reasoning: Rust at `src/app_loading.rs:718` sets `font_h = bar_height` (H) as a stand-in for the actual loading font height. Binary analysis of `FUN_00642ef0` at `0x00642ef0` (the row-height helper, called from `FUN_00643720`) shows the real gamemd sequence: `iVar4 = FUN_004a60d0()` retrieves `g_GAME_FNT`; `iVar4 = *(iVar4 + 0x1c)` reads its pixel height; then `if (iVar9 <= iVar4) iVar9 = iVar4` applies it as the third term in `max(side_icon_h, H+6, font_h)` before the `+4` padding. `FUN_004a60d0` decompiles to `return g_GAME_FNT` — a named global, not the bar height. The Rust substitution `font_h = H` makes the font term permanently dominated by `H+6` (since `H+6 > H` always), so if GAME.FNT height exceeds `H+6` the Rust `row_h` is shorter than gamemd's and the bar's vertical centering offset `(row_h - (H+6))/2` is correspondingly wrong. No algebraic proof of equivalence is possible without knowing GAME.FNT's actual pixel height (still deferred per OQ-08 in PROGBARM_PROGRESSCLASS_DRAW_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md), so exact equivalence is unproven. The finding's gamemd-side claim is correct. The Rust-side description is also accurate.

findings.title: Start handoff skips per-row AI arrays, color randomization, launch-table, and reset flags
findings.gamemd_behavior: On Start 0x617, FUN_006ACEE0 packs five parallel per-AI arrays (DAT_00A8B27C..A8B2FC), a compact eight-row launch table (DAT_00A8B3F0, item-data->type-code -1/0/1/2 -> 1/4/5/6), allocates a 0x85-byte local node record (+0x28 ten 0xFF bytes, name string, start/team at +0x5B/+0x63), runs ProcessRandomAssignments, mirrors all checkboxes/trackbars, and forces launch-state flags DAT_00A8B260=1 / DAT_00A8B31D/31F/26C=0. Color -2 is randomized to an unused color.
findings.rust_behavior: launch_session builds a tidy SkirmishLaunchSession (local + opponents + options) and passes it straight to begin_loading. It performs map-capacity, no-opponent, and same-explicit-team validation but does not model the launch-table type-code mapping, color-uniqueness randomization, the node-record byte layout, or the forced reset flags. The internal model differs and several observable resolutions (e.g. random color uniqueness) are absent.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_START_GAME_HANDOFF_SESSION_PACKING_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3 'Values packed from dialog controls' and §6 'Missing versus the verified binary slice'. Rust: src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/launch.rs:122-162.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Read `src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/launch.rs:78-162` in full and `src/skirmish_launch.rs` in full. Checked `src/app.rs:605-629`. Read the primary evidence doc at `docs/research/skirmish-ui/SKIRMISH_START_GAME_HANDOFF_SESSION_PACKING_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` in full (Ghidra-verified, high confidence).

Confirmed gaps:

1. **Random-country uniqueness (most player-visible):** `launch_country_from_menu` at launch.rs:19-20 returns `LaunchValidationError::RandomSelectionUnverified` when `random==true`, blocking launch entirely. gamemd calls `SessionClass__ProcessRandomAssignments @ 0x0069B8C0` (verified: doc §3, call at `0x006AD6F9`) which resolves random country via `Random__RandomRanged(0,9)` and random color via `Random__RandomRanged(0,7)` with uniqueness checks — the game launches successfully. Rust prevents launch; gamemd proceeds. This is a concrete divergent output path every time a player clicks Random Country and hits Start.

2. **Color -2 randomization:** The doc (§3, evidence `0x006AD600..0x006AD63B`, `FUN_0069B7E0`) confirms that color -2 is resolved to an unused color by `FUN_0069B7E0`. Rust `launch_color_index` (launch.rs:51-57) rejects any `color_index >= HOUSE_COLOR_COUNT` with `InvalidColorIndex` — there is no -2/random color path at all.

3. **Per-AI arrays `DAT_00A8B27C..A8B2FC` and compact launch table `DAT_00A8B3F0`:** These are gamemd internal globals consumed downstream by spawn/house creation. The doc confirms they are written during the handoff (§3, `0x006AD3C1..0x006AD5E8`). Rust builds `SkirmishLaunchSession` instead and passes it to `begin_loading` — different internal structure. Observable only if the consuming spawn code reads those globals; since Rust has its own spawn path consuming `SkirmishLaunchSession`, this is an internal-mechanism difference whose output parity depends on spawn correctness. Not independently driftful at this layer.

4. **Forced reset flags `DAT_00A8B260=1 / DAT_00A8B31D/31F/26C=0`:** gamemd-internal globals with no direct Rust equivalent; observable only if a prior session's stale flag value bleeds into the next game. Since Rust doesn't use these globals at all, the effect is moot for a Rust-only run. Not driftful at this layer.

5. **Node-record 0x85-byte layout:** gamemd-internal session structure passed to the spawn layer. Rust uses `SkirmishLaunchSession` instead. Internal-mechanism difference; parity depends on downstream spawn correctness, not this packing step.

The two genuinely player-visible drifts are (1) random-country launch is blocked in Rust but succeeds in gamemd, and (2) random-color (-2) is blocked in Rust but resolved in gamemd. Both fire every time a player selects Random for country or color and hits Start — common trigger in normal skirmish setup. The finding's gamemd claims are accurate per the verified doc. The Rust-side description is accurate per current code. The doc §6 file paths are stale (the code moved to `launch.rs`) but the behavioral gap is real.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding's §6 file references are slightly stale (the doc cites `src/ui/skirmish_shell/state.rs:70` and `src/app.rs:547`; the current Rust code lives in `src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/launch.rs:78-162` and `src/app.rs:605-629`). The behavioral description is otherwise accurate. The most player-visible drift is that Rust blocks launch on random-country/random-color selection (returning `RandomSelectionUnverified`/`InvalidColorIndex` errors) whereas gamemd resolves these via `ProcessRandomAssignments` at `0x0069B8C0` and launches successfully. The launch-table type-code mapping and node-record layout are internal-mechanism differences whose observability depends on downstream spawn correctness, not this packing step alone.

findings.title: Launch resolves the scenario file from the UI map index rather than the persisted selected-record filename buffer
findings.gamemd_behavior: The post-shell Skirmish file argument is ScenarioClass+0x125C, populated earlier by the selected-record loader 0x005E7BF0 from the accepted record's +0x58 file path; Start only mirrors the token/index (DAT_00A8B3C4/3C8) and clamps an out-of-range index to 0. The launcher opens the record's file path, not a UI-derived display lookup.
findings.rust_behavior: launch_session indexes maps[state.selected_map_idx] and stores selected_map.file_name as selected_map_file; start_skirmish_session then uses that filename (or falls back to 'auto'). The file comes from the live UI index, with no persisted accepted-record buffer and no out-of-range clamp-to-0 semantics.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_START_TO_LOADING_SCREEN_ACTIVATION_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §5 row 6 ('ScenarioClass+0x125C ... populated by 0x005E7BF0, not by Start deriving a display string') and Implementation Handoff. Rust: src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/launch.rs:83-85; src/app.rs:609-612.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed by reading launch.rs:83-85 and 156, app.rs:609-612. At launch time, Rust indexes `maps[state.selected_map_idx]` live (returning `LaunchValidationError::NoSelectedMap` if out of range), then stores `selected_map.file_name` in `SkirmishLaunchSession.selected_map_file`. There is no accepted-record buffer, no `ScenarioClass+0x125C`-equivalent persisted path, and no clamp-to-0 on out-of-range index (Rust aborts; gamemd loads map 0).

gamemd side confirmed by SKIRMISH_SELECTED_MAP_TOKEN_LOAD_CONSUMER_GHIDRA_REPORT.md (all OQs resolved, Ghidra evidence cited inline): `0x005E7BF0` copies `DAT_00A8B8CC[DAT_00A8B254]+0x58` into both `DAT_00A8B8E0` and `ScenarioClass+0x125C` at Choose-Map-accept time; Start Game only mirrors the live index into `DAT_00A8B3C8` with a clamp-to-0 (evidence `0x006AD364..0x006AD36B`); `Main_Game` then passes `ScenarioClass+0x125C` directly to `ScenarioClass__Start_Scenario` (evidence `0x0052E737..0x0052E745`). The post-shell filename is the buffer populated by the record loader, not a display-index lookup at launch time.

Two concrete divergences: (1) Rust re-resolves the filename from the live UI index at launch instead of consuming the pre-populated accepted-record path — if the map list changes between selection and launch, different files load; (2) an out-of-range index aborts in Rust vs. silently loads map 0 in gamemd. Neither divergence is provably equivalent across the full input space. No algebraic proof or bit-identical empirical check exists. Finding stands as confirmed drift.

findings.title: Generic-map-load loading path renders egui 'Mission deployment'/'Loading...'/map-name text not present in native Skirmish loading
findings.gamemd_behavior: Standard offline Skirmish loading draws only native LS country art + assigned markers + PROGBARM progress; it reads no LSLoadMessage/LSLoadBriefing/Briefing/UIName (those are g_GameMode==0 campaign-only) and shows no map name or explanatory status string.
findings.rust_behavior: The main-menu StartSelected path uses LoadingRequest::generic_map_load, whose loading render falls through to main_menu::draw_loading_screen drawing an egui dark panel with 'Mission deployment', a 'Loading...'/status string, and 'Map: {map_name}'. This non-native overlay is shown whenever the generic path is taken.
findings.evidence: LSLOADMESSAGE_SKIRMISH_LOADING_TEXT_SPLIT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md Negative Facts ('Do not display ... Map:{map_name} / explanatory egui loading text') and SKIRMISH_START_TO_LOADING_SCREEN_ACTIVATION_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §7. Rust: src/ui/main_menu.rs:341-345; src/app.rs:2358-2364.

verdict_obj.reasoning: Rust code verified at `src/ui/main_menu.rs:341-359`: `draw_loading_screen` paints an egui dark panel with the literal strings "Mission deployment" (line 344), "Loading..." (line 352), and `format!("Map: {}", map_name)` (line 358). This function is reached via `src/app.rs:2358-2364` whenever `render_loading_screen` returns `LoadingRenderResult::GenericFallback`, which happens at `src/app_loading.rs:592-593` whenever `session.native` is `None`. The `generic_map_load` constructor (`src/app_loading.rs:170-180`) always sets `presentation = GenericMapLoad` and `launch = Generic`, producing `native = None` (`src/app_loading.rs:251`). This path is triggered by `MenuAction::StartSelected` in `src/app.rs:738`, which is emitted by the "Start Game" button in the legacy main menu (`src/ui/main_menu.rs:246`). The gamemd claim is verified by `LSLOADMESSAGE_SKIRMISH_LOADING_TEXT_SPLIT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md`: `ScenarioClass__Full_Init @ 0x00686B20` reads `LSLoadMessage`/`LSLoadBriefing`/`Briefing`/`UIName` only under `g_GameMode == 0` (campaign); standard offline Skirmish is `g_GameMode == 5` and takes the non-text branch. `LOADING_SCREEN_WM_PAINT_MODE2_COMPOSITION_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` (cited in the Negative Facts section) confirms mode-2 `WM_PAINT_Handler @ 0x00621E90` draws only the PUDLGBG SHP frame with no text overlay. No equivalent text surface exists in the native Skirmish loading path. The player sees "Mission deployment" / "Loading..." / map name text where the original shows only native loading-screen art (country background + PROGBARM progress bar). This is a clear visible drift on every skirmish start via the legacy main menu path.

findings.title: Bar fill width ftol uses naive truncation for non-exact fractional values
findings.gamemd_behavior: Native fill width is ftol(frame0_width * lane/max) under the x87 control-word rounding mode in effect at the call site (round-to-nearest by default), giving a specific integer at fractional inputs.
findings.rust_behavior: gamemd_ftol_positive_domain rounds to nearest only when within FTOL_EPSILON of an integer; otherwise it falls back to a plain `value as i32` truncation. Code comment: 'Exact fractional x87 control-word behavior remains a narrow follow-up.' For mid-fill milestones this can produce a 1-pixel-narrower bar than native rounding.
findings.evidence: PROGBARM_PROGRESSCLASS_DRAW_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md Core Findings 'Visible fill width is ftol(frame0_width * (lane_value/max_value))'. Rust: src/app_loading.rs:987-996 with truncation fallback.

verdict_obj.reasoning: Disassembled Math__ftol at 0x007C5F00 via mcp__ghidra-mcp__disassemble_function. The function saves the current x87 control word (FNSTCW), compares it against a stored value at 0x00822D80, and if different loads that stored word before executing FISTP. read_memory on 0x00822D80 returned bytes [7F, 0E, 00, 00] = control word 0x0E7F. Bits 10-11 of 0x0E7F are 0b11 (RC=3), which is round-toward-zero (truncation), NOT round-to-nearest. This is the MSVC __ftol2 pattern: the runtime pre-sets the x87 CW to truncation mode before FISTP rather than relying on the ambient CW. The finding's core gamemd claim — "round-to-nearest by default" — is factually wrong; gamemd always truncates. Consequently the finding's drift description is also inverted: Rust's fallback path (value as i32, lines 994-995 of src/app_loading.rs) also truncates toward zero, so it matches gamemd correctly for mid-fill fractional inputs. The actual (undetected) discrepancy is the opposite: the epsilon branch (lines 989-992) rounds to nearest for values within FTOL_EPSILON (1e-6) of an integer, while gamemd truncates those too. That is a real but extremely narrow drift (triggers only when frame0_width * fraction lands within 1e-6 of an integer), which the finding did not identify and is far narrower than the broad mid-fill drift claimed.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding misstated the gamemd side. Math__ftol (0x007C5F00) uses x87 control word 0x0E7F (RC=truncate-toward-zero, confirmed via read_memory at 0x00822D80 = bytes 7F 0E), so gamemd truncates for all inputs. The Rust fallback (value as i32) is correct. The epsilon/near-integer branch (lines 989-992, rounds to nearest) is the actual — very narrow — drift: Rust returns ceiling for values like 2.9999999 while gamemd returns floor. The broad mid-fill 1-pixel-narrower claim in the finding is false.

findings.title: Progress update omits the old/new average-percent manager notification (message 0x11AE)
findings.gamemd_behavior: On a changed lane value, FUN_00643C50 computes old and new average percents and sends message 0x11AE with the old/new integer pair to the attached progress manager (when ProgressClass+4 and +0x60 are set) before the synchronous repaint.
findings.rust_behavior: advance_progress/set_percent only store the new value and return a changed/unchanged bool that gates a repaint; there is no average-percent manager notification step. This is currently an internal-only difference but is part of the verified update contract; no consumer of 0x11AE is modeled.
findings.evidence: PROGBARM_PROGRESSCLASS_DRAW_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §7 step 5 ('calls the attached progress manager vtable with message 0x11AE and the old/new integer average percent pair'). Rust: src/app_loading.rs:101-111.

verdict_obj.reasoning: Rust side confirmed (src/app_loading.rs:101-111): `set_percent` stores the new value and returns a bool; no manager notification step. Binary confirmed via `decompile_function 0x00643C50`: the vtable call `(**(code **)**(undefined4 **)(param_1 + 4))(0x11ae, &local_10)` does fire when `ProgressClass+4` (manager pointer) and `ProgressClass+0x60` (enable byte) are both non-zero — both are true in Skirmish (`FUN_00643E80` sets `+4`, `FUN_00642A60` sets `+0x60 = 1`). So the 0x11AE message does reach the LoadProgressMgr vtable slot 0, which is `FUN_00554400` (verified via `read_memory 0x7ecf64` → first entry `0x00554400`, and `get_xrefs_to 0x007ecf64` confirming the vtable is only installed by `LoadProgressMgr__Constructor`). However, `decompile_function 0x00554400` shows the handler immediately checks `*(int *)(param_1 + 0x60)` — the `+0x60` field on the LoadProgressMgr object itself. `disassemble_function 0x00552A40` shows the constructor zeroes all fields through `+0x60` of the LoadProgressMgr. `get_xrefs_to 0x00abc9bc` (the singleton) reveals only the constructor and destructor (`FUN_00552aa0`) touch the singleton. No function in the Skirmish call chain (`FUN_00552CC0`, `FUN_00642B10`, `FUN_00642C80`, `FUN_00643E80`, `FUN_00552BE0`, `FUN_00552C90`, `FUN_00642DF0`) writes to `LoadProgressMgr+0x60`. The destructor `FUN_00554370` (verified by decompile) also zeroes it. Therefore `LoadProgressMgr+0x60` is always zero in Skirmish, the handler body is never entered, and the 0x11AE notification produces zero player-visible output. This constitutes exhaustive caller verification: the call fires but the handler is a no-op in every Skirmish load. The finding's claim that "no consumer of 0x11AE is modeled" overstates the risk — the consumer exists but is gated behind a field (`LoadProgressMgr+0x60`) that is never set in Skirmish. The doc (`PROGBARM_PROGRESSCLASS_DRAW_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` §7 step 5) correctly describes the firing condition but does not trace the handler's own guard, which is the load-bearing detail.
verdict_obj.correction: The doc (§7 step 5) correctly states the call fires when `ProgressClass+4` and `ProgressClass+0x60` are set, but omits that `FUN_00554400` (the 0x11AE handler) has its own guard: it checks `LoadProgressMgr+0x60`, which is always zero in Skirmish (zeroed in constructor, no writer in the Skirmish path). The handler returns 1 immediately doing nothing. The gamemd behavior is: message 0x11AE fires but has no player-visible effect in offline Skirmish. The finding's characterization as "verified update contract" with player impact is overstated.

findings.title: Single Player -> Skirmish route correctly skips the FUN_006071E0 slide helper (no disparity)
findings.gamemd_behavior: Dialog 0x100 Skirmish control 0x579 writes route result 0x0B directly in proc 0x0052D640 with no call to FUN_00608260/FUN_006071E0; stock ShellButtonSlideSound is empty so there is no audible slide cue. The slide-in helper is a separate generic shell-transition path not proven on this command route.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust routes Single Player -> Skirmish through the shell action without invoking the (quarantined, DRIFT-labeled) shell transition helper, matching the native direct-result behavior. Verified as a non-finding; flagged only to record it was checked.
findings.evidence: SINGLE_PLAYER_0X100_SKIRMISH_0X579_ROUTE_GHIDRA_REPORT.md (0x579 writes 0x0B directly) and SINGLE_PLAYER_TO_SKIRMISH_FUN_006071E0_FLAGS_ASSETS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md Negative Facts. Rust: src/app.rs:756-782.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side (verified): `enter_native_skirmish_from_single_player` at `src/app.rs:556-564` sets `main_menu_to_skirmish_transition = None` and `shell_transition_pass = None`, then immediately shows the native skirmish shell — no slide transition helper is invoked on the Single Player → Skirmish route.

gamemd side (verified from docs): Raw PE disassembly of `0x0052D640..0x0052D785` confirms the dialog proc contains no CALL to `FUN_00608260` or `FUN_006071E0`; control `0x579` writes `0x0B` directly at `0x0052D6F1..0x0052D720`. This is solid — cited in both `SINGLE_PLAYER_0X100_SKIRMISH_0X579_ROUTE_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` and `SINGLE_PLAYER_TO_SKIRMISH_FUN_006071E0_FLAGS_ASSETS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md`.

The flaw in the finding: the finding conflates "the result-write proc doesn't call the helper" with "no slide transition fires on this route." The `SHELL_TRANSITION_CALLER_00612690_OWNER_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` (2026-05-27) establishes that `0x00612690` lives inside the generic shell child subclass dispatcher (`0x00610CA0..0x006128FE`) installed on ALL shell child windows by `FUN_0060F9A0`. For dialog `0x100` buttons (style `0x5000000B`), this dispatcher IS installed. The `0x00612690` branch fires during WM_PAINT when `record+0x1FC == 1`, not during the WM_COMMAND result write — meaning the slide-in could occur as a paint-phase event on the button itself before or after the dialog proc destroys `0x100`. The research explicitly classifies Single Player click reachability as "Unchecked/conditional" and defers OQ-09 ("Could an external owner-draw/state-machine caller wrap the 0x100 command with FUN_00608260 before the result write?") as requiring a live debugger trace.

Under CLAUDE.md burden-of-proof rules: exact equivalence of the visual output on this route (no slide transition vs possible slide transition) is not proven. The absence of the helper call inside `0x0052D640` is necessary but not sufficient — an external paint-phase wrapper via the subclass dispatcher remains an open path. `ShellButtonSlideSound` being empty in stock INI means there is no audible end-cue, but `FUN_006071E0` also moves SDBTNANM/SDMPBTN/SDWRNTMP visuals on screen — those are player-visible if the branch fires. The finding's confidence label "proven" overstates what the static evidence actually closes. Verdict is needs-research (OQ-09 requires live-debugger trace of FUN_00608260 while clicking 0x579 in retail), not false-positive and not confirmed-drift.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding's gamemd claim is accurate for the result-write proc: `0x0052D640` does not call `FUN_00608260`. However, the finding incorrectly concludes this proves no slide transition fires on the route. The `0x00612690 → FUN_00608260` path lives in the generic shell child subclass dispatcher installed on ALL shell child windows including dialog `0x100` buttons, and fires during WM_PAINT with `record+0x1FC == 1` — a paint-phase trigger orthogonal to the WM_COMMAND result write. Whether a retail `0x579` click activates that paint-phase state is explicitly unresolved (OQ-09 in the research docs, status: needs-runtime-debugger). The finding should be labeled needs-research, not proven/no-disparity.

findings.title: Validation modal centering omits the +1 term used by CenterChildWindow
findings.gamemd_behavior: CenterChildWindow @ 0x00777080 computes X = max(0, ((g_ScreenWidth - child_w) + 1) / 2), Y = max(0, ((g_ScreenHeight - child_h) + 1) / 2), centering directly in the live screen size. The +1 changes odd-size centering by one pixel.
findings.rust_behavior: centered_shell_dialog centers inside an 800x600 base box ((SHELL_BASE_W - w)/2) then adds center_offset(screen, base); it uses plain (base - w)/2 with NO +1 term. At 800x600 this yields dialog x=174 vs binary 175 (and y likewise); the missing +1 is a guaranteed 1px shift whenever (screen-child) is odd.
findings.evidence: decompile_function 0x00777080 (X/Y = ((parent-child)+1)/2 clamped >=0); SKIRMISH_VALIDATION_MODAL_NATIVE_PIXEL_RECTS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §4 and §8 explicitly note Rust 'uses (base - w) / 2 without the native +1 term'

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed: `centered_shell_dialog` at layout.rs:427-434 computes `(SHELL_BASE_W - w) / 2` (plain integer division, no +1). `compute_validation_modal_layout` at lines 838-849 calls this directly. The `center_offset` helper at line 423-424 also uses plain `/2` for the screen-to-base offset, so there is no +1 anywhere in the chain.

Binary side confirmed via `decompile_function 0x00777080`: `X = ((local_20._8_4_ - local_10._8_4_) + 1) / 2` with `if (X < 0) X = 0`. The +1 is literal in the decompiled output. The caller chain is live: `FUN_004c83e0` (a WM_INITDIALOG handler that calls `CenterChildWindow` when `g_hWnd != 0`) and `FUN_00531cc0` (skirmish shell flow that calls `CenterChildWindow` immediately after creating the dialog).

Numerical check at 800x600 with VALIDATION_MODAL_W=451: Rust X = 0 + (800-451)/2 = 349/2 = 174. gamemd X = ((800-451)+1)/2 = 350/2 = 175. One pixel off. The finding's example (174 vs 175) is exactly correct.

No refutation path is available: the output differs by 1px whenever (screen_dim - child_dim) is odd, which is the case at 800x600 with a 451px-wide modal. This is a guaranteed 1px drift on the canonical resolution.

findings.title: Validation modal double-centers (800-box + screen offset) instead of centering in live screen size
findings.gamemd_behavior: CenterChildWindow centers the dialog in g_ScreenWidth x g_ScreenHeight directly (parent is g_hWnd). At 1024x768 the modal x = (1024-451+1)/2 = 287.
findings.rust_behavior: centered_shell_dialog = center_offset(screen,800) + (800-w)/2. At 1024x768: 112 + (800-451)/2 = 112+174 = 286. Off by 1px from binary's 287, and the algorithm differs structurally (centering in an 800 box nested in a centered 800 box) rather than centering once in the full screen, so any non-(screen-800)-even resolution diverges further than 1px is guaranteed.
findings.evidence: decompile_function 0x00777080 (uses g_ScreenWidth/g_ScreenHeight, not an 800 base); SKIRMISH_VALIDATION_MODAL_NATIVE_PIXEL_RECTS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §4 OQ-06

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust code at layout.rs:427-434 confirmed: `centered_shell_dialog` computes x as `center_offset(screen_w, 800) + (800 - w) / 2`, which is `(screen_w-800)/2 + (800-w)/2`. This double-centering is structurally different from the native formula.

The research doc SKIRMISH_VALIDATION_MODAL_NATIVE_PIXEL_RECTS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §4 (OQ-06 resolved) verifies directly from decompile of `CenterChildWindow @ 0x00777080`: the formula is `max(0, ((g_ScreenWidth - child_w) + 1) / 2)` — a single centering in the full screen dimensions, not in an 800-wide base. The `+1` before integer division is present in gamemd but absent in Rust's `(SHELL_BASE_W - w) / 2`.

At 1024x768, assuming child_w=451 (candidate): gamemd x = (1024-451+1)/2 = 287; Rust x = (1024-800)/2 + (800-451)/2 = 112+174 = 286. The 1px discrepancy matches the missing `+1`. At non-(screen_w-800)-even resolutions the double-nesting produces further divergence.

The gamemd side is verified by decompile of 0x00777080 plus disassembly 0x00777060..0x0077714F (doc §3 step 5, §4). The Rust side is confirmed at layout.rs:423-424 (`center_offset` = `(screen-base)/2`) and lines 429-430. The finding correctly identifies both the structural algorithm difference and the missing `+1`. The one open uncertainty (exact runtime child_w from `CreateDialogIndirectParamA`) does not affect the confirmed structural drift — any child_w makes the double-centering formula produce a different x than the native single-centering formula when screen_w != 800.

findings.title: Validation modal size 451x326 is not binary-proven (candidate is 450x325)
findings.gamemd_behavior: RT_DIALOG 0xCE template is 300x200 DLU; the final client size is produced at runtime by CreateDialogIndirectParamA from MS Sans Serif 8pt metrics. The only binary-anchored candidate (MulDiv 6/13) is 450x325, and the doc marks even that Medium/unverified.
findings.rust_behavior: Hardcodes 451x326. No 451 or 326 (nor 450/325) appears in the validation-modal template doc as a proven value; the size is invented. Wrong dialog size shifts every centered child rect and the modal background edges.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_VALIDATION_MODAL_NATIVE_PIXEL_RECTS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §5 (candidate 450x325, Medium) and §8 ('Rust uses fixed 451x326'); grep of VALIDATION_MODAL_0X005D3490_DIALOG_TEMPLATE_CONTROL_RECTS doc finds no 451/326/450/325

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed at layout.rs:35-36: VALIDATION_MODAL_W=451, VALIDATION_MODAL_H=326, used directly in compute_validation_modal_layout (line 841) and verified by the test at line 1262 which asserts dialog=(174,137,451,326) for 800x600.

The DLU conversion formula in Rust uses BASE_X=6, BASE_Y=13 with round-to-nearest (mul_div_round). Applying that to the binary-proven template dimensions (300x200 DLUs from resource bytes at file offset 0x4F9A3C, verified via SKIRMISH_VALIDATION_MODAL_NATIVE_PIXEL_RECTS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §2): mul_div_round(300,6,4)=(1800+2)/4=450, mul_div_round(200,13,8)=(2600+4)/8=325. That yields 450x325 — which matches the doc's Medium/unverified candidate (§5), not 451x326. The +1 in each dimension is not derivable from any binary-proven formula. The 451x326 constants are invented.

The gamemd side is correctly stated: CreateDialogIndirectParamA performs font/DLU conversion at runtime with 8pt MS Sans Serif; the exact GetClientRect result after dialog creation is unproven (deferred OQ-10 in the doc). The doc explicitly says 450x325 is a candidate only (§5 table, Confidence=Medium) and §10/§11 mark the final pixel rect as needs-runtime-debugger.

A second related drift confirmed by the doc but not called out in the finding: centered_shell_dialog (layout.rs:429) uses (base-w)/2 without the +1 term, while the binary-proven CenterChildWindow formula at 0x00777080 uses ((parent_dim-child_dim)+1)/2 — this shifts centering by up to 1 pixel on odd-difference dimensions.

The finding's gamemd claim is accurate. No refutation is available: 451x326 is not algebraically equivalent to 450x325 or to any runtime-derivable value, the difference is 1 pixel in each dimension, and no runtime capture has been made to determine the true native size. Default verdict is DRIFT per CLAUDE.md burden-of-proof rule.

findings.title: Choose-map listbox scrollbar reuses combo-dropdown scrollbar constants; native LISTBOX overflow scrollbar geometry not separately verified
findings.gamemd_behavior: OwnerDraw_ListBox_00618D40 creates a child 'Scrollbar' (OwnerDraw_ScrollBar_0061C690) on overflow, width DAT_00AC1DF0*2+0x12 = 20px, arrow buttons 22px, min thumb 14px, track-click jumps to click-centered absolute position. These match the combo scrollbar values.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust borrows the combo-dropdown scrollbar constants (22/14/20) for the choose-map listbox. The values coincide with the verified ones, but the listbox path and combo path share the same constant set in layout.rs without an independent listbox-side proof that the listbox overflow scrollbar uses the identical button/thumb metrics; flagged because the choose-map listbox renderer/state is otherwise unimplemented per the row-paint doc.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_OWNERDRAW_LISTBOX_00618D40_ROW_PAINT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md OQ-07 (20px) and §Scrollbar; SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md (22px arrows, 14px min thumb)

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Checked Rust at layout.rs:20-34 and the two cited docs.

Key fact the finding missed: both the combo scrollbar and the listbox scrollbar delegate to the same binary callback, `OwnerDraw_ScrollBar_0061C690 @ 0x0061C690`. `SKIRMISH_OWNERDRAW_LISTBOX_00618D40_ROW_PAINT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` §5 Integration Points (line 137) explicitly states the listbox creates a `"Scrollbar"` child handled by `OwnerDraw_ScrollBar_0061C690`. `SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` line 39 states: "Scrollbar arrow buttons are 22 px high, minimum thumb height is 14 px ... decompile `0x0061C690`". Because there is only one `OwnerDraw_ScrollBar` callback and both paths use it, the 22/14 values are verified for the listbox path by binary identity, not by coincidence or assumption.

Rust side (layout.rs:20-22): `COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_BUTTON_H = 22`, `COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_MIN_THUMB_H = 14` — these names are reused in the listbox scroll functions at lines 762, 764, 769, 790-793. The 20 px scrollbar width is now also a dedicated `CHOOSE_MAP_LISTBOX_SCROLLBAR_W = 20` constant (layout.rs:34), aliased from the same verified value. The shared constant names reflect a genuine gamemd identity (one callback), not an unverified assumption.

The finding's claim that there is "no independent listbox-side proof" is refuted by the listbox doc's explicit citation of `0x0061C690` as the child scrollbar handler — the same function whose decompile produced the 22/14 values. Exact equivalence is proven by binary identity of the handler, satisfying the algebraic/exhaustive-caller test in CLAUDE.md.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding incorrectly states there is no independent listbox-side proof. The listbox doc (`SKIRMISH_OWNERDRAW_LISTBOX_00618D40_ROW_PAINT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` §5) confirms the listbox's child `"Scrollbar"` is handled by `OwnerDraw_ScrollBar_0061C690 @ 0x0061C690` — the same callback whose decompile (cited in `SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` line 39) produced the 22 px arrow / 14 px min-thumb values. The Rust constants are verified correct for both paths via binary identity of the handler.

findings.title: Combo dropdown scrollbar track-click pages by visible rows instead of absolute jump (state layer, constants in layout.rs)
findings.rust_location: src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/combos.rs (combo scrollbar track-click handler; reachable via mod.rs re-export scroll_open_combo_by_rows / top_index_from_scrollbar_track_click)
findings.gamemd_behavior: OwnerDraw_ScrollBar_0061C690 track clicks outside the thumb JUMP the current value by centering the thumb on the click and converting to a top/range value; they are NOT page-up/page-down by visible-row count.
findings.rust_behavior: Per the combo-scrollbar doc §4, Rust track-click behavior 'scrolls by visible row count' (page step) for the open combo dropdown, diverging from native absolute proportional jump. (The choose-map listbox track-click in layout.rs:778 IS absolute and matches; the combo path does not.)
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §4 ('Rust track-click behavior scrolls by visible row count; retail owner-draw scrollbar jumps to the absolute thumb position') and §7 handoff row

verdict_obj.reasoning: Read combos.rs lines 237-257: `top_index_from_scrollbar_track_click` centers the thumb on the click via `mouse_y - thumb.h / 2`, clamps to button zones, computes `local = thumb_top - scrollbar.y - COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_BUTTON_H`, then returns `(local * max_top + track_span / 2) / track_span` — an absolute proportional jump, not a page step. The handler at combos.rs lines 650-658 routes track clicks through this function and calls `set_open_combo_top_index` with the result. No page-step (`visible_rows`) arithmetic appears anywhere in the track-click path. The research doc `SKIRMISH_0X102_COMBO_DROPDOWN_SCROLLBAR_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §4` listed "Rust track-click behavior scrolls by visible row count" as a delta and §7 lists it as a handoff item — but the current combos.rs already implements the fix. The doc was written before the fix landed; the finding is sourced from the stale §4/§7 text. gamemd `OwnerDraw_ScrollBar_0061C690` (verified per doc §2, OQ-CG-005) does the same center-thumb-on-click absolute jump. Both sides now match.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding's "rust_behavior" claim is stale. combos.rs `top_index_from_scrollbar_track_click` (lines 237-257) already implements the absolute proportional jump matching gamemd, not a page-by-visible-row-count step. The doc's §4 delta was written before this function was implemented; the finding was generated from the stale doc text rather than current code.

findings.title: Main-menu INTRO music plays at hardcoded 0.5, gamemd ScoreVolume default is 0.4
findings.gamemd_behavior: gamemd plays the [INTRO] (Drok) theme at the player's ScoreVolume (music) option. OptionsClass__SetDefaults (0x005FA350) initializes the music volume float param_1[0x10] = 0x3ecccccd = 0.4 (the three sliders SoundVolume/VoiceVolume/ScoreVolume init to 0.7/0.7/0.4 respectively; EVA_SYSTEM_DEEP_DIVE confirms VoiceVolume lives at Options+0x3C = param_1[0xf], so ScoreVolume = param_1[0x10] = 0.4). On a fresh install with no saved RA2MD.INI [Options] ScoreVolume, the menu music is heard at 0.4.
findings.rust_behavior: MusicPlayer::new() hardcodes volume = 0.5 and never reads the saved ScoreVolume option, so the menu INTRO theme is played 25% louder than gamemd's default (0.5 vs 0.4). The value is also never sourced from any options file, so even after the user has a saved RA2MD.INI it is ignored at startup.
findings.evidence: Ghidra decompile_function 0x005FA350 shows param_1[0x10]=0x3ecccccd (0.4f). EVA_SYSTEM_DEEP_DIVE_GHIDRA_REPORT.md identifies the music slider as ScoreVolume distinct from VoiceVolume(+0x3C). Rust src/audio/music.rs:75.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed: `MusicPlayer::new()` at `src/audio/music.rs:75` initializes `volume: 0.5`. The `set_volume` call at line 158 applies `self.volume` to the rodio player, so it inherits the 0.5 default. No options-loading system exists anywhere in `src/` — grep for `ScoreVolume`, `score_volume`, `RA2MD`, and `load_options` all return zero hits. The `map_or(0.5, ...)` fallbacks in `app.rs:2533` and `app.rs:2669` also propagate the wrong default to the dev overlay.

gamemd side verified via `decompile_function 0x005FA350` (OptionsClass__SetDefaults): `param_1[0xe]=0x3f333333` (0.7f, SoundVolume), `param_1[0xf]=0x3f333333` (0.7f, VoiceVolume), `param_1[0x10]=0x3ecccccd` (0.4f, ScoreVolume). The finding's claim of 0.4 is correct.

Minor correction: the finding attributes drift partly to `music.rs:158` ("player.set_volume on start"), but line 158 merely applies `self.volume` to the rodio player — it is not itself the source. The sole source is line 75 (`volume: 0.5`) combined with the absence of any options-loading path. The magnitude of the drift is 0.5 vs 0.4 = 25% louder, audible on every cold start and every session where the user has not touched the dev overlay slider.
verdict_obj.correction: Line 158 (`player.set_volume(self.volume as f32)`) is not itself the drift source — it correctly applies the stored `self.volume` field. The drift originates entirely at line 75 (`volume: 0.5`) and the missing options-loading code. The gamemd side is correctly stated.

findings.title: Music volume never loaded from saved RA2MD.INI [Options] ScoreVolume on launch
findings.gamemd_behavior: gamemd reads ScoreVolume from RA2MD.INI [Options] at startup (OptionsClass__ReadFromINI) and applies it to ThemeClass playback, so the menu music honors whatever level the player last set. A user who set music to e.g. 0.0 (off) hears silence on the menu; a user at 1.0 hears full volume.
findings.rust_behavior: MusicPlayer is constructed with a fixed 0.5 and there is no path that reads ScoreVolume from any options/INI file at launch. The player's previously saved music level is ignored every launch; the in-game dev-overlay slider (app.rs:2708) is the only way to change it and it does not persist.
findings.evidence: GLOBAL_TIMING_MODEL / EVA_SYSTEM_DEEP_DIVE establish the OptionsClass INI read pattern (OptionsClass__ReadFromINI); EVA_SYSTEM_DEEP_DIVE_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §10 documents per-category volume options. Rust grep shows no ScoreVolume/options read feeding music_player.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed: `MusicPlayer::new()` at `src/audio/music.rs:75` hardcodes `volume: 0.5`. `src/app.rs:2175` constructs `MusicPlayer::new()` with no options argument. Grep over all of `src/` finds zero references to `ScoreVolume`, `RA2MD.INI` options loading, or any persistent volume read at startup. The only volume-setting paths are the dev-overlay slider (`app.rs:2708`) and pause menu (`app.rs:2574`), neither of which persists or reads from file.

gamemd side confirmed via `OPTIONS_DIALOG_CASE5_AND_FIELD_MAP_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` (verified from `decompile_function 0x005fa620` — `OptionsClass__ReadFromINI`): `[Audio] ScoreVolume` at struct offset `0x40`, type float, clamped 0.0–1.0, marked "Active in YR: Yes". This is read from `RA2MD.INI` at startup and applied to ThemeClass/music playback. `IsScoreRepeat` (0x44) and `InGameMusic` (0x45) are also read from the same INI block.

The drift is concrete and player-visible every launch: a player who previously set music volume to 0 (or any non-0.5 value) hears the wrong volume on the menu and in-game from the first tick. No refutation is possible — the Rust side has no options INI loading infrastructure at all, not just a wrong value.

findings.title: Track filename: gamemd appends .WAV; Rust tries .wav then .aud
findings.gamemd_behavior: ThemeClass builds the stream filename by concatenating the Sound= stem with the literal '.WAV' (string at 0x00844768) and opens that single name through the StreamPlayer. The theme stream is a .wav file; gamemd does not fall back to a .aud for theme tracks.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust load_track tries '{stem}.wav' first and falls back to '{stem}.aud'. For the retail Drok track resolved as .wav this produces the same payload, so output matches in the common case; the .aud fallback would only differ if a stem existed only as .aud, which does not occur for shell theme tracks. Documented as drift because the resolution order/extension set differs from gamemd's single .WAV append.
findings.evidence: MAIN_MENU_MUSIC_TRACK_AND_LOOP_GHIDRA_REPORT.md anchor table 0x00844768 = '.WAV' appended; doc flags exact opened extension as MEDIUM/out-of-scope. Rust src/audio/music.rs:307.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Checked three layers:

1. gamemd binary (verified via decompile_function 0x005b4430): MIX lookup calls FUN_007dcfc4 (0x007dcfc4) which uppercases every character (subtracts 0x20 from a-z range) before passing the filename to CRCEngine__AddData. So gamemd hashes "DROK.WAV" (uppercase).

2. Rust mix_hash (src/assets/mix_hash.rs:62): `name.bytes().map(|b| b.to_ascii_uppercase())` — identical uppercasing before CRC. Therefore mix_hash("drok.wav") == mix_hash("DROK.WAV"), and AssetManager::lookup_location_for_name (asset_manager.rs:442) resolves both to the same MIX entry. The extension case difference is irrelevant at the hash level.

3. The .aud fallback (music.rs:307): only activates if the .wav lookup returns None. Since "drok.wav" hashes identically to "DROK.WAV" and finds the same entry, the fallback never fires for any retail theme track. The finding acknowledges no .aud-only theme stem exists in retail data.

The mechanism differs (one extension vs two), but the output is bit-identical for all retail inputs. The finding's gamemd facts are correctly stated; the drift conclusion is wrong because Rust's MIX lookup is case-insensitive by design (same uppercasing algorithm as gamemd before CRC computation).

findings.title: INTRO loop seam re-queues on next frame (player.empty poll), like gamemd's poll-based Theme::AI
findings.rust_location: src/audio/music.rs:203-223 (update: detects empty() then start_track) ; src/app.rs:2284-2285 (per-frame play_menu_theme + update on MainMenu)
findings.gamemd_behavior: gamemd loops INTRO via poll: Theme::AI (FUN_007209d0, called from the audio pump FUN_00406f70) checks StreamPlayer::IsPlaying each tick and re-queues the pending index from g_Theme+0x8 only after the stream reports finished. There is a natural inter-tick gap at the loop seam (no gapless/crossfade callback) sized by the audio pump cadence.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust update() polls Player::empty() once per rendered frame and restarts via start_track. The seam gap is therefore sized by the render frame interval rather than gamemd's audio-pump tick interval. Whether the resulting silence gap length matches gamemd's is unproven; both are poll-based with a small gap, but the poll period differs (render fps vs audio pump rate), so the exact loop-seam silence duration may differ.
findings.evidence: MAIN_MENU_MUSIC_TRACK_AND_LOOP_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §Loop/re-queue (Theme::AI poll via FUN_00406f70, no completion callback). Rust src/audio/music.rs:204-223.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side verified at music.rs:204-207 and app.rs:2284-2285: `update()` polls `player.empty()` once per render frame on the main menu (inside the winit `render()` callback). The restart (music.rs:215) fires on the next frame after `empty()` returns true — so the seam gap is bounded by one render frame interval (~16.7ms at 60fps).

gamemd side confirmed via MAIN_MENU_MUSIC_TRACK_AND_LOOP_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §Loop/re-queue: `Theme::AI` (FUN_007209d0) is called from `FUN_00406f70` which is itself called from `Network_ServiceLoop @ 0x0048D080` and `FUN_006071E0`. The MP_SYNC_CRC_DESYNC_RECONNECT doc confirms `FUN_00406F70` is the first call inside `Network_ServiceLoop`. The shell loop runs this each iteration of the Win32 message pump; the pump cadence during idle shell is not documented in any research doc and was not verified in binary — it could be very fast (uncapped message pump, potentially hundreds of Hz) or vsync-throttled.

The finding's factual claims about both sides are correct: both poll-based, no gapless callback, gap sized by poll interval. The drift claim stands because the poll periods are structurally different (render frame interval in Rust vs Win32 message-pump cadence in gamemd), and no algebraic proof, empirical bit-identical check, or exhaustive caller verification exists to establish equivalence. "Both have a small gap so probably the same" is explicitly excluded by CLAUDE.md burden of proof. Cannot refute as false positive.

findings.title: No EVA voice on main-menu entry (Rust matches gamemd verified-negative)
findings.rust_location: src/app.rs:2274-2286 (MainMenu screen plays only play_menu_theme; no EVA call)

findings.rust_behavior: Rust MainMenu render path triggers only the looping INTRO theme and plays no EVA on entry — correct parity, no welcome cue. Listed for completeness (no disparity), per the auditing scope item.
findings.evidence: EVA_WELCOME_BACK_MAIN_MENU_TRIGGER_GHIDRA_REPORT.md (verified negative, HIGH on all axes). Rust src/app.rs:2274-2286 confirms no EVA call on MainMenu.

Grep across app.rs shows EVA registry is loaded at startup and stored in state, but is never invoked on the MainMenu screen path.

gamemd side confirmed via docs/research/EVA_WELCOME_BACK_MAIN_MENU_TRIGGER_GHIDRA_REPORT.md (dated 2026-05-18, source: ghidra/verified, HIGH confidence on all three axes). The doc reports: full decompile of `FUN_00531CC0` (dialog 0xE2 proc, the main-menu construction/pump function) and `Main_Game @ 0x0052D9A0` — neither contains any `VoxClass::PlayEVA` or `VoxClass::QueueVoice` invocation. `VoxClass::PlayEVA` at `0x00752700` has 70+ callers; none reside in any shell-construction or shell-message-pump function. No `EVA_Welcome*` entry exists in `evamd.ini`. The only audio on shell entry is the `[INTRO]` theme music (track key `Drok`).

Both sides produce identical output (no EVA on main-menu entry). This is a correctly-labeled verified-negative: no disparity exists, no fix needed. findings.title: Transition is a whole-screen horizontal pixel-slide + crossfade; gamemd's slide is an SHP-frame-index wave with no positional slide and no crossfade
findings.gamemd_behavior: FUN_006071E0 does NOT translate any window/sprite by a per-frame X/Y delta and does NOT crossfade. It advances per-cell SHP frame indices (SDBTNANM frames cycle 10->5 over 6 steps then settle at frame 1 on show; 5->10 then settle at 10 on close), producing a wave illusion. The only spatial offset that ever moves is a single discrete +0x50 (80px) shift of the SDTP/radar shape above a screen-width threshold, keyed by phase not a per-frame ramp.
findings.rust_behavior: Shader translates source by -round(progress*32) px and destination by round((1-progress)*64) px horizontally, fades source alpha 1.0->0.7 and crossfades destination alpha 0->1. This is a continuous geometric slide + alpha crossfade of two full-screen render targets, a mechanism gamemd never uses for shell transitions.
findings.evidence: SHELL_DIALOG_SLIDE_TRANSITION_TRANSFORM_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §Executive Summary + §2/§3 ('animation is NOT a position slide', 'no per-frame (x,y) translation', frame-index 10->5/5->10); contradicts shell_transition.wgsl source_offset_px/destination_offset_px/source_alpha math

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side verified from `src/render/shell_transition.wgsl:42-55`: `source_offset_px = -round(progress*32)`, `destination_offset_px = round((1-progress)*64)`, `source_alpha = 1.0 - 0.3*progress`, `destination_alpha = progress`. This is a continuous horizontal pixel-slide of two full-screen render targets with an alpha crossfade. `src/app_shell_transition.rs:117-181` drives the pass via `ShellTransitionPass` rendering both shell states to offscreen targets and compositing them.

gamemd side verified from `docs/research/SHELL_DIALOG_SLIDE_TRANSITION_TRANSFORM_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` (HIGH confidence, live Ghidra decompile of `FUN_006071E0 @ 0x006071E0..0x00607FC0`): §3 "No per-frame (x,y) translation in the body" — no `SetWindowPos` call, no per-frame rect mutation; cell Y advances once per cell not per frame; the only horizontal offset is a discrete +0x50 SDTP shift keyed by phase classifier branch not a ramp. §2 confirms the animation is SHP frame-index cycling: per-cell stagger schedule, 6-step linear transition of frame index, `Sleep(30ms)` per tick. Corroborated by `docs/research/FUN_006071E0_SLIDE_IN_FRAME_SCHEDULE_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` §5 (full 4-case formula, HIGH content/identity confidence).

`docs/research/SHELL_TRANSITION_ON_MAIN_MENU_CLICK_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` §8 further states that for the main-menu-click path specifically, parity is NO animation at all between dialogs at that step — making the Rust slide+crossfade doubly wrong.

The finding's description of the gamemd frame direction ("10->5 on show") uses DL=1=show convention from the TRANSFORM doc; the FRAME_SCHEDULE doc uses cVar14=0 for slide-in with base=5, step=+1. Both agree on SHP-frame cycling with no position slide. The finding's core claim is accurate on both sides.

findings.title: No between-page transition should exist on the main-menu->Skirmish path at all; gamemd shows an instant dialog swap
findings.gamemd_behavior: Dialog 0xE2 main-menu click never sets the +0xC1 slide gate, so FUN_00608070 bails during cleanup and the dialog is DestroyWindow'd with no animation; the next dialog appears instantly. The route is 0x683->1->dialog0x100->0x579->0x0B->0x102; the 0x579 result is written directly by proc 0x0052D640 with no call to the transition helper. Route-active reachability of the helper is explicitly UNPROVEN.

findings.evidence: SHELL_TRANSITION_ON_MAIN_MENU_CLICK_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §1/§8 ('do NOT implement a between-page transition'); SHELL_MENU_TRANSITION_SYSTEM_MODEL_SYNTHESIS.md §8 Do-Not-Implement; module is self-described as bridge/DRIFT in src/app_shell_transition.rs:1-6

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed: `src/app_shell_transition.rs` lines 42-93 implement `ShellBridgeTransition::new_main_menu_to_skirmish` with a 14-frame × 30 ms whole-screen compositor. `render_main_menu_to_skirmish_transition` (lines 117-181) actually runs this animation every frame while the transition is active. The module header (lines 1-6) self-labels "bridge/DRIFT" but the animation code is live and runs on main-menu → Skirmish clicks.

gamemd side confirmed via `SHELL_TRANSITION_ON_MAIN_MENU_CLICK_GHIDRA_REPORT.md`: exhaustive byte-pattern searches across all 8 register encodings for `MOV byte ptr [reg+0xBD], 1` found exactly one match (address `0x006083D3` inside `FUN_00608380`), and `get_xrefs_to 0x00608380` returned only one caller — `CDFileClass__Constructor` in the Load/Save save-success branch. No main-menu code path sets `record[+0xC1]`. Therefore `FUN_00608070` bails at its first check in dialog cleanup (§3 step 7), and `DestroyWindow` runs instantly with no animation. This is verified via `decompile_function` of `0x00531CC0`, `0x00531F60`, `0x00608070`, `0x00622720` (all cited in §9 of that report).

The stated route `0x683 → result 1 → dialog 0x100 → 0x579 → result 0x0B → 0x102` is confirmed in `SHELL_MENU_TRANSITION_SYSTEM_MODEL_SYNTHESIS.md` §1/§2, with proc `0x0052D640` writing `0x0B` directly, no call to the slide helper.

The one open question (synthesis §7: whether `0x00612690` is reached during the `0x579` click on the intermediate Single Player shell dialog `0x100`) does not apply here — the finding concerns dialog 0xE2 (the initial main menu), where the slide gate proof is exhaustive and rated HIGH. Even if the `0x100→0x102` leg eventually proves to have a native slide, the Rust animator runs on the `0xE2→Skirmish` click, which is a different and fully-proven-no-transition path.

No algebraic proof, bit-identical check, or exhaustive caller verification can downgrade this to false-positive. The Rust output (animated cross-fade lasting ~420 ms) differs from gamemd output (instant dialog swap) on a player-visible path.

findings.title: Transition runs a fixed 14 frames; gamemd's slide runs (N+8) frames where N = child-cell count
findings.gamemd_behavior: total_frames = N + 8, where N is the number of cells returned by EnumChildWindows; for the right-column with its button rows N varies, so the duration is data-driven, not a fixed 14.
findings.rust_behavior: Hardcoded frame_count = SHELL_BRIDGE_FRAME_COUNT = 14, independent of any cell/button count.
findings.evidence: SHELL_DIALOG_SLIDE_TRANSITION_TRANSFORM_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §1 ('total_frames = N + 8'); FUN_006071E0_SLIDE_IN_FRAME_SCHEDULE_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §1; const at app_shell_transition.rs:15

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Checked three sources:

1. Rust side (`src/app_shell_transition.rs:1-5`): The module's own `//!` doc declares it "explicitly bridge/DRIFT code" — a temporary whole-screen compositor used until the real skirmish-shell path exists. `SHELL_BRIDGE_FRAME_COUNT = 14` (line 15) drives `frame_count` at line 47. This is not a parity attempt at the gamemd slide system; it is an acknowledged placeholder.

2. gamemd side — `SHELL_TRANSITION_ON_MAIN_MENU_CLICK_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` (exhaustive Ghidra verification, session 2026-05-29): The slide animation `FUN_006071E0` does **not fire on standard YR main-menu button clicks**. The entire system is gated by `record[+0xC1] != 0`, and the only setter (`FUN_00608380` at `0x006083D3`) is reachable only from `CDFileClass__Constructor`'s save-success branch. Exhaustive byte-pattern search across all 8 register encodings confirmed exactly one match in the binary. `decompile_function` calls on `0x00531CC0` (launcher), `0x00531F60` (dialog proc), and `0x00622720` + `0x00608070` (cleanup chain) confirm the slide-out trigger bails immediately on `+0xC1 == 0` for dialog 0xE2. No animation plays between main-menu click and next dialog.

3. The `(N+8)` formula is real — confirmed in `SHELL_DIALOG_SLIDE_TRANSITION_TRANSFORM_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` §1 — but applies only to dialogs where `+0xC1` is set (Load/Save, possibly WOL), not to main-menu navigation.

The finding's `gamemd_behavior` claim ("total_frames = N + 8 ... for the right-column with its button rows N varies") mis-attributes the slide system to the main-menu surface where gamemd never runs it. There is no gamemd slide animation to be 14-vs-(N+8) frames different from. The Rust bridge transition is a self-declared placeholder for a completely different UX (a whole-screen compositor covering the instant hard-snap to skirmish), not a mis-timed clone of the gamemd slide. The finding is a false positive because the gamemd side was mis-stated: the slide system is not active on main-menu clicks in YR.
verdict_obj.correction: The gamemd_behavior claim is wrong for this surface. `FUN_006071E0` with its `(N+8)` frame schedule is gated by `record[+0xC1]`, which is never set for dialog 0xE2 (main menu). gamemd plays no slide animation on a main-menu button click — the dialog is destroyed instantly. The `(N+8)` formula applies to Load/Save dialogs (and possibly WOL screens) where `+0xC1` is set via `FUN_00608380`. The Rust `ShellBridgeTransition` is a self-labeled temporary bridge for the hard snap to skirmish, not a parity attempt at the slide system; it should not be compared to `FUN_006071E0` at all.

findings.title: 30 ms per-frame tick is correct in value but driven by wall-clock Instant catch-up, not a per-frame blocking Sleep loop
findings.gamemd_behavior: FUN_006071E0 runs a synchronous loop with Sleep(0x1E)=30 ms per tick, advancing exactly one frame per iteration; total wall-clock = (N+8)*30 ms. The 30 ms cadence is correct, but gamemd advances one frame per Sleep, never multiple frames to 'catch up' a missed deadline.
findings.rust_behavior: advance_to() runs a while-loop that can advance multiple frame_index steps in one render call if more than one frame_duration elapsed since last_step, so a slow frame collapses several animation steps at once rather than blocking 30 ms each.
findings.evidence: SHELL_DIALOG_SLIDE_TRANSITION_TRANSFORM_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §1 (Sleep(0x1E)=30ms, one frame per iteration); advance_to while-loop at app_shell_transition.rs:67-72

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed by reading src/app_shell_transition.rs lines 65-72: advance_to() runs a while-loop that checks now.duration_since(last_step_at) >= frame_duration and can increment frame_index multiple times per render call if the render frame took longer than 30 ms. This is a wall-clock catch-up mechanism.

gamemd side confirmed by SHELL_DIALOG_SLIDE_TRANSITION_TRANSFORM_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §1 (High confidence, assembly-verified): FUN_006071E0 at 0x006071E0 runs a synchronous blocking loop with PUSH 0x1e / CALL Sleep at 0x00607f0f-0x00607f11, advancing exactly one tick per Sleep(30 ms). No catch-up logic exists — the loop index advances by one per iteration unconditionally.

Under load (render frame > 30 ms), Rust collapses multiple animation steps into one rendered frame; gamemd would still render each intermediate SHP frame state (one per 30 ms Sleep). Under normal conditions (render < 30 ms/frame) both produce the same per-frame output.

The Rust module's own header (line 1-5) explicitly self-labels the module as "bridge/DRIFT code," corroborating rather than refuting the finding.

No refutation grounds: this is not TS-legacy (doc §6 confirms no SpecialFlags gate, YR-active). The behaviors are not algebraically equivalent for slow-render inputs. The finding correctly states both sides.

findings.title: Per-cell 1-frame stagger schedule is absent; all content moves uniformly instead of as a swept wave
findings.rust_location: src/render/shell_transition.wgsl:42-55 (uniform progress applied to whole screen)
findings.gamemd_behavior: Each child cell idx receives a 1-frame stagger offset (local_17c[idx]); its local progress is tick - stagger_offset, so cells begin animating on successive frames, producing a wave that sweeps the dialog top-to-bottom.
findings.rust_behavior: A single scalar progress drives the entire framebuffer uniformly; there is no per-button/per-cell phase offset, so all content slides together with no wave.
findings.evidence: SHELL_DIALOG_SLIDE_TRANSITION_TRANSFORM_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §2 (1-frame stagger, local_17c); FUN_006071E0_SLIDE_IN_FRAME_SCHEDULE_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §5 (per-cell delta); shell_transition.wgsl uses one params.progress

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Read `src/render/shell_transition.wgsl` lines 42-55 (confirmed: uniform `params.progress` scalar, no per-cell stagger) and `src/app_shell_transition.rs` (header line 1-5 explicitly labels this "bridge/DRIFT code... not a verified native YR flow").

Then pulled `SHELL_TRANSITION_ON_MAIN_MENU_CLICK_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` (the most recent and authoritative doc, which explicitly supersedes the two docs cited in the finding). Key conclusions verified against binary this session:

1. The stagger-wave animation (`FUN_006071E0`) is gated by `record[+0xC1]`. Only one setter exists in the entire binary: `FUN_00608380` at `0x006083D3`, reachable only from `CDFileClass__Constructor`'s save-success branch (Load/Save dialogs). Verified via exhaustive byte-pattern search across all 8 register encodings. (`search_byte_patterns C6 80/81/82/83/84/85/86/87 BD 00 00 00 01` → exactly one match.)

2. Dialog 0xE2 (main menu) never sets `+0xC1`. The full click path was traced: OwnerDraw_Button_00612B70 → WM_COMMAND → MainMenuDialog0xE2_Proc_00531F60 → FUN_00531CC0 exits message pump → FUN_00622720 (cleanup) → FUN_00608070 bails immediately on `+0xC1 == 0` gate → DestroyWindow. No animation fires. (`decompile_function` on 0x00531CC0, 0x00531F60, 0x00622720, 0x00608070, 0x00612B70 all confirmed.)

3. The research doc the finding cites (`FUN_006071E0_SLIDE_IN_FRAME_SCHEDULE_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §5` and `SHELL_DIALOG_SLIDE_TRANSITION_TRANSFORM_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §2`) describe a real mechanism, but `SHELL_TRANSITION_ON_MAIN_MENU_CLICK_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §4` explicitly refutes those docs' earlier claim that this mechanism "fires on every main-menu button click" — that claim was wrong. The transform doc is corroborated for the Load/Save flow but not for the main-menu sub-surface.

Conclusion: on the `mm-transition` / `main_menu` sub-surface, gamemd produces **no animation at all** (instant dialog swap). The Rust compositor is a known placeholder (bridge code, self-labelled DRIFT). There is no stagger wave to be absent from either side on this specific surface. The finding's gamemd claim is mis-stated: gamemd does not produce a per-cell stagger wave on main-menu clicks, so there is no drift to confirm.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding's gamemd claim is wrong for the mm-transition sub-surface. gamemd plays NO stagger animation when a main-menu button (dialog 0xE2) is clicked — the slide system (`FUN_006071E0` with `local_17c[]` stagger) is gated by `record[+0xC1]`, which is set only by `CDFileClass__Constructor`'s save-success branch (Load/Save dialogs only). The main-menu click path never sets `+0xC1`; `FUN_00608070` bails immediately, and `DestroyWindow` fires with no animation. The stagger wave described in the cited docs is real but fires only on Load/Save and (likely) WOL screens, not on main-menu navigation. The Rust code is a self-acknowledged placeholder (`app_shell_transition.rs` line 1-5: "not a verified native YR flow") that correctly produces no stagger wave — matching gamemd's actual behavior on this surface.

findings.title: Source 30% alpha fade and destination 0->1 crossfade have no gamemd counterpart
findings.gamemd_behavior: FUN_006071E0 does no alpha blending between an old and new full-screen image; it redraws SHP shape frames opaquely (CC_Draw_Shape). There is no source-fade or destination-fade.
findings.rust_behavior: fs_main fades source to 0.70*progress brightness and crossfades a destination image in via destination_alpha=progress, blending two screens — an effect absent from the binary.
findings.evidence: SHELL_DIALOG_SLIDE_TRANSITION_TRANSFORM_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3 (frame transitions via CC_Draw_Shape, no blend); shell_transition.wgsl:47-53

This is an active GPU composite that runs during every main-menu-to-skirmish transition (invoked via `ShellTransitionPass::draw` in `app_shell_transition.rs:168`).

gamemd side confirmed from `SHELL_DIALOG_SLIDE_TRANSITION_TRANSFORM_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3` (verified against `FUN_006071E0 @ 0x006071E0..0x00607FC0`): the animation cycles SHP frame indices per child cell — no `SetWindowPos`, no alpha blend, no second screen composited in. The only spatial offset is a discrete `+0x50` px horizontal shift at specific phase branches, not a per-frame ramp or opacity curve.

`app_shell_transition.rs:1-5` (module `//!` header) explicitly labels this as `bridge/DRIFT code` — the codebase itself acknowledges no parity claim. The Rust transition runs for `SHELL_BRIDGE_FRAME_COUNT = 14` frames at 30 ms each (420 ms total), showing a crossfade visible on every main-menu → skirmish click. gamemd shows an opaque SHP-frame-cycling animation with no counterpart blend. The difference is player-visible on every transition invocation.

findings.title: Resize-during-transition policy snaps return-to-menu vs complete-to-skirmish at 50% progress; no gamemd analog
findings.gamemd_behavior: gamemd's synchronous Sleep-loop transition cannot be interrupted by a window resize mid-animation (the message pump is blocked during the loop); there is no concept of a 50%-progress branch that either cancels back to the menu or jumps forward to the destination.
findings.rust_behavior: On resize, the transition is taken and resolved by a progress<0.5 threshold to either ReturnToMainMenu or CompleteToSkirmish, a bespoke behavior with no binary counterpart.
findings.evidence: SHELL_DIALOG_SLIDE_TRANSITION_TRANSFORM_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §1 (synchronous Sleep loop); resolve_resize at app_shell_transition.rs:103-115; this is bridge/DRIFT code per module header

verdict_obj.reasoning: Rust side confirmed: `src/app_shell_transition.rs` lines 103-115 implement `resolve_resize` exactly as described — `transition.progress() < 0.5` branches to `ReturnToMainMenu`, `>= 0.5` to `CompleteToSkirmish`. The module header (lines 1-5) self-declares this as "bridge/DRIFT code." gamemd side confirmed: `SHELL_DIALOG_SLIDE_TRANSITION_TRANSFORM_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` §1 (hit via research_search) documents the synchronous Sleep loop at `0x006071E0` (`PUSH 0x1e` / `CALL Sleep`), which blocks the Win32 message pump for the full ~420 ms animation. A WM_SIZE during this period queues but cannot be dispatched until the loop exits — gamemd has no mid-transition interrupt path and therefore no concept of a progress-threshold branch. The 0.5 snap policy is entirely Rust-invented. No algebraic proof, bit-identical check, or exhaustive caller verification exists to establish equivalence. The finding is correctly stated on both sides. The drift is real: Rust can cancel a transition back to the menu (or skip it forward) in a scenario that gamemd simply cannot reach. Player visibility is low (requires resizing mid-transition), but per CLAUDE.md that does not downgrade the verdict — it only affects scheduling priority.

findings.title: All shell input is blocked for the full transition duration; gamemd blocks input via a synchronous loop but the durations/semantics differ and this path has no transition at all
findings.rust_location: src/app_shell_transition.rs:95-101 (blocks_shell_input returns true whenever a transition exists)

findings.evidence: SHELL_TRANSITION_ON_MAIN_MENU_CLICK_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §6 (instant dialog swap, no animation); transition_blocks_shell_input at app_shell_transition.rs:99-101
