Rust side confirmed at `src/app_loading.rs:718`: `font_h = bar_height` (H = 14.0 from PROGBARM.SHP frame 0 pixel height used in tests at line 1272).

gamemd side confirmed via `decompile_function 0x00642ef0`: the row height formula is `row_h = max(side_icon_h, H+6, font_h) + 4` where `font_h = *(g_GAME_FNT + 0x1c)` (the BitFont line-height field). `g_GAME_FNT` is returned by `FUN_004a60d0` (confirmed). The BitFont `+0x1c` field is documented as 17 px in `BITFONT_SHELL_TEXT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` struct layout table (GAME.FNT cell_height = 17), cross-confirmed by `BitFont__Constructor` at `0x00433880` storing `inner+0xC` into `param_1[7]` (= offset 0x1c).

Algebraic proof the stand-in produces identical output for all H ≥ 11:
- gamemd: `max(side_icon_h, H+6, 17) + 4`
- Rust:   `max(side_icon_h, H+6, H)  + 4`
Both simplify to `max(side_icon_h, H+6) + 4` when H ≥ 11 (so H+6 ≥ 17 ≥ H, making the third max term irrelevant in both formulas).

With the actual PROGBARM.SHP bar height H = 14 (test fixture at `app_loading.rs:1272`, consistent with binary geometry analysis): H+6 = 20 > font_h = 17 > H = 14. The `H+6` term dominates in both the gamemd and Rust formulas. The stand-in substitution `font_h = H` has no effect on row_h because `H (14) < H+6 (20)` always makes the stand-in the non-dominant term in the same way `font_h=17` would be non-dominant.

The finding's stated risk — "when the real loading font height exceeds (H+6) it would dominate row_h" — requires H < 11. PROGBARM.SHP frame-0 height is 14, so H+6 = 20, which exceeds font_h = 17. No divergence occurs for the actual retail asset. The finding is a false positive.

Caveat: OQ-08 in `PROGBARM_PROGRESSCLASS_DRAW_GEOMETRY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` formally defers numeric SHP frame dimensions; H = 14 comes from the Rust test fixture, not an asset dump. If the real asset were ≤ 10 px tall the finding would be real — but that is inconsistent with the observed loading bar width/height ratio and test assumptions.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding's gamemd formula is correctly stated. The Rust stand-in behavior is also correctly described. The error is in the risk assessment: font_h = 17 (GAME.FNT) does NOT exceed H+6 = 20 (bar height 14 + 6) for the actual PROGBARM.SHP dimensions, so the stand-in never produces a different row_h in practice. Both the gamemd and Rust formulas collapse to max(side_icon_h, H+6) + 4 = max(side_icon_h, 20) + 4 = 24 for the Skirmish loading bar with no side icon.

findings.title: ftol positive-domain fill width skips exact x87 fractional truncation
findings.gamemd_behavior: Native fill width is ftol(frame0_width * lane/max) using the x87 FPU control word (round-to-nearest by default, or whatever CW is set) producing a specific integer per fraction at every milestone.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust rounds when within an epsilon of an integer, otherwise truncates via `value as i32`. The code comment acknowledges 'Exact fractional x87 control-word behavior remains a narrow follow-up.' For fractional products not near an integer this can yield a 1-pixel-different fill width than native ftol at some milestone percentages.
findings.evidence: PROGBARM_PROGRESSCLASS_DRAW_GEOMETRY OQ-03 (clipped width ftol(frame0_width*fraction)); decompile_function 0x00643400 (Math__ftol call); Rust src/app_loading.rs:994

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Read Rust at src/app_loading.rs:987-996 and 113-116. The function `gamemd_ftol_positive_domain` rounds to nearest when `|value - round(value)| <= 1e-6`, otherwise truncates via `value as i32`. The `fill_width_gamemd_ftol_positive_domain` method at line 113-116 feeds `frame0_width * fraction` into this.

Disassembled `Math__ftol` at 0x007c5f00 (verified via `disassemble_function 0x007c5f00`): the function reads the current x87 FPU CW with `FNSTCW`, compares it against `DAT_00822d80`, and if different loads `DAT_00822d80` via `FLDCW` before issuing `FISTP qword`. Read `0x00822d80` (verified via `read_memory 0x00822d80`): bytes `7f 0e 00 00` = CW value `0x0e7f`. Bits 10-11 of `0x0e7f` = `11` = RC field = round toward zero (truncate). So `FISTP` always executes in truncation mode.

For positive values, truncation toward zero equals floor. Rust introduces an epsilon-based round-to-nearest branch that gamemd does not have: when a product lands within 1e-6 of an integer, gamemd still truncates but Rust rounds up. For example, if `frame0_width * fraction = 99.9999995`, gamemd yields 99 and Rust yields 100 — a 1-pixel fill-width difference on the loading bar.

The finding's gamemd description is mis-stated: it says "round-to-nearest by default, or whatever CW is set." The binary shows `Math__ftol` always normalizes CW to `0x0e7f` (truncate toward zero) before `FISTP` — it does not use "whatever CW is set" and does not round-to-nearest. The corrected gamemd behavior is: unconditional truncation toward zero. The drift is real: Rust's epsilon rounding introduces an upward round at near-integer products that gamemd never produces.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding mis-states the gamemd side. It claims Math__ftol uses "round-to-nearest by default, or whatever CW is set." The binary at 0x007c5f00 shows Math__ftol explicitly normalizes the x87 FPU control word to 0x0e7f (RC=11, truncate toward zero) via FLDCW before every FISTP. The behavior is always truncation toward zero — never round-to-nearest. For positive values this is floor(). The drift is that Rust adds an epsilon-based round-to-nearest step (within 1e-6 of an integer) that does not exist in the original Math__ftol.

findings.title: Per-frame loading render clears to CLEAR_COLOR instead of seeding from prior surface
findings.gamemd_behavior: The first renderer 0x00552D60 path and the mode-2 WM_PAINT path both seed/compose onto the existing surface; the LS background SHP is drawn opaque over the full centered rect (covering the screen at native size). There is no flat clear-to-arbitrary-color exposed where the SHP does not cover.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust clears the whole color attachment to CLEAR_COLOR every loading frame, then draws the background top-left. Combined with the (0,0) anchor (see ls-composition-text-background-not-centered), at non-native sizes the uncovered margin shows CLEAR_COLOR rather than the native composition. At native size the background fully covers so this is benign; the disparity is conditional on resolution.
findings.evidence: LOADING_SCREEN_WM_PAINT_MODE2_COMPOSITION handoff (seed copy before SHP, no branch-local clear); LOADING_FIRST_RENDERER_00552D60 (centered full-rect background); Rust src/app_loading.rs:642,887

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust code confirmed at app_loading.rs:642 and app_loading.rs:887: both render paths use `LoadOp::Clear(CLEAR_COLOR)` (pure black wgpu::Color {r:0,g:0,b:0,a:1}) before drawing the background SHP. The background is placed at [0.0, 0.0] top-left (build_native_loading_instances line 786).

gamemd side: LOADING_FIRST_RENDERER_00552D60_GHIDRA_REPORT.md (composition ledger row 1) confirms the LS background SHP is drawn with a centered rect: origin `(screen - 640/800, screen - 480/600)/2` from `0x00552B10`. At native 640x480 or 800x600 this is (0,0), so background fully covers. LOADING_SCREEN_WM_PAINT_MODE2_COMPOSITION_GHIDRA_REPORT.md (composition ledger row 1, OQ-3 resolved) confirms mode-2 path seeds from `DAT_00887310` (copies existing surface) before drawing PUDLGBG* SHP — no branch-local clear.

At native resolution (640x480 or 800x600): the LS SHP is 640x480 or 800x600 pixels and fully covers the surface regardless of anchor, so the clear-vs-seed distinction produces identical output. This is benign.

At non-native resolution: gamemd centers the SHP leaving margins, and those margins show the seeded prior surface (mode-2 path) or the freshly-allocated BSurface content (first-renderer path). Rust clears to black then draws at (0,0), producing black margins rather than centered art with whatever-the-seeded-surface shows. This is a real output difference conditional on non-native resolution.

Factual correction: the finding says "the first renderer 0x00552D60 path ... seed/compose onto the existing surface" — this is slightly imprecise. The first-renderer path (0x00552D60) allocates a fresh BSurface (composition ledger row 0: "allocates manager BSurface at +0x60; vtable clear/copy path"). The seed-copy behavior is specific to the mode-2 WM_PAINT path only. The finding conflates the two paths. The correct statement is: mode-2 WM_PAINT seeds from the prior surface; the first renderer draws to a freshly allocated BSurface. In both cases, at non-native resolution, gamemd's centered anchor leaves margins that Rust's (0,0) anchor + full-screen clear does not reproduce.
verdict_obj.correction: The claim that "the first renderer 0x00552D60 path seeds/composes onto the existing surface" is imprecise. Per LOADING_FIRST_RENDERER_00552D60_GHIDRA_REPORT.md composition ledger row 0, that renderer allocates a fresh BSurface (vtable clear/copy setup); it is the mode-2 WM_PAINT path (0x00621E90) that seeds from the existing DAT_00887310 surface. The finding correctly identifies that Rust clears to CLEAR_COLOR where gamemd does not issue a flat clear in either path, but the "seed from prior surface" characterization applies only to mode-2 WM_PAINT, not to the first renderer.

findings.title: Country side icon (G4 PCX) draw not proven to exist in first-renderer composition
findings.gamemd_behavior: PROGBARM draw geometry (FUN_00643720) reserves W+0x15 horizontal space and FUN_004e3560 returns a side-emblem object used for vertical centering when +0x70=1, consistent with a side emblem drawn near the bar. The exact emblem asset (whether the *i.pcx insignia files Rust uses, or another SHP/emblem) and its actual blit in the first-renderer vs progress-row path was not bit-verified in this audit.
findings.rust_behavior: Rust loads per-country *i.pcx insignia (usai.pcx etc., from FUN_004e3560 mapping cited in code) and draws it at base_x + bar_w + 0x15, vertically centered. The mapping is plausibly correct but the emblem's pixel placement/centering and whether it is drawn at the progress row vs the LS-background pass needs binary confirmation against FUN_004e3560 + FUN_00643720 emblem branch.
findings.evidence: decompile_function 0x00643720 (local_30 = *param_4 + 0x15 + W; FUN_004e3560 emblem object with vtable +0x7c/+0x80 dims); loading_screen_chrome.rs:82 cites FUN_004e3560

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Read src/app_loading.rs:738-747 and src/render/loading_screen_chrome.rs:82-96. Decompiled FUN_00643720 (0x00643720) and FUN_004e3560 (0x004e3560) directly via Ghidra MCP.

Three load-bearing claims from the finding checked against binary:

1. Asset filenames: FUN_004e3560 is a dispatch table — param_1==0→usai.pcx, 1→japi.pcx, 2→frai.pcx, 3→geri.pcx, 4→gbri.pcx, 5→djbi.pcx, 6→arbi.pcx, 7→lati.pcx, 8→rusi.pcx, -3→obsi.pcx, 9→yrii.pcx. Rust's LoadingVariant::side_icon_pcx() matches this mapping exactly (loading_screen_chrome.rs:82-95).

2. X position: FUN_00643720 computes local_30 = *param_4 + 0x15 + local_8, where *param_4 is base_x and local_8 is the PROGBARM frame width (W) from SHP_frame_rect_getter. Rust uses base_x + bar_width + SIDE_ICON_GAP where SIDE_ICON_GAP = 0x15 as f32 (app_loading.rs:47, 745). Formulas are identical.

3. Vertical centering: gamemd calls FUN_004e3560() vtable +0x80 to get icon height then centers within row_h. Rust centers using atlas.side_icon.pixel_size[1] (app_loading.rs:831). Both use the actual PCX height — equivalent.

4. Draw gate: the emblem blit in FUN_00643720 is gated by (char)param_6 != '\0', which is the +0x70 skirmish flag (confirmed via FUN_00642c80: *(char*)(param_1+0x70) = param_5). In skirmish param_5 is non-zero, so the icon always draws. Rust's if let Some(icon) = atlas.side_icon mirrors this — icon is Some whenever the PCX loads (which always succeeds for valid countries). Equivalent.

5. Ghidra mistyped FUN_004e3560 as void __fastcall — it actually returns the result of FUN_006ba140 (an undefined4 PCX object pointer), which is why FUN_00643720 uses its return value. The finding's claim that it "returns a side-emblem object" is correct; Ghidra's void signature is an artifact. This does not affect the Rust comparison.

No unresolved ambiguity on the gamemd side. The finding's "not bit-verified" uncertainty was unfounded — the binary directly confirms all three load-bearing elements (filenames, x-formula, centering) match Rust.
verdict_obj.correction: FUN_004e3560 is mistyped as void by Ghidra; it returns a PCX object pointer (undefined4) via FUN_006ba140. The finding correctly described it as returning a side-emblem object. The x-position formula in gamemd is base_x + W + 0x15 (not W + 0x15 alone); Rust's base_x + bar_width + 0x15 matches this correctly.

findings.title: Default player name is hardcoded literal "Player" instead of the seeded profile name global
findings.gamemd_behavior: Setup FUN_006AE6E0 seeds edit 0x6A0 from the player-name global DAT_00A8B380 (converted to wide via FUN_00735120 and sent with message 0x4B2), and Start FUN_006ACEE0 reads the edit back into DAT_00A8B380. DAT_00A8B380 is a persistent global written elsewhere (e.g. SimpleWonlineDialogControl__Constructor at 0x00786922 WRITE per get_xrefs_to 0x00A8B380), i.e. the last-used / profile player name, NOT a fixed string literal baked into the skirmish dialog.
findings.rust_behavior: PlayerNameEditState::default() always initializes text to the constant string "Player". There is no read of any persisted/profile name; every fresh skirmish always shows exactly "Player".
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_PLAYER_NAME_EDIT_CONTROL_0X6A0_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3.1 and Negative Facts ("Do not hardcode the displayed name to Player; setup sources DAT_00A8B380"); decompile_function 0x006AE6E0 shows FUN_00735120()->0x4B2 seeding from the global; get_xrefs_to 0x00A8B380 shows external WRITE at 0x00786922.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed at `src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/player_name.rs:16` (`PLAYER_NAME_DEFAULT = "Player"`) and `:32` (`text: PLAYER_NAME_DEFAULT.to_string()`). `SkirmishShellState::default()` at line 248 calls `PlayerNameEditState::default()`, which always sets `text` to the literal "Player". A full Grep of the skirmish_shell tree found zero reads of any persisted, profile, or saved name. The launch path at `state/launch.rs:157` reads `state.player_name_edit.text` directly, so whatever was seeded at open time is what gets passed to the session.

gamemd side confirmed by `SKIRMISH_PLAYER_NAME_EDIT_CONTROL_0X6A0_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` §3.1 (verified from binary): `FUN_006AE6E0` at `0x006AE6F2..0x006AE735` seeds the edit control via `CALL 0x00735120` on `DAT_00A8B380` then sends message `0x4B2` — the current value of the persistent player-name global, not a hardcoded literal. §3.5 and integration table confirm `FUN_006ACEE0` at `0x006AD375..0x006AD39F` reads the edit back into `DAT_00A8B380` on Start, making it persist across sessions. `get_xrefs_to 0x00A8B380` shows external writers (e.g., `0x00786922`).

The finding's description of the research doc note ("Do not hardcode the displayed name to Player; setup sources DAT_00A8B380") is accurate. The doc's §6 status table was stale (written before `PlayerNameEditState` was added to the codebase), but the core drift — initialization from a hardcoded literal instead of the persistent global — is fully present in the current code. No algebraic proof, bit-identical check, or exhaustive caller verification can close this gap: `"Player" != DAT_00A8B380` unless the persistent global happens to contain exactly that string, which is not guaranteed after any prior match where the player changed their name.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding's §6 status note ("No player-name field or edit focus/caret/input state found in the scanned Skirmish shell state") is stale — `PlayerNameEditState` with full keyboard/focus/caret state now exists and is wired. The drift is narrower than the original finding implies: the edit control, 19-char cap, caret, and launch readback are all implemented. The sole remaining gap is the seed value: `PlayerNameEditState::default()` uses the literal "Player" rather than reading from any persisted global equivalent to `DAT_00A8B380`.

findings.title: Player-name insert filters all control chars; gamemd forwards typed input to the native Win32 single-line Edit
findings.gamemd_behavior: 0x6A0 is an ordinary Edit (OwnerDraw_Edit_00614190); typed characters fall through to the native edit WndProc, which for a single-line edit accepts printable characters and rejects/strips raw control codes per Win32 edit rules. The visible character-acceptance set is whatever the native single-line Edit applies, not an is_control() filter.
findings.rust_behavior: insert_text() drops every char where char::is_control() is true (covers e.g. tab 0x09, but is_control() is a Unicode category that differs from the native edit's accepted set; e.g. it would also drop DEL and various Unicode control ranges, and does not special-case paste of multi-line text the way the native edit collapses it).
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_PLAYER_NAME_EDIT_CONTROL_0X6A0_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §2 (old Edit forwards unhandled messages to native edit WndProc at 0x00614872..0x00614889) and §3.2 (class Edit). Native single-line edit char acceptance is the spec, not an is_control() pass.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed at player_name.rs:122: `text.chars().filter(|ch| !ch.is_control())` drops every Unicode control character before insertion.

Gamemd side verified by decompiling OwnerDraw_Edit_00614190 at 0x00614190. The WM_CHAR (0x102) branch intercepts only wParam==0x9 (Tab, consumed via SetFocus) and wParam==0xD (Enter, consumed or forwarded depending on style bit 0x4). All other WM_CHAR values fall through to LAB_00614872, which calls `CallWindowProcA(lpPrevWndFunc, hwnd, msg, wParam, lParam)` — the native single-line edit WndProc. There is no character-value filter in the gamemd callback.

The finding's gamemd claim is correct. The Rust `is_control()` filter is a divergent mechanism. Two concrete divergence cases:

1. DEL (0x7F): `char::is_control()` returns true in Rust, so it is dropped. The native Win32 ANSI single-line edit WndProc (reached via CallWindowProcA) may insert 0x7F as a glyph depending on the font/codepage — it is not intercepted by the callback. This is the most plausible real-world case where a typed character is accepted in gamemd but silently dropped in Rust.

2. The finding's claim that Unicode control ranges (U+0080–U+009F) differ is technically accurate for `is_control()`, but is not a realistic divergence: gamemd's path uses CallWindowProcA (ANSI), so WM_CHAR wParam is a single ANSI byte (0x00–0xFF); Windows TranslateMessage/DispatchMessage does not generate WM_CHAR for raw control-key sequences in the 0x80–0x9F range under normal keyboard input. This part of the finding is overstated.

3. The finding claims native edit "rejects/strips raw control codes per Win32 edit rules" but this is imprecise: it is TranslateMessage+DispatchMessage in the Windows message pump that prevents most control codes from appearing as WM_CHAR in the first place, not the edit WndProc itself. The net effect is that only characters that survive the message pump arrive at WM_CHAR, and for those (printable chars + potentially 0x7F), the native edit passes them through while Rust's is_control() may drop some.

The drift is confirmed: Rust drops chars that the native edit WndProc would accept (most consequentially 0x7F). The finding's severity/frequency assessment (low/rare) is correct — this only manifests when a user pastes or somehow delivers a DEL character into the name field, which is rare but theoretically possible via clipboard paste.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding overstates the Unicode control range divergence. Gamemd uses CallWindowProcA (ANSI path), so WM_CHAR wParam is a single ANSI byte; Unicode control characters above U+007F are never generated as WM_CHAR by the Windows message pump under normal keyboard use. The realistic divergence is limited to DEL (0x7F): is_control() drops it in Rust, but it reaches the native edit WndProc in gamemd via the pass-through at LAB_00614872. The finding's framing of "Native single-line edit char acceptance is the spec, not an is_control() pass" is correct in principle. The "does not special-case paste of multi-line text" claim is unverified from the binary and should be marked uncertain.

findings.title: focus_select_all forces scroll_x=0, but gamemd EM_SETSEL(-1,-1) leaves caret at end so the field scrolls to show the end
findings.gamemd_behavior: On WM_SETFOCUS old Edit sends EM_SETSEL(-1,-1) selecting all text with the caret at the end; the edit painter (FUN_00623880) horizontally scrolls to keep the caret visible with a 5px margin. With a long (>visible) name selected, the caret-at-end means the view scrolls to show the END of the text, not the start.
findings.rust_behavior: focus_select_all sets caret to len (end) but unconditionally resets scroll_x to 0, so a name longer than the visible field shows its START on focus while the real caret is at the end. update_player_name_scroll_for_caret runs later and would re-correct, but the committed focus state momentarily/initially differs (scroll snapped to 0 with caret at end is internally inconsistent vs gamemd's caret-driven scroll).
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_PLAYER_NAME_EDIT_CONTROL_0X6A0_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3.4 (5px cursor margin, scrolls horizontally to keep cursor visible) and §3.5 (EM_SETSEL(-1,-1) on focus); FOCUS_MESSAGES report §3.2.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
The claimed drift is that `focus_select_all` writes `scroll_x = 0` while `caret = len`, leaving the field momentarily inconsistent vs gamemd's caret-driven scroll. The Rust code at `player_name.rs:101-110` does set `scroll_x = 0` in `focus_select_all`. However, reading the call site in `app.rs:1329-1331` shows the sequence is entirely synchronous within one input handler: `focus_player_name_edit` (calls `focus_select_all`) → `sync_player_name_edit_scroll` (calls `update_player_name_scroll_for_caret`) → `state.window.request_redraw()`. The scroll correction runs before `request_redraw()` is ever called, so no rendered frame is produced with the `scroll_x=0/caret=end` inconsistency. The GPU never sees the intermediate state.

On the gamemd side: `SKIRMISH_PLAYER_NAME_EDIT_CONTROL_0X6A0_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3.4` confirms the edit painter (`FUN_00623880`) "scrolls horizontally to keep the cursor visible with a 5px margin." This is a caret-driven scroll at paint time, not a fixed scroll-to-end. Rust `update_player_name_scroll_for_caret` (player_name.rs:418-443) uses the same caret-driven logic with the same `PLAYER_NAME_CARET_MARGIN_PX = 5` constant. With caret at end and a long name, both gamemd's painter and Rust's corrector produce the same final scroll value.

The finding also incorrectly frames gamemd as "snapping to END" — the doc shows it is caret-margin-driven, not an explicit end-snap. With EM_SETSEL(-1,-1) the caret is at end, and caret-margin logic happens to scroll to show the end, but so does Rust's `sync_player_name_edit_scroll` called in the same frame.

No rendered output difference exists: the transient intermediate state never reaches a frame, and the corrected scroll value is identical to gamemd's painter output for the same caret position. Algebraic equivalence holds for the rendered frame: both use caret-at-end with a 5px right margin on the same field width.
verdict_obj.correction: The gamemd painter does not "snap to END" explicitly — it runs caret-margin logic (5px, FUN_00623880) that happens to scroll to show the end when the caret is there. Rust does the same thing via update_player_name_scroll_for_caret with PLAYER_NAME_CARET_MARGIN_PX=5. The transient scroll_x=0 set by focus_select_all is corrected synchronously (app.rs:1330) before request_redraw() at app.rs:1331, so no frame ever renders the inconsistent state.

findings.title: focus_select_all on an empty field sets selection=None; gamemd's EM_SETSEL(-1,-1) is sent regardless
findings.gamemd_behavior: WM_SETFOCUS always sends EM_SETSEL(-1,-1); the resulting selection on an empty edit is an empty selection with caret at 0 (observably identical to no selection). The behavior is uniform: select-all is always issued, then a printable char replaces the selection.
findings.rust_behavior: Only creates a selection when len>0; for an empty name no selection range is stored. Observably this produces the same result (typing replaces nothing on empty), so this is likely benign, but it is a divergence in the modeled focus contract that the handoff explicitly calls out (first focus selects existing name before typing).
findings.evidence: FOCUS_MESSAGES report §9 acceptance "Default name is focused, pressing a printable key replaces the selected default"; §3.2 EM_SETSEL(-1,-1).

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust source read at `/src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/player_name.rs` lines 101-111 (`focus_select_all`) and lines 61-72 (`normalized_selection`). Doc verified: `SKIRMISH_PLAYER_NAME_EDIT_FOCUS_MESSAGES_0X4B0_0X4AF_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` §3.2 (address `0x006143BE..0x006143FA`) and post-impl audit `SKIRMISH_PLAYER_NAME_EDIT_0X6A0_POST_IMPLEMENTATION_AUDIT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` §3.

gamemd side (verified): `EM_SETSEL(-1,-1)` on an empty Win32 edit control produces an empty selection with start=0, end=0, caret=0.

Rust side (line 107): when `len==0`, `selection = None`, `caret = 0`.

The algebraic proof that outputs are identical: every downstream consumer in Rust routes through `normalized_selection()` (lines 61-72), which explicitly returns `None` when `start == end` (line 65-66). Therefore `Some((0,0))` and `None` are indistinguishable to all callers — `delete_selection`, selection-highlight rendering, and caret placement all see the same result. The next keypress lands `insert_text` → `delete_selection()` → `normalized_selection()` returns `None` → no-op → insert at `caret=0`, which is exactly what gamemd produces from an empty-selection edit. The divergence exists only in the internal field value, never in any observable output. The finding's own description acknowledges "typing replaces nothing on empty" and describes the outputs as identical — and the algebraic closure via `normalized_selection` confirms this across all consumers, not just the happy-path typing case.

findings.title: Tab in player-name edit blurs the field but does not move focus to the next dialog tab control
findings.gamemd_behavior: On WM_CHAR Tab (0x09) the old Edit calls GetParent + GetNextDlgTabItem + SetFocus, moving keyboard focus to the next dialog tab-order control (and returns handled). It is a focus-advance, not merely a blur of the edit.
findings.rust_behavior: handle_player_name_tab only blurs the player-name edit; focus does not advance to any next control, so the observable Tab navigation chain through the skirmish dialog is absent.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_SHELL_INPUT_FOCUS_MESSAGE_BROAD_RECHECK_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3.4 (WM_CHAR Tab calls GetNextDlgTabItem then SetFocus) and §9 acceptance "focus leaves edit to next tab control".

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed: `handle_player_name_tab` at `src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/player_name.rs:445-447` does exactly two things — calls `blur_player_name_edit(state)` and returns its bool. No focus advancement, no next-control lookup.

gamemd side confirmed via Ghidra-verified assembly cited in `SKIRMISH_SHELL_INPUT_FOCUS_MESSAGE_BROAD_RECHECK_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3.4`: `WM_CHAR` with `wParam=9` in `OwnerDraw_Edit_00614190` calls `GetParent`, `GetNextDlgTabItem`, then `SetFocus` and returns handled. Secondary confirmation in `SKIRMISH_PLAYER_NAME_EDIT_0X6A0_POST_IMPLEMENTATION_AUDIT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §7` at assembly `0x0061451B..0x00614555`. Both docs are tagged ghidra/verified with inline assembly spot-checks.

The finding's description of both sides is accurate. The observable difference: pressing Tab while the player-name field has focus should advance keyboard focus to the next tab-order control in the skirmish dialog; in Rust it only removes focus from the edit with no successor gaining focus. This is an observable input-navigation difference, not an internal-only divergence.

findings.title: Choose Map listbox row height is 16px but gamemd owner-draw lists use font_height+2 (19px)
findings.rust_location: src/ui/skirmish_shell/layout.rs (CHOOSE_MAP_LIST_ROW_H = 16; consumed by choose_map.rs row hit/scroll math)
findings.gamemd_behavior: OwnerDraw_ListBox_00618D40 init (message 0x497) sets LB_SETITEMHEIGHT to measured shell font height + 2; with GAME.FNT height 17 this is 19px. A 211px-tall list shows 11 full rows plus a 2px remainder, not 13 rows. This drives both row hit-testing (custom 0x4E8 = top_index + y/item_height) and how many maps are visible at once.
findings.rust_behavior: The choose-map modal uses a fixed 16px row constant; map_row_count/scroll/top-index logic in choose_map.rs is built on that grid, so the number of visible map rows and the y->row mapping differ from gamemd by 3px per row (off by ~2 rows over the list height).
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_OWNERDRAW_LISTBOX_00618D40_ROW_PAINT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3 Row Height (font+2=19px) and §9 handoff ("not 16"); §6 names CHOOSE_MAP_LIST_ROW_H=16 as the mismatch.

verdict_obj.reasoning: Checked the Rust source directly. `CHOOSE_MAP_LIST_ROW_H` at `src/ui/skirmish_shell/layout.rs:32` is `19`, not `16`. It has been 19 since its first introduction in commit 470fae5 ("Add native skirmish shell, loading flow, and PCX/PAL UI variants") — the value was never 16 at any point in git history. `CHOOSE_MAP_LISTBOX_ROW_H` is an alias to the same constant (line 33). All hit-test and scroll math in `layout.rs` (`choose_map_listbox_row_at` line 834, `choose_map_modal_list_row_at` line 820, `choose_map_listbox_row_rect` line 737) and `state/choose_map.rs` consume only the layout constants — there is no hardcoded 16 anywhere in the choose-map path. The gamemd behavior described in the finding (font_height 17 + 2 = 19px) matches what Rust already implements. The finding's Rust-side claim is factually wrong; the drift it describes does not exist in the current codebase.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding states "The choose-map modal uses a fixed 16px row constant." This is incorrect. `CHOOSE_MAP_LIST_ROW_H` has always been 19 in this codebase (first commit 470fae5). There is no drift: Rust uses 19px, gamemd uses 19px (font 17 + 2). The finding should be closed as false-positive.

findings.title: refresh_records clamps map_top_index with visible_rows=1, so the scroll position can sit one full window past where gamemd allows
findings.gamemd_behavior: gamemd's listbox top-index is bounded by the actual visible row capacity (client height / item_height, ~11 rows for the 211px list); after a mode change/repopulation the selected/committed row is scrolled into view and the top index never exceeds row_count - visible_capacity.
findings.rust_behavior: On every record refresh the map list top index is clamped with visible_rows hardcoded to 1, allowing max_top = row_count - 1. After switching modes to a shorter list, the retained map_top_index can leave the list scrolled almost to the bottom showing a single trailing row, whereas the real listbox would clamp so a full window of rows is shown.
findings.evidence: src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/choose_map.rs:177-183 passes literal 1; correct capacity is ~11 per SKIRMISH_OWNERDRAW_LISTBOX_00618D40_ROW_PAINT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3 (19px rows in 211px list). gamemd reselects/repopulates with proper top-index per SKIRMISH_CHOOSE_MAP_0X6B_PREVIEW_REFRESH_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3.5 step 9 (LB_SETTOPINDEX).

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed by reading choose_map.rs:177-183: `refresh_records` calls `set_top_index_clamped(Map0x553, row_count, 1, self.map_top_index)`. The formula at line 104 is `max_top = row_count.saturating_sub(visible_rows.max(1))` — with `visible_rows=1` this gives `max_top = row_count - 1`. However the `map_top_index` fed in is always 0 in practice (initialized to 0 on line 47; reset to 0 on line 69 in `select_mode` before `refresh_records` is called; never mutated between those resets and the `refresh_records` call). So the `visible_rows=1` clamp is not the active bug mechanism — the retained top is 0, safely within any clamp.

The real drift is what value is passed as the top index. Binary read of 0x005E6C80..0x005E6DB3 (210 bytes via `read_memory`) reveals the LB_SETTOPINDEX sequence: `6a 00 53 68 97 01 00 00 68 53 05 00 00 57 ff d5` immediately after `6a 00 53 68 86 01 00 00 68 53 05 00 00 57 ff d5` (LB_SETCURSEL). Both calls push `ebx` as the wParam. `ebx` at that point holds the matched-row index from the text-comparison search loop — i.e., gamemd passes the **selected row index** to both LB_SETCURSEL and LB_SETTOPINDEX. Windows then internally clamps LB_SETTOPINDEX so the window is filled from the bottom. This is confirmed by SKIRMISH_CHOOSE_MAP_0X6B_PREVIEW_REFRESH_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3.5 step 9.

Rust instead passes `self.map_top_index` (0) to the clamping function. Result: after a mode switch or on modal open, if the currently-selected/committed map is not in the first visible rows, gamemd scrolls the list so the selected row is visible at the top; Rust leaves the list scrolled to row 0. The player sees the selection highlighted off-screen while the list shows the top rows — a visible discrepancy whenever the previously-committed map is not near the top of the filtered list.

The finding's stated mechanism (visible_rows=1 allowing max_top=row_count-1 after a mode change) is incorrect — the retained top is 0 in all paths that reach refresh_records, so that clamp is never exercised. The correct diagnosis is: wrong source value for the top-index argument (retained 0 vs. selected-row-index). The symptom (list not showing selected row) is real drift.
verdict_obj.correction: The `visible_rows=1` clamp is not the active bug: `map_top_index` is always 0 when `refresh_records` is called (initialized to 0, reset to 0 in `select_mode` before calling `refresh_records`), so `max_top=row_count-1` is never reached. The actual drift is that Rust passes the retained `map_top_index` (0) as the LB_SETTOPINDEX argument, whereas gamemd (confirmed via binary read at 0x005E6C80..0x005E6DB3) passes the matched selected-row-index as `wParam` to both LB_SETCURSEL and LB_SETTOPINDEX, letting Windows clamp to ensure a full window of rows is shown. The net player-visible effect (selected map not scrolled into view) is the same, but the root cause is the wrong source value, not the wrong clamp constant.

findings.title: Switching game mode resets map highlight to row 0; gamemd preserves the row by saved text key before falling back
findings.gamemd_behavior: On 0x6EB category-change, the modal rebuilds 0x553 and then preserves/reselects a row using a saved text/key (DAT_00AC0EC8 / DAT_00AC11C9) comparison via LB_SETCURSEL/LB_SETTOPINDEX (report §3.5 step 9). Only when no matching saved row exists does it fall to a default. The same-named map across modes stays highlighted.
findings.rust_behavior: select_mode calls refresh_records with preferred_record_index=None, so the highlighted row always resets to filtered index 0 on any mode switch; there is no saved-text reselection. Browsing modes and returning visibly loses the previously highlighted map.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_CHOOSE_MAP_0X6B_PREVIEW_REFRESH_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3.5 step 9 (preserve/reselect row via DAT_00AC0EC8 text comparison, LB_SETCURSEL, LB_SETTOPINDEX); Rust choose_map.rs:69 passes None.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed at choose_map.rs:70 — `select_mode` calls `refresh_records(modes, records, None)`, and `refresh_records` at lines 169-175 resolves `None` by falling back to `Some(0)` (first row) when the filtered list is non-empty. There is no saved-text comparison or name-based reselection anywhere in the function.

gamemd side confirmed from SKIRMISH_CHOOSE_MAP_0X6B_PREVIEW_REFRESH_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3.5 step 9 and the globals table (DAT_00AC0EC8 / DAT_00AC11C9): the 0x6EB category-change branch, after rebuilding list 0x553, runs a text comparison against saved key DAT_00AC0EC8 and issues LB_SETCURSEL + LB_SETTOPINDEX to preserve the previously highlighted row. Only when no row matches does it fall through to a default. This is an active YR path (doc marks it "Conditional: runs when init guard DAT_00AC11C8 == 0 and notification high word is 1"), not TS-legacy.

The finding's gamemd description is accurate. The finding's Rust description is accurate. The observable difference: switching game modes in the Choose Map dialog resets the highlighted map to the first entry in Rust, while gamemd attempts to keep the same-named map highlighted. This is player-visible whenever a user browses modes and the same map name exists in both filtered lists.

findings.title: Main-menu map list (list_available_maps) sorts maps alphabetically; gamemd never sorts the scenario list
findings.gamemd_behavior: The scenario record list (DAT_00A8B8CC) is built in append order: MISSIONSMD.PKT MultiMaps entries first (in PKT order), then loose *.PKT, *.YRO-derived, then loose *.YRM, subject only to filesystem enumeration order for loose groups. The modal loop increments forward and never sorts (0x005E6F17..0x005E6F45; helper 0x005EEE40 appends with no comparison).
findings.rust_behavior: list_available_maps() (the main-menu selector path) re-sorts the gathered maps by lowercased display name. While list_skirmish_scenario_records() preserves MultiMaps order, the alphabetical sort path produces a map ordering the original never shows for that selector.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_CHOOSE_MAP_LIST_POPULATION_ORDER_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §5 ("increments EBX...and never sorts"; append order MISSIONSMD.PKT then loose) and OQ-3 ("Are records sorted in the modal? No"); Rust sort at src/app_list_maps.rs:49.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side confirmed: `src/app_list_maps.rs:49` applies `maps.sort_by_key(|m| m.display_name.to_ascii_lowercase())` to the result of `list_available_maps()`, which is the vec fed into the skirmish shell's `available_maps` (app.rs:2016) backing `selected_map_idx` map selection. No equivalent sort exists in `list_skirmish_scenario_records()`.

gamemd side confirmed via `SKIRMISH_CHOOSE_MAP_LIST_POPULATION_ORDER_GHIDRA_REPORT.md` §5 and OQ-3: live Ghidra decompile at `0x005E6F17..0x005E6F45` shows the modal loop increments `EBX` forward with no sort; append helper `0x005EEE40` writes at current count and increments, no comparison. Order is MISSIONSMD.PKT MultiMaps first, then loose *.PKT, *.YRO-embedded PKT, loose *.YRM — each loose group in `FindFirstFileA` filesystem enumeration order.

One factual correction to the finding: it frames `list_available_maps()` as a "main-menu map selector path" distinct from skirmish. In current code it actually feeds the skirmish shell map picker (via `available_maps` at app.rs:2016), so the drift is in the skirmish selector itself, not a separate main-menu surface. The core claim — that Rust sorts where gamemd never sorts — stands and is fully confirmed by both code inspection and verified Ghidra evidence. Cannot be refuted.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding calls `list_available_maps()` the "main-menu map selector path" as if it is separate from the skirmish selector. In current code, `list_available_maps()` is the source for `available_maps` in app.rs:2016, which directly backs `SkirmishShellState::selected_map_idx` — the skirmish shell map picker. The drift is in the skirmish selector, not a separate main-menu-only surface.

findings.title: Default selected map index comes from SkirmishSettings, not the committed scenario index global DAT_00A8B254
findings.gamemd_behavior: Initial map selection in the modal is pointer-based: it loads DAT_00A8B8CC[DAT_00A8B254] (the previously committed scenario record pointer) and scans listbox 0x553 item data for that exact pointer to set LB_SETCURSEL; if DAT_00A8B254 is -1 or out of range it falls to index 0. Selection identity is the record pointer, not a display-list ordinal.
findings.rust_behavior: selected_map_idx is a plain ordinal seeded from SkirmishSettings::default(); ChooseMapModalState matches the preferred record by record_index equality against filtered_record_indices. This works when records are identical, but the identity model and the default source differ from the committed-pointer scheme, and a non-existent/stale index silently falls to row 0 rather than preserving the last committed scenario.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_CHOOSE_MAP_LIST_POPULATION_ORDER_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §6 and OQ-5/OQ-6 (item data is the scenario-record pointer; initial selection by pointer match to DAT_00A8B8CC[DAT_00A8B254]).

verdict_obj.reasoning: Rust side mis-stated by the finding. The modal does NOT use `selected_map_idx` as a raw ordinal for initial highlight. `app.rs:869-878` (`current_choose_map_record_index`) translates `skirmish_shell_state.selected_map_idx` into a scenario-record index by file_name case-insensitive lookup in `skirmish_scenario_records`, then passes that as `current_record_index` to `ChooseMapModalState::open` (`app.rs:861-866`). Inside `refresh_records` (`choose_map.rs:169-175`), the modal finds the filtered-list row by ordinal equality against `filtered_record_indices`. This is functionally equivalent to gamemd's pointer scan: gamemd loads `DAT_00A8B8CC[DAT_00A8B254]` (the committed record pointer) and scans listbox item data for that pointer; Rust loads the committed `selected_map_idx`, resolves it to a record by file_name, and scans the filtered index list for that record. Both land on the same listbox row for any valid committed selection. The -1 / out-of-range fallback also matches: if `skirmish_shell_maps[selected_map_idx]` has no matching record (equivalent to gamemd's `DAT_00A8B254 == -1` or out-of-range), `current_record_index` is `None`, causing `refresh_records` to fall to `Some(0)` — identical to gamemd falling to listbox row 0. The doc §7 (Rust status) cited by the finding is stale; it predates the `open_choose_map_modal` + `current_choose_map_record_index` implementation in `app.rs`. The finding's claim that "identity model and default source differ" is refuted by the bridge function at `app.rs:869-878`. No observable output difference exists for any valid input.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding incorrectly states that `selected_map_idx` is passed directly as the modal's initial highlight ordinal. In reality, `app.rs:869-878` converts it to a scenario-record index via file_name lookup before opening the modal. The doc §7 status table is stale and does not reflect the current implementation.

findings.title: Arrow Left/Right collapse selection to a moved caret instead of collapsing to the selection edge
findings.rust_location: src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/player_name.rs:173-185 (move_left/move_right clear selection then move caret by 1 from caret)
findings.gamemd_behavior: 0x6A0 forwards arrow keys to the native single-line Edit. Standard Win32 edit behavior: with an active selection, Left collapses the caret to the LEFT edge of the selection (no further move), Right collapses to the RIGHT edge; only with no selection does the caret step by one.
findings.rust_behavior: move_left/move_right always clear the selection and then move the caret by one from the current caret position. After select-all (caret at end), pressing Left moves caret to len-1 instead of collapsing to position 0 (selection start); pressing Right after select-all keeps caret at end which happens to match, but Left is wrong.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_PLAYER_NAME_EDIT_CONTROL_0X6A0_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3.5 (arrow/key messages routed through native edit; old Edit has no custom arrow handling so native single-line edit semantics apply). Native edit collapse-to-edge is the standard contract.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust code at player_name.rs:173-185: `move_left` unconditionally clears selection then does `caret.saturating_sub(1)` from the current caret; `move_right` clears selection then does `(caret+1).min(len)`. No selection-edge collapse logic exists.

Ghidra decompile of `OwnerDraw_Edit_00614190` (0x00614190, verified this session) shows the old Edit's message dispatch: WM_KEYDOWN (0x100) is NOT handled by the custom callback — it falls through to `LAB_0061477c`, which calls `InvalidateRect` (for the ComboBox-parent check), then falls through to `LAB_00614872` which executes `CallWindowProcA(lpPrevWndFunc, hwnd, msg, wParam, lParam)`. Arrow keys are forwarded verbatim to the native Edit WndProc. The native single-line Edit WndProc implements standard Win32 collapse-to-edge: VK_LEFT with active selection collapses caret to the left edge (selection start), VK_RIGHT collapses to the right edge (selection end), with no additional step. This is the documented, deterministic Win32 contract.

Concrete drift scenario: after focus (focus_select_all sets selection=Some((0,len)), caret=len), pressing Left — Rust produces caret=len-1 (wrong), native produces caret=0 (correct). Pressing Right after select-all happens to match by coincidence (caret is already at len, right edge), but this is not algebraic equivalence and any other mid-string selection state will also produce wrong Left results.

The finding's gamemd claim is fully supported by the binary: old Edit forwards WM_KEYDOWN to native WndProc via `CallWindowProcA(lpPrevWndFunc)` at LAB_00614872. The research doc (SKIRMISH_PLAYER_NAME_EDIT_CONTROL_0X6A0_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §3.5) notes "key up/system key/mouse down messages invalidate the edit" and forwards unhandled messages at 0x00614872..0x00614889 — consistent with the binary. No mis-statement on the gamemd side.

findings.title: Hover hit-test uses a fixed hand-authored priority list, not Win32 child z-order (topmost-first)
findings.gamemd_behavior: gamemd resolves the hovered control by calling ChildWindowFromPointEx(dialog, pt, CWP_SKIPINVISIBLE=1) in the common shell WM_NCHITTEST handler. This returns the single TOPMOST child in USER32 z-order whose rect contains the point, skipping only invisible windows. The tie-breaker on overlap is therefore Win32 z-order (creation/template order, last-created sibling on top, raised combo popups on top), not control role.
findings.rust_behavior: hovered_shell_control walks a hardcoded priority sequence: open-combo item, then status_help, player_name, owner-draw buttons, map_preview, checkboxes, trackbars, then per-row combos (Side/Color/Start/Team x8, AiType x7), then flags, then right-panel labels. First rect that contains the point wins. This priority is independent of the dialog template's actual z-order, so for any pair of overlapping controls whose template z-order disagrees with this list, the resolved control (and its STT status help text) can differ from gamemd.
findings.evidence: decompile_function 0x00622B50 shows `pHVar3 = ChildWindowFromPointEx(param_1,pt,1)` then SendMessage 0x4E8/0x4E9 chain; flag 1 = CWP_SKIPINVISIBLE only (no SKIPDISABLED/SKIPTRANSPARENT). MAIN_MENU_HOVER_MESSAGES doc section 5 documents the same flow. Rust uses an explicit ordered scan.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Verified three things:

1. **Gamemd binary** (`decompile_function 0x00622B50`): `ChildWindowFromPointEx(param_1, pt, 1)` confirmed — flag=1 is CWP_SKIPINVISIBLE only. The finding's gamemd claim is accurate.

2. **Rust code** (`src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/hit_test.rs:41-113`): `hovered_shell_control` walks a hardcoded sequence (open-combo item → status_help → player_name → owner-draw buttons → map_preview → checkboxes → trackbars → per-row combos → flags → right-panel labels). The finding's Rust description is accurate.

3. **Geometric analysis** using the verified PE resource template (`SKIRMISH_SHELL_LAYOUT_ASSETS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md`, dialog 0x102, baseX=6, baseY=13): At 800x600, all controls are fully separated with non-overlapping rects. Critical measurement — CBS_DROPDOWNLIST comboboxes have HWND height = face height (~24px) when collapsed, NOT the template dropdown height (74 DLU = 120px); Rust already applies this via `combo_face_rect(rect)` which clips to `COMBO_FACE_H=24`. After applying correct Win32 model: flags (x: 225–273) have a 14px gap from side combos (x: 287+); consecutive AI combo faces (y stride 26px, height 24px) have a 2px gap between rows and do not reach checkboxes (y≥286). No two different controls share any pixel at 800x600 or 1024x768.

The mechanism difference (priority list vs z-order) only produces divergent output when two controls share a pixel — that condition never occurs in this dialog's well-separated, tile-regular layout. The priority list in Rust always encounters each test in non-overlapping order, so the first-hit result is identical to what ChildWindowFromPointEx would return. No player-visible output differs.

findings.title: Static controls (flags, game-type/scenario labels) are tested last on the assumption they never overlap interactive widgets — unproven against template z-order
findings.gamemd_behavior: In gamemd these statics are children of dialog 0x102 with their own z-order positions (flag pictures 0x6DA..0x6E1, game-type label 0x6EC, scenario label 0x5A8, map preview 0x468), all mapped by FUN_006040B0. ChildWindowFromPointEx returns whichever is topmost in z-order at the point, regardless of interactive vs static role; if a static sits above an interactive sibling in template order at an overlapping pixel, the static wins.
findings.rust_behavior: The code comment asserts statics 'do not overlap the interactive widgets above, so order does not change any result' and tests them strictly last. This non-overlap claim is not backed by a verified rect-overlap audit across all 8 rows + flags + labels + preview; it is an assumption. If any overlap exists, Rust would resolve to the interactive widget where gamemd resolves to the static (or vice versa).
findings.evidence: decompile_function 0x006040B0 0x102 branch lists flags 0x6DA..0x6E1 -> STT:SkirmishPictureFlag, 0x6EC -> STT:SkirmishLabelGameType, 0x5A8 -> STT:SkirmishLabelScenario, 0x468 -> STT:SkirmishMapThumbnail, all in the same dialog as the combos/buttons; gamemd ordering is z-order not role-based.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Read src/ui/skirmish_shell/state/hit_test.rs lines 97-110 and src/ui/skirmish_shell/layout.rs in full, then ran an exhaustive Python rect-overlap audit across all 8 flag rows against every interactive control.

Flags occupy x=225..272 (DLU-derived, verified against test assert at line 996: flags[0]=(225,59,48,20)). AI type combo faces end at x=208 (16px gap to flag left edge). Side combo faces start at x=287 (14px gap to flag right edge). Start/color/team combos are further right still (x=423+, x=486+, x=546+). Checkboxes are at x=71..221 and x=302..550 — neither band reaches x=225..272. Trackbars are at x=404+ — no x overlap. Player name spans x=58..208, ending 16px before flags start. The x-separation is structural: flags live in the gap between the AI-name column (x<209) and the Side column (x>286), a gap that is 77px wide. No y-axis overlap test is even needed for most controls because the x bands are disjoint.

For game_type_label (x=649, y=167) and map_label (x=649, y=189): these are in the right panel, separated from the interactive right-panel controls by at least 19px vertically (map_preview ends y=148, game_type starts y=167; map_label ends y=221, start button begins y=241). No overlap.

The centering translation (dx,dy added identically to all rects via translate_layout) preserves all gap relationships, so the non-overlap holds at every supported screen resolution, not just 800x600.

This constitutes an algebraic proof: the layout constants define disjoint x-bands and non-overlapping y-ranges for every static vs. interactive pair across the full valid input space. The Rust comment at line 97-99 is correct. The finding's claim that the non-overlap is unproven is wrong — it is structurally guaranteed by the layout geometry.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding states the non-overlap claim is "not backed by a verified rect-overlap audit" and is "an assumption." This is incorrect. The layout geometry in layout.rs provides an algebraic proof: flags are confined to x=225..272, which is strictly between the AI-name combo column (x max 208) and the Side combo column (x min 287), a gap that exists by construction in the DLU template. The game_type and map_label labels are in the right panel (x=649+) between map_preview and the action buttons, with verified pixel gaps on all sides. No overlap exists at any supported resolution.

findings.title: Choose Map modal hover resolves status-help before button before preview before lists; gamemd order is Win32 z-order (CWP_SKIPINVISIBLE)
findings.gamemd_behavior: The Choose Map screen is a separate fullscreen shell dialog 0x6B processed by the same common WM_NCHITTEST handler (FUN_00622B50 -> ChildWindowFromPointEx, CWP_SKIPINVISIBLE). Hovered child is resolved by Win32 z-order over controls 0x694(title), 0x468(preview), 0x6EB(mode list), 0x553(map list), 0x6C5/0x583/0x5C0(buttons), 0x695(status strip).
findings.rust_behavior: hovered_choose_map_modal_control tests status_help, then buttons, then preview, then mode_list, then map_list, in a fixed role-based order. Same model mismatch as the parent shell: tie-breaking on any overlapping pair follows the hardcoded list rather than the dialog 0x6B child z-order.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_CHOOSE_MAP_MODAL_0X6B_RECT_BOUNDARY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md: dialog 0x6B uses the common fullscreen shell path (0x00622820 -> 0x0060C540); the common hover handler is FUN_00622B50 (ChildWindowFromPointEx). Control set verified there.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Read hit_test.rs lines 173-201, the layout test assertions in layout.rs, the SKIRMISH_CHOOSE_MAP_MODAL_0X6B_RECT_BOUNDARY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md, SKIRMISH_CHOOSE_MAP_MODAL_VISUAL_CONTROL_LAYOUT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md, and SKIRMISH_CHOOSE_MAP_0X6B_POST_IMPLEMENTATION_GAP_AUDIT_GHIDRA_REPORT.md (§3.4).

The finding's gamemd claim is accurate: FUN_00622B50 handles WM_NCHITTEST via ChildWindowFromPointEx(param_1, pt, CWP_SKIPINVISIBLE=1), which resolves the hovered child by Win32 z-order. This is confirmed by MAIN_MENU_DIALOG_0XE2_TOOLTIP_HOVER_FLOW_GHIDRA_REPORT.md §2 and the gap-audit at §3.4 for dialog 0x6B.

The finding's Rust claim is also accurate: hovered_choose_map_modal_control (hit_test.rs:173-201) checks status_help → buttons → preview → mode_list → map_list in a fixed role order rather than by z-order.

However, the finding's conclusion that this constitutes drift is wrong. The ordering only produces a different result when two controls overlap and both contain the same cursor point. The verified final rects at 800x600 (confirmed by layout.rs test assertions at lines 1220-1233) are spatially disjoint:
- mode_list 0x6EB: x=116..311, y=127..470
- map_list 0x553: x=338..533, y=127..470
- status_help 0x695: y=579..599 (bottom strip, full width but no overlap with lists or right panel)
- preview 0x468: x=644..788, y=37..149
- buttons 0x6C5/0x583/0x5C0: x=644..800, y=199..241, 241..283, 535..577

No pair of controls can simultaneously contain the same cursor point. Therefore, ChildWindowFromPointEx and the Rust fixed-order check always produce the same result for every possible cursor position — the game mechanism differs but the output is bit-identical across the full input space. This satisfies CLAUDE.md's algebraic/exhaustive equivalence standard for downgrading from drift.

findings.title: Parent hover suppressed when choose_map_modal/validation_modal present; gamemd has no overlay — it is a separate dialog that owns all input
findings.gamemd_behavior: On Choose Map, gamemd HIDES the setup dialog 0x102 and enters a separate shell dialog 0x6B; there is no parent dialog visible underneath, so parent controls cannot be hovered at all. Validation is a true modal. Net observable result (parent controls produce no hover/status) matches Rust's early-return, so this is behaviorally equivalent for hover.
findings.rust_behavior: hovered_shell_control returns None when either modal state is Some, blocking parent hover. Observable hover output matches gamemd (no parent status text while modal/chooser owns input). Flagged only because the internal mechanism differs (overlay-suppress vs dialog-swap); no output divergence found for hover.
findings.evidence: SKIRMISH_CHOOSE_MAP_MODAL_0X6B_RECT_BOUNDARY_GHIDRA_REPORT.md: 0x5AA hides setup and enters separate dialog 0x6B; SKIRMISH_SHELL_INPUT_FOCUS_MESSAGE_BROAD_RECHECK section 3.6 confirms parent hidden/blocked.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side (hit_test.rs:48-50): `hovered_shell_control` returns `None` for every screen coordinate whenever `state.choose_map_modal.is_some() || state.validation_modal.is_some()`. This blanket suppression covers the whole screen.

gamemd Choose Map side: confirmed correct by SKIRMISH_CHOOSE_MAP_MODAL_SHELL_COMPOSITION_GHIDRA_REPORT.md (section 3, evidence `0x006AD93C..0x006AD93F`): setup `0x102` is explicitly `ShowWindow(setup, 0)` before entering the `0x6B` modal. Parent is not visible; no parent controls exist to hover. Rust's None return matches for this case.

gamemd Validation Modal side: NOT equivalent. VALIDATION_MODAL_0X005D3490_PAINT_COMPOSITION_GHIDRA_REPORT.md and SKIRMISH_VALIDATION_MODAL_NATIVE_PIXEL_RECTS_GHIDRA_REPORT.md confirm that `0x005D3490` creates dialog `0xCE` via `CreateDialogIndirectParamA` with parent `g_hWnd` — NOT `0x102` — and does NOT call `ShowWindow(setup, 0)`. The parent setup shell `0x102` stays fully visible. The VALIDATION_MODAL_0X005D3490_DISABLED_START_UNDERLAY_TIMING_GHIDRA_REPORT.md confirms: only the Start button `0x617` is `EnableWindow(FALSE)`; the dialog itself is not hidden. The modal is centered at roughly 450×325 pixels (candidate: x=175, y=138 at 800×600), leaving large areas of the parent `0x102` shell visible and uncovered — top strip, bottom, and sides. In native gamemd, WM_MOUSEMOVE over those uncovered areas is delivered by Win32 to the parent's child controls, which can trigger `FUN_00622B50`'s status-text update path (verified: `0x00622CCB..0x00622E83`). So hovering a trackbar, combo, or flag picture outside the modal rect produces status text in gamemd. Rust returns `None` for the whole screen — the status strip stays blank everywhere while the validation modal is open. This is an output divergence: a player can hover the visible trackbar/checkbox/combo area outside the validation modal and see no status text in Rust, while gamemd shows it.

The finding's "behaviorally equivalent" claim rests solely on the Choose Map case (where the parent IS hidden) and then incorrectly treats validation as the same. No algebraic proof, no bit-identical empirical check, and no exhaustive caller verification exists for the validation case. The burden of proof per CLAUDE.md is not met; verdict is confirmed-drift.
verdict_obj.correction: The finding conflates two distinct gamemd mechanisms under one verdict. For Choose Map (`0x5AA`): parent `0x102` is hidden (`ShowWindow(setup,0)` at `0x006AD93C`), so no parent hover is possible — Rust's None return is equivalent. For Validation modal (`0xCE`): parent `0x102` is NOT hidden; only the Start button is disabled (`EnableWindow(0x617, 0)`). The modal covers only ~450×325 px centered on screen, leaving the rest of the 800×600 parent visible. Win32 delivers WM_MOUSEMOVE to parent controls in the uncovered region, which updates status text via `FUN_00622B50`. Rust suppresses hover for the whole screen during validation — this is a real output drift, not behavioral equivalence.

findings.title: Inactive AI-row combos still produce hover/status help (matches gamemd EnableWindow-disabled, not hidden) — no disparity
findings.gamemd_behavior: FUN_006AE6E0 disables inactive AI-row Side/Color/Start/Team combos via EnableWindow(hwnd,0) but does NOT hide them (no ShowWindow(FALSE)). ChildWindowFromPointEx passes only CWP_SKIPINVISIBLE, so disabled-but-visible combos are still returned and resolve generic STT combo help on hover.
findings.rust_behavior: The combo hover loop tests combo_rect() (always Some for valid rows) without consulting combo_enabled(), so hovering an inactive-row combo still yields ComboFace -> generic STT help. This MATCHES gamemd. Recorded as a verified non-disparity to prevent a future 'skip disabled combos' regression.
findings.evidence: decompile_function 0x006AE6E0: EnableWindow(pHVar3,0) for the four combos when item==-1 (inactive); no ShowWindow call. decompile_function 0x00622B50: ChildWindowFromPointEx flag=1 (CWP_SKIPINVISIBLE) does not skip disabled.

verdict_obj.reasoning:
Rust side (hit_test.rs:78-95): the combo hover loop iterates all rows for Side/Color/Start/Team/AiType combos calling only `combo_rect(layout, id).is_some_and(|rect| combo_face_rect(rect).contains(x, y))` — `combo_enabled()` (combos.rs:378-393) is never consulted. This matches gamemd.

gamemd side verified via live decompilation:
- `decompile_function 0x006AE6E0`: when an AI row is inactive, `EnableWindow(pHVar3,0)` is called for all four per-row combos (Side/Color/Start/Team) but no `ShowWindow(FALSE)` appears anywhere in the function — the combos remain visible.
- `decompile_function 0x00622B50`: the `WM_NCHITTEST` (0x84) handler calls `ChildWindowFromPointEx(param_1, pt, 1)` with flag=1, which is `CWP_SKIPINVISIBLE`. `CWP_SKIPDISABLED` would be flag=2; disabled-but-visible combos are therefore still returned and produce hover events.
- `decompile_function 0x006AE3F0` (the skirmish dialog proc, confirmed via `get_function_callers 0x006AE6E0` — sole caller is `FUN_006AE3F0`, which is itself called by callers including `MainMenuDialog0xE2_Proc_00531F60`): the `0x4E9` (status-help) handler dispatches STT string lookups with no enabled-state guard for any combo ID.

One minor imprecision in the finding: the gamemd condition is described as "item == -1 (inactive)"; in the binary the actual gate is `local_14 == -1`, which is derived from the combo item data value 1 (the AiType=None sentinel). The semantic meaning is the same. The four combos disabled are the per-row Side/Color/Start/Team widgets, not the AiType combo itself.

Both sides agree: hovering a disabled-but-visible inactive-row combo still produces a `ComboFace` hover target and generic STT status help. No drift exists.
verdict_obj.correction: Minor imprecision only: the finding says "when item == -1 (inactive)" but the binary gate is local_14 == -1, which is set when the AI-type data value equals 1 (the None/inactive sentinel). Semantically equivalent, but the raw condition is a mapped internal value, not a direct item comparison to -1.

findings.gamemd_behavior: Open dropdown row for Side/Color combos resolves item-specific status text (STT:PlayerSide*, STT:PlayerColor*) before falling back to generic combo help, when 0x4E8 returns a non-(-1) row index.
